Progressive reliability method and its application to offshore mooring

a b s t r a c t

Assessing the reliability of complex systems (e.g. structures) is essential for a reliability-based optimal
design that balances safety and costs of such systems. This paper proposes the Progressive Reliability
Method (PRM) for the quantification of the reliability of complex systems. The proposed method is a
closed-form solution for calculating the probability of failure. The new method is flexible to the definition
of ‘‘failure’’ (i.e., can consider serviceability and ultimate-strength failures) and uses the rules of proba-
bility theory to estimate the failure probability of the system or its components. The method is first dis-
cussed in general and then illustrated in two examples, including a case study to find the safest
configuration and orientation of a 12-line offshore mooring system. The PRM results are compared with
results of a similar assessment based on the Monte Carlo simulations. It is shown in the example of two-
component that using PRM, the importance of system components to system safety can be quantified and
compared as input information for maintenance planning.

 2013 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
1. Introduction

The safety and cost-effectiveness of a system can be maximized
if a reliability-based method is used for its design. For example, the
safety of a structural system usually depends on numerous random
variables, including environmental demands (loads), material
properties and structural capacities (strengths). Therefore, the
safety of such system is uncertain. In practice, for the design of reli-
able structures, a number of safety criteria are fulfilled as recom-
mended by design codes, normally through the use of factors of
safety (FoS). Using FoSs that locally assesses the system safety
has been practiced for many years and has produced structures
that function under various circumstances. However, concerns
remain in terms of the system reliability. Using FoS provides little
quantitative information about the overall reliability of the struc-
tural systems. In turn, quantitative reliability methods quantify
the system safety and reliability in terms of their failure probabil-
ity and provide a basis for changing and optimizing the design vari-
ables to achieve sufficiently safe structures that are not
overdesigned.
This paper proposes a Progressive Reliability Method (PRM) as a
closed-form solution for quantifying the system reliability using
the basic rules of the probability theory. This general method can
compute the probability of system failure in terms of its function-
ality and performance or the failure of certain system components
(this method is flexible to the definition of system failure). For
example, in structural engineering, the maximum deformation of
the structure to a certain level or the ultimate-strength failure of
one or some of the system components usually defines the system
failure. In a PRM, the reliability assessment accounts for the possi-
ble progressive failures of the system components until the system
failure criterion is met. Such assessment requires calculating the
probability of various failure scenarios and calculating the proba-
bility of system failure under each scenario. Because after each
component failure, the probability space of the remaining compo-
nents changes (due to the redistribution of the demands), and any
damaged state of the system may occur through multiple failure
scenarios, a general solution that accounts for all such complexities
is developed.

The PRM uses known rules of probability to formulate a general
method for calculating the reliability of complex systems. The pro-
posed method provides a closed-form solution that accounts for
the dynamic changes in the stochastic model associated with the
possible progressive failures of the components. This general
method captures the complexities that are not captured when only



2132 M.E. Mousavi et al. / Engineering Structures 56 (2013) 2131–2138
one or two failures of a limited number of components are consid-
ered. For example, multiple paths can lead to the same damaged
systems with several failed components. By using the well-known
total probability rule, converting the problem to a fragility (condi-
tional probability of system failure given system demands) prob-
lem, and making the assumption that only one component fails
at a time, PRM is a novel formulation based on known concepts
to compute the probability of failure of complex systems.

A PRM can be applied in the design of many complex systems.
As an illustration, this paper shows how the proposed method
can be applied to an offshore mooring system. Offshore mooring
systems are important structures, especially in development of
oil and gas drilling and production systems. The main function of
a mooring system is to maintain a floating structure within a cer-
tain offset of its still position. The offshore industry has used sev-
eral mooring systems in deepwaters; however, some of them
have failed during service or extreme conditions. A mooring sys-
tem consists of multiple mooring lines. Each line includes a few
components. Because of the uncertainties in the strength of the
system components and the environmental loads, a reliability-
based design optimization can improve the design of the mooring
systems more accurately than the conventional FoS-based meth-
ods. Furthermore, the failure of any individual component in a
mooring system does not necessarily compromise its functionality;
therefore, a PRM can be used for an unbiased and comprehensive
reliability-based optimization.

Calculating the probability of failure of offshore mooring sys-
tems or their components has been the subject of a number of
studies in the last few years. Stiff et al. [1] calculated the probabil-
ity of failure of mooring systems using a closed-form solution
accounting for the ultimate-strength (overloading) failure only
and based on a quadratic model for the failure of the individual line
components. Shah et al. [2] assessed the reliability of mooring sys-
tems by calculating the probability that incoming waves have a
frequency near the natural frequency of the mooring system. The
reliability of individual components and individual lines in a moor-
ing system was discussed by Vazquez-Hernandez et al. [3] and
Montes-Iturrizaga et al. [4] concerning the design criteria and a
method to quantify the probability of a component failure in an in-
tact system. Grimea and Langley [5] used a simplified model to
compare the probability of failure of mooring systems under differ-
ent extreme environmental loads. Ku et al. [6] performed a limited
number of Monte Carlo simulations to estimate the probability of
mooring system failure with various system properties. The prob-
ability of failure of suction caissons in mooring systems was dis-
cussed by Najjar and Gilbert [7]. Montes-Iturrizaga et al. [8] also
analyzed individual mooring lines for their reliability and used a
new equation to calculate the probability of failure of the
individual lines in the intact system. Mousavi [9] calculated the
probability of failure of mooring systems using Monte Carlo
simulation and a new definition for the failure based on system
functionality by making the assumption that one mooring compo-
nent fails at a time.

The main function of a mooring system is to maintain a vessel
within an allowable offset of its still position. However, a review
of the existing studies on the calculation of the probability of fail-
ure of mooring systems shows that the probability of serviceability
and functionality failure (the probability that the offset of a
moored vessel exceeds an allowable limit) is typically not consid-
ered. Mousavi [9] discussed this probability, suggesting a method
to evaluate the probability of serviceability failure in an offshore
mooring system. However, the proposed method is based on the
Monte Carlo simulations. The simulation-based methods are used
for the reliability analysis of other complex systems considering
serviceability limit states. For example, Hernandez-Fajardo [10]
used a Monte Carlo analysis to quantify the reliability of urban sys-
tems under seismic hazard accounting for the interdependency of
the component fragilities. However, the simulation-based methods
are usually time consuming and require extensive analyses. The
proposed PRM can quantify the reliability significantly faster than
such simulation-based approaches, accounting for the dependency
of the component failures using the total probability rule and by
computing the conditional probabilities of failure of components
under all relevant configurations. PRM can also be used to quantify
the likelihood of each scenario of system failure and compare the
importance of the components to maintain system safety. A simpli-
fied example is used in the paper to highlight such application.

In the following sections, the PRM is first formulated in general
terms and explained also using an idealized system with two com-
ponents. Then, as an illustration, it is used in two case studies for
mooring systems.
2. Progressive Reliability Method (PRM)

A PRM calculates the failure probability of a structural system
accounting for the progressive failure of system components until
the system fails. In this method, two important terminologies are
essential and frequently referred to, which are (a) a configuration
and (b) a failure scenario. We will first introduce these terms and
then discuss the method.

2.1. System configurations and failure scenarios

We define a configuration as any possible state of the structure
in terms of the possible state (failure or no failure) of its compo-
nents. This definition is based on the assumption that any compo-
nent may fail and is always either in the state of failed or in the
state of not failed. Any structural system has an initial arrangement
of its components. We call the initial state of a structural system
(with no failed components) as the intact configuration. Under
the effect of environmental loads, some components may fail.
Any component failure leads the system to a new configuration.
If a system has N components that may potentially fail, the total
number of possible configurations is 2N, which includes the intact
and 2N – 1 unique damaged configurations (including a configura-
tion with all components failed). A system configuration can be
shown using a binary row vector with N columns so that each col-
umn refers to the status of a component under that configuration;
if component j fails, the jth column is 1; otherwise it is 0. We refer
to each configuration with Xi, where the index i is the decimal rep-
resentation of the binary vector corresponding to that configura-
tion. The intact and the most damaged (all components failed)
configurations are therefore shown as X0 ¼ ½|0fflffl0fflfflffl0ffl{.z.fflfflffl.fflffl0ffl} and

N
X2N1 ¼ ½|1fflffl1fflfflffl1ffl{z. .fflfflffl.fflffl1ffl}, respectively.

N
We define a failure scenario of Xi as a group of steps (or the

path) that leads the system from X0 (the intact configuration) to
Xi. In general, the occurrence of Xi can be through one or more fail-
ure scenarios. However, because in a structural system, the envi-
ronmental loads usually vary within a time frame until reaching
to a maximum load, and that the capacity of the components are
random, it is almost impossible that in more than one component,
the internal force exceed the capacity at the same time. Therefore,
as suggested by Mousavi [9], we assume that one component fails
at a time. This assumption means that after each component fail-
ure, any next failure may occur only after the loads are redistrib-
uted among the remaining components under the new
configuration. With this assumption, if Xi has ni failed components,
the total number of possible unique failure scenarios leading to
this configuration are:

2.2. Fragility model

In a PRM, the conditional probability of system failure under a
given environmental load depends on the probability that each
configuration occurs as well as the probability that the system fails
under that configuration and demand. The configurations of a sys-
tem constitute a finite set of mutually exclusive and collectively
exhaustive events because they are all unique and span all the pos-
sible options. Therefore, for a given demand (environmental load)
dE, we can calculate the conditional probability of failure, or fragil-
ity [11,12], as

2XN1
Pðgs 6 0jdEÞ ¼ fPðgs 6 0jXi;dEÞ  PðXijdEÞg ð2Þ

i¼0

where gs is the system limit-state function defined such that
Pðgs 6 0jXi;dEÞ indicates the probability of system failure under Xi
and dE, and P() denotes the probability of the event described in
the parenthesis. Eq. (2) formulates the fragility of a structural sys-
tem based on the PRM. By using the likelihoods of the demands,
the overall system failure probability can then be calculated using
the total proZbability rule [13] Z 2XN1
Pðgs 6 0Þ ¼ Pðgs 6 0jdEÞ  f ðdEÞddE ¼ fPðgs

B B iZ¼0

2XN1
6 0jXi; dEÞ  PðXijdEÞg  f ðdEÞddE ¼ 1 fPðgs

B i¼0

> 0jXi;dEÞ  PðXijdEÞg  f ðdEÞddE ð3Þ
where f() is the probability density function (PDF) and B is the do-
main of dE.Eqs. (2) and (3) indicate that the number of possible con-
figurations in a system exponentially increases by increasing the
number of components, implying that numerous deterministic
analyses should be completed to evaluate the terms in these equa-
tions. However, as will be discussed later, it is expected that the
term P(gs > 0|Xi,dE)becomes 0 or negligible for most configurations
with multiple failed components. For any such Xi and dE, the rest
of the terms in Eq. (3) become irrelevant. Moreover, any larger de-
mand on that configuration or any progressive failures following
that configuration also become irrelevant (i.e., their conditional
probabilities of system failure are 0 too). Finally, if some of the com-
ponents form groups of series subsystems so that the failure of any
component in the group makes other components of that group
irrelevant to the system safety, then one deterministic analysis is
sufficient to account for the failure of each series subsystem under
a given demand and configuration. Therefore, to use Eq. (3), we only
need to analyze the system for a limited number of configurations
and range of demands with nonzero P(gs > 0|Xi,dE). Hence, the decay
of P(gs > 0|Xi,dE) with progressive failures has an important role on
reducing the analyses of the PRM, because it identifies the configu-
rations and the range of demands that are irrelevant in Eq. (3).

2.3. Calculating the conditional probability that system does not fail
under each configuration

Eq. (3) requires calculating P(gs > 0|Xi,dE) for all the relevant Xi
and dE. This conditional probability depends on the definition of
system failure. For example, a structural failure can be defined
based on the ultimate-strength failure of some structural compo-
nents, the serviceability of the structure, or a combination of these
two. If a system failure is defined based on the ultimate-strength
failure of certain components or certain number of components
in a system, then if in Xi, those components or number of compo-
nents are already failed, then P(gs > 0|Xi,dE) is 0; otherwise, it is 1.
Similarly, if a system failure is defined by a certain limit for the sys-
tem response (e.g. deformation in a structure) and the modeling
error is negligible, then P(gs > 0|Xi,dE) is either 0 (when the failure
criterion is met) or 1 (when the failure criterion is not met). How-
ever, if the modeling error is not negligible, then P(gs > 0|Xi,dE) is a
number between 0 and 1 that should be estimated using a reliabil-
ity analysis (e.g. FORM, SORM).

2.4. Calculating the conditional probability of each configuration

According to Eq. (3), for calculating the system failure probabil-
ity, we should calculate the conditional probability of each config-
uration given the demand on the system. Because any
configuration Xi can occur through any of the Ki failure scenarios
leading to this configuration, we need to determine the conditional
probabilities of all these failure scenarios to compute the condi-
tional probability of this configuration. The failure scenarios of
each configuration are mutually exclusive and collectively exhaus-
tive; hence, we have

2.5. Deterministic analysis

A PRM requires deterministic analysis of all the system config-
urations (Xi) and demands (dE) with P(gs > 0|Xi,dE) > 0. To do so, we
should first discretize the environmental loads and calculate the
response of the intact configuration and find the range of dE within
B that P(gs > 0|X0,dE) > 0. This range is also the largest range of dE
that should be included in deterministic analysis of all the other
configurations because all other configurations are products of fail-
ure in the intact system. We call this range of dE as the functional
range of demands. We should then consider N new configurations
where each of them has one fewer component compared to the
intact configuration. For each new Xi, deterministic analysis should
be performed. Then, only if P(gs > 0|Xi,dE) > 0 within the functional
range of demands, all possible new configurations with one fewer
components should be considered. The functional range of
demands with P(gs > 0|Xi,dE) > 0 may shrink for each new configu-
ration and thus for other configurations with further failures. Anal-
ysis of configurations with progressive failure should continue
until P(gs > 0|Xi,dE) becomes 0 or negligible for any dE, which deter-
mines an end to that path of progressive failures that must be con-
sidered. At the end of deterministic analysis, the internal forces for
all the demands and configurations with P(gs > 0|Xi,dE) > 0 are
available and can be used to calculate the conditional probabilities
of relevant configurations.

In summary, in a PRM analysis, after the deterministic analyses
are completed for all the configurations and demands with
P(gs > 0|Xi,dE) > 0, all the corresponding failure scenarios have to
be determined and their conditional probabilities must be com-
puted. Then, using Eq. (4), the conditional probability of each con-
figuration can be determined. Next, Eq. (2) gives the conditional
probability of system failure. Finally, Eq. (3) computes the proba-
bility of failure of the system. The proposed formulation can be
used also to compute the probability of exceeding a specified dam-
age level by defining an appropriate system limit-state function gs
so that gs 6 0 indicates exceedance of the selected damage level.

The computations for the PRM are illustrated using an example
of a two-component structural system.

3. Example of two-component structural system

We illustrate the computations of the PRM using an example
two-component structural system. In this example, given the envi-
ronmental load, we calculate the conditional probabilities of (1)
serviceability failure of the system, (2) ultimate-strength failure
of the system (exceedance of one component failure in the system),
and (3) failure of each component. The components of this system
are two springs arranged in a parallel form as shown in Fig. 1. Each
spring is under a tension of dj induced by dE and has a fixed stiff-
ness of kj but an uncertain strength of cj. The cylinder is a rigid ele-
ment with negligible friction with the two side-walls. Because of
the side-walls, dE will be distributed among the active (not failed)
component(s) as axial loads only. The capacity of each component
is independent from the other.

For this example, we define the serviceability failure as if D, the
cylinder displacement, exceeds D0, an allowable displacement,

Eqs. (13), (16), and (18) calculate the system and component condi-
tional probabilities of failure given dE and Xi based on the PRM.

Here, we calculate the conditional probabilities of system and
component failures under dE for some arbitrary input information.
We assume that the component capacity, cj, follows a lognormal
distribution. The component stiffness and the mean and standard
deviation of each cj are given in Table 1. The environmental load
dE and the allowable displacement D0 are taken to be 50 kN and
1.2 m, respectively.

Considering the four possible configuration of this system in Eq.

ponents under dE become 0.5721 and 0.3268, respectively.

One of the applications of the proposed PRM is identifying the
most critical failure scenarios or components of a system, which
can be used for maintenance planning and failure prevention. By
assessing the likelihood of each failure scenario or configuration
and their influence on system functionality, the significance of
each component to the system safety can be assessed and com-
pared. For example, results in Table 2 show that an initial failure
of Component 1 already corresponds to a system failure (because
even if Component 2 does not fail, the system displacement still
exceeds the allowable limit). Conversely, the system could remain
functional after the initial failure of Component 2. Therefore, Com-
ponent 1 seems to be the more critical to the safety of this system.
Using the calculations in Fig. 2, initial failure of Component 1 has a
(conditional) probability of about 0.1365 and thus the likelihood
that the system failure occurs through an initial failure in Compo-
nent 1 equals 0.1365. In turn, if Component 2 initially fails, this
system still has a 0.8235 chance of survival. Based on the calcu-
lated probabilities, the likelihood that this system loses its func-
tionality through an initial failure of Component 2 is
0.2828  0.1765 = 0.0499. This implies that in the intact condition
of this system, preventing the failure of Component 1 is about 2.7
times more important compared to Component 2 for maintaining
the system safety. This information can be used as an input to
maintenance planning of such system. Similar analysis can be per-
formed in more complex systems to identify the likelihood of each
failure scenarios and optimize their maintenance planning.
Load Direction 

Fig. 3. Schematic view of the mooring Systems A (left), B (middle), and C (right).
4. Case study: a mooring system

In this section, we show the application of the PRM for calculat-
ing the failure probability of offshore mooring systems. This sec-
tion has two parts. First, the results of the PRM are compared
with those we produced using Monte Carlo simulations following
the algorithm developed by [9] for three mooring systems of differ-
ent water depths under determined environmental loads. Then the
PRM is used to identify a safer arrangement from among multiple
mooring systems with a same number but different arrangements
of the mooring lines under multidirectional environmental loads.
Since the main function of a mooring system is to hold the vessel
within allowable offset, a maximum allowable offset is used to de-
fine the system failure. However, because in practice it is common
to set a limit for the allowable number of line failures, the effect of
setting a limit for number of failed lines is also assessed.

The analyses for this study were performed using Mooring-3D
[9] based on quasi-static method; however, the proposed method
can be used based on more advanced analysis methods (e.g. dy-
namic time or frequency domain). For calculation of the mean
capacity of the suction caisson, Mooring-3D used the method rec-
ommended by Aubeny et al. [15]. For probabilistic analysis of this
study, Mooring-3D first calculated the internal forces in all the
Table 3
Basic properties of the mooring system in the first part of the case study.

ID Arrangement Water depth (m) Initial pretension (KN) Mooring line

Top

A 3  2 500 850 4 (150)
B 4  2 1000 1500 4 (200)
C 4  3 1500 400 3 (200)
mooring components and the vessel offset under all configurations
and environmental loads with P(gs > 0|Xi,dE) = 1. Then, the PRM
calculations or Monte Carlo simulations were performed. Because
each mooring line is a series subsystem, one deterministic analysis
was completed when assessing the component failures of each line
under given demand and system configuration. In the Monte Carlo
simulations, Mooring-3D was set to continue the simulations until
either completing a hundred million realizations or reaching a
COV. of less than 0.005 for the system failure probability.

In the first part of this case study, results of the PRM are com-
pared with those of Monte Carlo simulations for three typical cat-
enary mooring systems located at 500, 1000, and 2000 m waters.
All the three mooring systems held a spar with a radius of 25 m.
The allowable offsets of the spar under the determined 100-year
eastward loads of 850, 1350, and 800 kN were 20, 40, and 60 m
(4% of the water depth) from its still position. The general proper-
ties of the mooring systems are given in Table 3. Fig. 3 shows the
intact configurations of the mooring lines in each system.

Each mooring line consisted of three segments and a suction
caisson at the seafloor. Thus, the number of capacity random vari-
ables for Systems A, B, and C were 24, 32, and 48 respectively. In
reference to Table 3, the properties of the mooring segments types
are provided in Table 4. All the mooring lines in each mooring sys-
tem had the same properties. The seabed slope was taken to be
negligible in all systems. Each mooring line was attached to a suc-
tion caisson at 67% of the length of the caisson below the seabed.
The length-to-diameter ratios of all the suction caissons were ta-
ken to be six. The sizes of the suction caissons in each mooring sys-
tem are also given in Table 3. The capacities of the suction caissons
were calculated based on a soil shear strength of 3.8 kPa (80 psf) at
the seabed with a linear gradient of 1.57 kPa/m (10 psf/ft) below
the seabed; the coefficient of variations (COV) of the lateral capac-
ity of the suction caissons was taken to be 0.3 [7]. Both the lateral
capacity of the suction caissons and the tensile strength of the
mooring line segments were assumed to follow lognormal distri-
butions; however, a lower-bound capacity of 55% the mean capac-
ity was assumed for the suction caissons [7].

Results of probabilistic analysis of Systems A, B, and C using
both the PRM and the Monte Carlo simulations are given in Table 5.
The results are compared in two levels. First, the deterministic
analysis was limited to the intact configuration only, thus, a large
offset or any component failure defined a system failure. Then,
the functionality of all the configurations with one failed line
was also assessed, so the system failure was defined as if at least
two components fail or the vessel offset exceed the allowable off-
set. Comparison between the results of the two methods in this ta-
ble suggests that the differences between the results of these two

methods are negligible. This indicates the significant advantage of
the PRM because it is a closed-form solution and its computations
are considerably faster than those of Monte Carlo simulations.

The second part of this case study compares the safety of some
alternative arrangements of the 12 mooring lines of System C. As
shown in Fig. 4, the 12 mooring lines of System C can spread as
individual lines (System D), or be arranged in three groups of four
paired lines (System F), or in six groups of two paired lines (System
H). Also, given the asymmetrical directional 100-year environmen-
tal loads as shown in Fig. 5, rotating the asymmetrical Systems C
and F for 30 clockwise can lead to possibly safer Systems E and
G respectively. Identifying the safest system from among all these
12-line alternatives is possible by calculating and comparing their
fragilities using the PRM. In this assessment, again the system
functionality is defined based on the vessel offset and the ulti-
mate-strength failures of the lines. We considered three definitions
for the system failure. The system was assumed to fail after either
exceeding the allowable vessel offset of 60 m or the failure of at
least one, two, or three lines. Therefore, the deterministic analyses
were extended to all the possible system configurations with up to
2 failed lines.

Fragility results of the PRM for the alternative systems with 12
mooring lines are shown in Fig. 6. An overview of the results in this
figure indicates that the calculated fragilities have reduced when a
second line failure is also assessed compared to when a first line
failure was considered only; however, accounting for a third line
failure has not changed the fragilities. This indicates that after
the two line failures, the system is no longer functional and thus
the range of dE with P(gs > 0|Xi,dE) = 1 is empty due to the vessel
offset criterion; hence, this criterion governs such results. On the
other hand, results in this figure show that the relative difference
between the reliability of the alternative systems becomes more
significant when accounting for higher number of progressive fail-
ures. For example, among the six analyzed arrangements of the 12-
Table 5

C 8.05  102 8.03  102 8.00  102 7.95  102

Fig. 4. Schematic view of other alternative 12-line mooring systems.



2138 M.E. Mousavi et al. / Engineering Structures 56 (2013) 2131–2138
line mooring systems, System F has the highest and System C has
the lowest safety. The fragility of System F is about 17% less than
that of System C when the analysis accounts for the first failure
only; however, this difference is increased to near 50% when the
analysis is extended to a second line failure. These results highlight
the significance of accounting for the damaged but functional con-
figurations in the risk assessments. Results also suggest that Sys-
tems C, D, E, and H are equally safe despite the differences in
their arrangement. In summary, System F is found to have the saf-
est arrangement for 12-mooring lines for the given environmental
load and alternative options.
5. Conclusions

This paper proposed a Progressive Reliability Method (PRM) for
calculating the probability of failure of structural systems. The PRM
is a closed-form solution based on known rules of probability to
quantify the reliability of system functionality, ultimate-strength
failure (exceeding a damage level), or component failures. In this
method, all the possible system configurations in terms of the fail-
ure statuses of its components are first determined. By developing
a fragility model, the response of the system under conditioned
environmental loads is then evaluated. Using the probability den-
sity functions of the system component capacities (strengths)
and results of structural (deterministic) analysis for the component
demands (internal forces), the fragilities of the system under any
configuration and environmental loads that is considered to be
not-failed according to the definition of system failure is calcu-
lated. The total probability rule is then used to summarize the
probabilistic results and calculate the system failure probability.
The definition of failure in this method is flexible and can refer
to the serviceability failure, the ultimate-strength failure of certain
subsystems within the main system, a combination of the service-
ability and ultimate-strength failures, or any other criteria as long
as it can be checked given a configuration and demand. This meth-
od can be used to quantify the reliability and safety of complex
structures. PRM can also be used to assess and compare the likeli-
hoods of various failure scenarios and the importance of the sys-
tem components to system safety, as illustrated in the example
of a two-component structure. Such information can be also used
as input for maintenance planning or risk assessments of the sys-
tem components.

The PRM is illustrated in a case study for offshore mooring sys-
tems. First, the results of this method are compared with those of
Monte Carlo simulations. Then a 12-line mooring system is
analyzed under directional environmental loads to identify the saf-
est arrangement and orientation among several possible options.
The results show that the PRM results are very close to those of
Monte Carlo simulations. They also indicated that for an example
mooring system of this study, the reliability of this system is al-
most the same when the lines are arranged individually or in
groups of two or three paired lines with different orientations.
However, the reliability of the mooring system is considerably im-
proved, up to 50%, when the lines are formed in three groups of
four and oriented against the peak directional loads. Results show
that the PRM must be extended to two damaged lines to accurately
quantify the reliability of system concerning the allowable vessel
offset in this example.

a r t i c l e i n f o
Article history:
Received 26 August 2014
Received in revised form 26 December 2015
Accepted 25 January 2016

Keywords:
Safety management
Offshore helicopter operations
System modelling
Task analysis
Hazard identification and risk analysis
a b s t r a c t

A precondition for a holistic, systemic and systematic management of offshore helicopter operations’
safety is an in-depth understanding of the corresponding system. This is essential because the compo-
nents and the interactions between these components largely shape system and human performance,
with the potential to create safety hazards and risks. However, current techniques of system component
description in aviation are poorly specified, which contributes to a silo approach to hazard identification
and risk analysis, poor safety management and missed safety targets. This paper addresses this shortcom-
ing by developing a novel process to characterise the component-based system architecture of commer-
cial air operations and implementing the process to create the Offshore Helicopter Transportation System
Model. The paper discusses the theoretical justifications for generating such a system model and the
shortcomings of the techniques currently recommended. In order to redress these, the process combines
two task analysis techniques to derive an unbiased seminal systemmodel, which is then compared to rel-
evant models in the safety literature to ensure completeness. The process and resulting system model are
validated by carefully selected subject matter experts during training sessions in high-fidelity flight sim-
ulators. The process can be applied to describe, by means of a system model, the activities of other indus-
tries in the aviation domain. Moreover, the model derived provides, for the first time, the necessary
anchoring for future safety analyses in offshore helicopter operations.

 2016 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
1. Introduction

Effective safety management in complex human-centred activ-
ities, such as offshore helicopter operations (Nascimento et al.,
2014a, 2012), requires a holistic systemic view capable of mapping
all system components that may influence human and system
performance (e.g., Carvalho et al., 2009; Endsley, 1995; Hollnagel,
2009; Dougherty, 1990).

Characterising the components of a system usually involves the
elicitation of subject matter expertise, which in turn requires some
form of task analysis to be applied (Hollnagel, 2012, 2009, 2004;
Marti, 2000; Militello and Hutton, 2000; Ainsworth and Marshall,
2000). By analysing the tasks involved in the operation of an indus-
try, the industry’s safety-critical components can be identified and
systematically organised to specify the overarching system
architecture. The system architecture may then be used to support
the holistic identification of what might otherwise appear to be
disconnected and unfamiliar hazards (Hollnagel, 2012, 2009,
2004; Kontogiannis and Malakis, 2011; FAA, 2006; ICAO, 2012;
Arendt and Adamski, 2011; Stolzer et al., 2008; FAA Air Traffic
Organization, 2008; Deming, 2000; Roland and Moriarty, 1990).

In aviation, the use of task analysis to describe an industry’s
safety-critical components in anticipation of hazard identification
is widely recommended (e.g., Stolzer et al., 2008; FAA Air Traffic
Organization, 2008; FAA, 2006; Arendt and Adamski, 2011; ICAO,
2012; Wilke et al., 2014). However, the task analysis techniques
suitable for this purpose are poorly specified, with the conse-
quences that the uptake of such techniques is still very limited in
offshore helicopter operations and the characterisation of the
industry’s safety-critical components is currently absent.

The guidelines for using task analysis for describing an indus-
try’s safety-critical components in the aviation sector are vague
adaptations of systems engineering, probabilistic risk assessment
(PRA) and human reliability analysis (HRA) processes (e.g., FAA
Air Traffic Organization, 2008; ICAO, 2012). However, such
processes are best suited for tightly-controlled operations and they
fail to account holistically for the totality of the system which
may promote human and organisational failures in adaptable



F.A.C. Nascimento et al. / Safety Science 85 (2016) 194–204 195
commercial operations (e.g., Apostolakis, 2004; Dougherty, 1990;
Hollnagel, 2012; 2009). Therefore, a novel process is required to
enable the description of the safety-critical components of
commercial air operations in support of hazard identification
and risk analysis. This is particularly relevant in the offshore heli-
copter industry since poor hazard identification and risk analysis
currently contributes to high accident rates (Nascimento et al.,
2013, 2014; Oil and Gas UK, 2011; OGP, 2010), missed safety
targets (Stevens, 2013), close public scrutiny (CAA, 2014) and the
potential of discontinued operations (Mitchell and Braithwaite,
2008).

Given the background above, this paper aims to (i) develop a
novel task analysis-based process to describe the safety-critical
components of an industry in the aviation sector and (ii) imple-
ment the process to generate the Offshore Helicopter Transporta-
tion System Model. These aims add to current knowledge and
practice by providing a generic process applicable to commercial
air operations and a specific model which may be used to support
hazard identification and risk analysis in the offshore helicopter
domain. Section 2 describes the pursuit of the first aim. The second
aim is pursued in Section 3.
2. Process development

2.1. Task analysis techniques

With over 100 techniques available in the literature (Annett and
Stanton, 2000b; Kirwan and Ainsworth, 1992; Drury, 1983;
Stanton and Annett, 2000; Chipman et al., 2000), case-based
reasoning (i.e., the study of similar cases of successful and unsuc-
cessful task analyses to inform the selection of an approach for a
new task analysis problem; Chipman et al., 2000) indicates that a
combination of high-level mission analysis (Jarvis and Harris,
2008; Arendt and Adamski, 2011) and Hierarchical Task Analysis
(HTA; Annett and Duncan, 1967) have the potential to address
the first research aim.

High-level mission analysis enables the operation to be
described by its constituent parts prior to further in-depth analy-
sis. This technique was used by Jarvis and Harris (2008) to support
human factors analyses on the basis of phases of glider flights. HTA
in turn is the most consistently-applied, flexible and fundamental
approach to task analysis and involves describing a task by a hier-
archy of sub-goals and plans that indicate the conditions under
which the sub-goals should be executed (Shepherd, 2000;
Shryane et al., 2000; Salmon et al., 2011). HTA has been applied
in various domains and, in aviation, has been used to support
pilot-friendly cockpit design and risk assessment in airport
operations (Wilke et al., 2014; Harris et al., 2005).
2.2. Process’ steps

The combined use of high-level mission analysis and HTA
begins with the use of the former for the identification of typical
flight profiles. For each profile, the typical phases of flight are iden-
tified from the literature. HTA is then applied on the basis of vari-
ous sources of information (e.g., operational documentation and
unstructured interviews of pilots reviewed by senior pilots
(Kirwan and Ainsworth, 1992)) to highlight the tasks that typically
need to be undertaken for the successful completion of each of the
phases. This requires that the purpose of and stopping rules for
conducting the HTA are established (Kirwan and Ainsworth,
1992; Hollnagel, 2012, 2009). Such purpose and stopping rules
are essential in order to ensure that the system model is suffi-
ciently broad to fulfil its creational purposes whilst still compact
enough to be manageable (Harris, 2008; Kirwan and Ainsworth,
1992; Annett and Stanton, 2000a).

For each task identified, the actors (defined in Section 3.4) who
should perform or participate in them are identified. Then, by
grouping the actors meaningfully, a system model is provisionally
outlined. Subsequently, the provisional model is compared to rele-
vant component-based structures in aviation, risk and accident
models and performance shaping factors. This is aimed at ensuring
completeness of the developing model. Finally, the model is vali-
dated. Fig. 1 summarises the steps of the procedure to outline
the system model.

Whilst the system description is component-based, where the
components are described through the actors that execute and
support helicopter operations, the couplings between the actors
described in the model (i.e., the processes, dependencies, controls
present during the operation of the industry) are embedded into
the HTA exercise. These are described in textual format in the
tables produced. However, since such couplings are deliberately
generic and may vary considerably in response to specific condi-
tions, coupling may be better understood on a conditional basis,
e.g., by instantiating accident/conflict scenarios of interest prior
to conducting a detailed risk assessment (Hollnagel, 2012; NASA,
2013; CAA, 2010; Svedung and Rasmussen, 2002).

An example of this is offered in Section 3.8, using the statistical
analyses of worldwide offshore helicopter accidents during the
1997–2011 period, produced by Nascimento et al. (2014a, 2013).
In such analyses, bivariate testing revealed that nighttime acci-
dents were significantly associated with compromised pilot perfor-
mance (in the form of controlled flights into terrain – CFIT), mid-
weight twin-turbine (MT) helicopters and region operation (i.e.,
when operating away from the USA’s Gulf of Mexico).

2.3. Process validation

In order to ensure that the process outlined could fulfil its aim,
experts in the field were invited to comment on the face validity of
the process and its constituent steps. The experts were a consul-
tant in airport safety and a lecturer in aviation and transport, both
with a PhD degree in a related subject, and two senior offshore
helicopter pilots involved in safety management. All experts
agreed that the process can deliver the results intended.
3. Process implementation and discussion

3.1. High level mission analysis

The definition of offshore helicopter operations presented in
Nascimento et al. (2013) was used to analyse the mission at a
high-level and derive the associated flight profiles. According to
the definition, offshore helicopter transportation occurs to, from
or between installations at sea (i.e., fixed platforms and moving
boats) and the following eight flight profiles encompass this: from
onshore airport to offshore fixed platform; from onshore airport to
offshore moving boat; from offshore fixed platform to onshore air-
port; from offshore moving boat to onshore airport; and the fol-
lowing types of inter-installation shuttling: between offshore
fixed platforms; between offshore moving boats; from offshore
fixed platform to offshore moving boat; from offshore moving boat
to offshore fixed platform.

3.2. Identification of typical phases of flight

Phases of offshore helicopter flights were described in Teixeira
(2006) and represent a considerable improvement over current
taxonomies derived from the fixed wing-domain (e.g., CAST/ICAO,



196 F.A.C. Nascimento et al. / Safety Science 85 (2016) 194–204

interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article).
2011; see discussions in Nascimento et al. (2015, 2014b)).
Teixeira’s phases are used in this step and shown in Fig. 2.

3.3. Identification of tasks to be performed

For each phase of flight of each flight profile, HTA was applied
with the following purpose: to consolidate a holistic description
of the offshore helicopter transportation industry with respect to
its safety-critical components. The stopping rule determines that
the analysis stops when further analysis would require describing
tasks specific to aircraft types, operators (e.g., company proce-
dures) or countries. This aims to ensure that the system model is
developed at a holistic and generic level with worldwide
applicability.

The realisation of the HTA exercise was based upon the analysis
of operational procedures, observations and unstructured inter-
views (i.e., open-ended conversations over the tasks typically exe-
cuted) with over 100 experts in the field (i.e., pilots, maintenance
engineers, air traffic controllers, flight dispatchers, helideck land-
ing officers, safety officers and safety research managers) during
on-site visits to offshore helicopter operators, regulators, high-
fidelity flight simulation facilities and offshore installations. The
on-site visits occurred in the UK, Norway, Netherlands, Spain,
France, Brazil and USA.

Appendix A presents in tabular format the results of the first of
the eight flight profiles analysed, together with the identified
actors further detailed in Section 3.4. The sub-goals and the typi-
cally sequential HTA plans are embedded in the texts of the table
produced (i.e., a task should typically be executed as the preceding
one is completed). The tables covering the other flight profiles are
omitted for practicality.
Fig. 2. Typical offshore helicopter flight pro
3.4. Identification of actors

From the tasks outlined, both online and offline actors were
identified (Sklet, 2004; Svedung and Rasmussen, 2002;
Rasmussen, 1997; Helmreich and Merritt, 1998). Online actors rep-
resent the human and non-human components (e.g., people, proce-
dures, machinery) jointly operating within an environment. Their
actions, inactions or states might immediately affect safety. Offline
actors are those whose actions, inactions, or states lie dormant in
the system during task execution, frequently remaining uncalled
for until some triggering conditions occur. This classification refers
to the safety-critical components of the industry and is in line with
the concepts of active and latent failures (Reason, 1997) and sharp
end versus blunt end relations in the working context (Hollnagel,
2004; Annett and Stanton, 2000a). The following actors were iden-
tified: flight crew, cabin crew, maintenance engineers, aircraft, air-
port, offshore installations, procedures, environment, air traffic
control (ATC), helicopter company’s administrative support per-
sonnel, helicopter company’s policies, customer requirements,
original equipment manufacturer (OEM), regulatory framework.

3.5. Grouping of actors

By re-arranging the actors above, the following parts of the sys-
tem model are developed: (i) helicopter operators, which host:
flight crew, cabin crew and procedures, under its ‘flight operations’
part; maintenance engineers under the ‘aircraft & systems mainte-
nance’ part; helicopter company administrative support personnel
(e.g., flight dispatchers) under the ‘ground operations’ part; and
helicopter company policies subsumed under the ‘corporate’ part;
(ii) aircraft/original equipment manufacturer (OEM); (iii) airport;
file and phases (Teixeira, 2006, pp.52).



F.A.C. Nascimento et al. / Safety Science 85 (2016) 194–204 197
(iv) air traffic control (ATC); (v) customers, which host customer
requirements (relabelled ‘contractual requirements’) and offshore
installations; (vi) environment; and (vii) regulatory framework.
Fig. 3 outlines the initial version of the system model, which
encapsulates the parts above.
3.6. Ensuring the completeness

System structures applicable to aviation are reviewed to com-
plement the developing Offshore Helicopter Transportation System
Model and ensure that it is exhaustive and complies with the stop-
ping rules. This review is important as it corrects any bias stem-
ming from the task analysis approach which covered mostly the
tasks executed by pilots during the previous steps.

Because system structures are seldom outlined with the aim of
framing the operation of aircraft by means of a system model, this
section also reviews relevant risk and accident models. These mod-
els represent a pre-defined understanding (i.e., a set of assump-
tions) of how risks exist or accidents occur in an industrial
setting (Svedung and Rasmussen, 2002; Salmon et al., 2011;
Hollnagel, 2012; Lundberg et al., 2009; Hokstad et al., 2001). As
such, risk and accident models usually refer to some sort of pre-
conceived system structure, even though this is not always made
explicit (Hollnagel, 2012).

By reviewing only the accident models which incorporate an
explicit underlying system structure (i.e., morphological accident
models; Sklet, 2004), this section does not intend to be an exhaus-
tive review of all possible models since such reviews already exist
in the literature (e.g., Hollnagel, 2004, 2009, 2012; Rollenhagen
et al., 2010; Lundberg et al., 2009; Salmon et al., 2011; Reason,
2008; Sklet, 2004; Kjellén, 2000; Borys, 2000; Saleh et al., 2010).
Finally, the literature on performance shaping factors is also dis-
cussed since such literature refers to system elements, i.e., those
which affect human performance.
3.6.1. Aviation system and risk models
3.6.1.1. Subotic et al. (2005). The authors described the Air Trans-
port System from an Air Traffic Management’s (ATM) perspective,
in order to derive target levels of safety (TLS) for equipment failure.
The parts of Subotic et al.’s model used to complement the devel-
oping Offshore Helicopter Industry System Model are ‘aircraft sys-
tems’, ‘aircraft structures’ (both placed under ‘aircraft/OEM’),
‘ground-based ATM’, ‘airborne ATM’ (both placed under ‘ATC’), ‘in-
frastructure’, ‘handling’ and ‘fire-fighting’ (all placed under ‘air-
ports’). The other parts of the model are not directly relevant
given the stopping rules adopted.
Offshore H

Procedures

Fig. 3. Developing Offshore Helicopte
3.6.1.2. Greenberg et al. (2005). The authors proposed a model for
the safety assessment of fixed-wing civil aviation operations using
a Bayesian belief network. The model is based on each crew mem-
ber’s readiness to fly (e.g., level of fatigue, age and medical situa-
tion); level of airworthiness of aircraft (e.g., severity of any
existing malfunction); roster policy, company’s economic situation
and safety culture; society type (i.e., hierarchical or not) and
authority gradient; atmospheric weather and visibility and finally
mission characteristics (e.g., flight length and altitude).

Whereas the crew, aircraft and company aspects were covered
by the developing system model’s ‘flight crew’, ‘aircraft/OEM’ and
‘corporate’ parts, respectively, society type and authority gradient
might be largely determined by national traits (Helmreich and
Merritt, 1998; Li et al., 2007). Therefore, the ‘country of operation’
part of the model was created.

The ‘atmospheric weather’ and ‘visibility’ were incorporated as
a breakdown of the ‘environment’ part of the developing system
model. Finally, the ‘mission characteristics’ were incorporated into
the ‘regulatory framework’ part since, in offshore helicopter trans-
portation, regulations primarily dictate the limits of the opera-
tional envelope (e.g., Quintão, 2010; ICA, 2008).

3.6.1.3. Hokstad et al. (2001) and Herrera et al. (2010). The risk
model originally developed by Hokstad et al. (2001), which was
improved by Herrera et al. (2010) confers a structure for the
assessment of offshore helicopter risk on the Norwegian continen-
tal shelf. The model is composed of two sub-models which enable
the assessment of the frequencies and consequences of a range of
factors relevant to risk. The consequence sub-model was deemed
excessively detailed in comparison to the analysis’ stopping rules
adopted. The frequency sub-model led to the incorporation of ‘he-
licopter design’, ‘continued airworthiness’ and ‘design organisa-
tions’ under the ‘aircraft/OEM’ part of the developing system
model. ‘Helideck’ was incorporated as a breakdown of the ‘offshore
installations’ sub-part of the ‘customer’ part. Additionally, ‘proce-
dures’ and ‘platform metalwork’ were incorporated to this sub-
part in order to account for the marshalling and obstacle avoidance
activities listed in the tables produced (see Appendix A). Moreover,
‘airworthiness’, ‘licensing’, ‘mission requirement’ and ‘flight safety’
were incorporated into the ‘contractual requirements’ sub-part to
reflect the safety management role of the oil and gas industry.
Finally, ‘international aviation authorities and organisations’ were
incorporated as an overarching part called ‘international regula-
tions’ in the developing system model.

3.6.1.4. Gibb et al. (2010). The authors proposed an aviation system
model without specifying the origins of the elements considered,
elicopter Transportaon System

ATC Customers Environment Regulatory 
framework

Contractual

198 F.A.C. Nascimento et al. / Safety Science 85 (2016) 194–204
though these seem to have been derived from experience and com-
mon sense. The three main parts considered (i.e., environment,
pilot and aircraft) are already covered by the developing system
model. The discrimination of these parts into sub-parts led to the
addition of ‘lighting’ and ‘maritime’ under the ‘environment’ part,
accounting for the ‘day/night’ and ‘discernible horizon’ sub-parts
in Gibb et al.’s model.

3.6.1.5. Harris and Smith (1997). The 5 M system model (Harris and
Smith, 1997, cited in Harris (2006) and Stolzer (2008)) incorporates
the following six aspects that must be considered in order to assess
operations in engineered transportation systems: man, machine,
mission, management, physical medium and societal medium
(e.g., regulatory requirements). These aspects are already covered
in the developing system model.

3.6.2. Morphological accident models
Three main and four subsidiary accident models which refer to

system structures were identified. The main models were the Swiss
Cheese model (Reason, 2008), Risk Management Framework and
Migration of Work Practice model (Rasmussen, 1997) and Systems
Theoretic Accident Modelling and Processes (STAMP; Leveson,
2004). The subsidiary models identified were the Tripod Diagnostic
Evaluation Tool for Accident Prevention (i.e., DELTA) model and the
Human Factors Analysis and Classification System (HFACS), both
derived from the Swiss Cheese model; and the AcciMap and Actor-
Map, which were derived from Rasmussen’s model. Sections
3.6.2.1,3.6.2.2 and 3.6.2.3 discuss each model and their associated
subsidiary models sequentially, with the aim to complete the
developing model of the offshore helicopter transportation system.

3.6.2.1. Swiss Cheese and subsidiary models. The Swiss Cheese
model, described in Reason (1997, 2008), introduces the idea that
production systems are typically protected by defences-in-depth.
These refer to multiple layers of controls which aim to prevent
active failures from leading to an accident.

Whilst ‘board decisions and policy’ are covered by the ‘corpo-
rate’ part of the developing system model, ‘line management’
(i.e., ‘maintenance supervision’) was incorporated since it is impor-
tant for the supervision of aircraft maintenance (Herrera et al.,
2009).

From the version of the Swiss Cheese model outlined in ICAO
(2012), ‘training’ was additionally identified as a transferable ele-
ment, which was placed under the ‘flight operations’ sub-part of
the developing system model.

The Tripod DELTA (Hudson et al., 1994) is formed by a set of
checklists designed to perform health checks in organisations
according to the elements of the Swiss Cheese model. Therefore,
the elements of the Tripod DELTA have already been covered.

The HFACS was also developed from the Swiss Cheese model to
construct taxonomies of latent failures and unsafe acts using mili-
tary aviation accident reports (Wiegmann and Shappell, 2003).
HFACS has since been adapted to several domains, including ship-
ping and mining operations. The most complete version of HFACS
was developed for rail operations and incorporates failures outside
the organisation under analysis. This version was introduced in
Salmon et al. (2011).

The ‘technological environment’ led to the incorporation of
Helicopter Flight Data Monitoring (i.e., ‘HFDM’) under the ‘flight
operations’ part of the developing system model. The phases of
flight of Section 3.2 were developed from HFDM analysis.

3.6.2.2. Risk Management Framework and Migration of Work Practice
model, AcciMap and ActorMap. Rasmussen (1997; cited in Salmon
et al., 2011, pp. 3) developed the model aforementioned to deal
with accidents as failures in control loops. The model outlines a
hierarchy of actors (e.g., individuals and organisations), from
which interaction risks might emerge. Rasmussen’s model has
been applied in many domains, such as public health (Woo and
Vicente, 2003).

The element ‘associations’ of Rasmussen’s model was incorpo-
rated into the developing taxonomy to account for the influence
that class associations (described as ‘workers’ unions’) have in
rule-making and on work conditions, with an associated impact
on safety (CAA, 2014; Nascimento et al., 2012).

The AcciMap and ActorMap (Rasmussen and Svedung, 2000) are
derivatives of Rasmussen’s model and enable safety events to be
analysed by mapping them onto a system’s model structure
(Hollnagel, 2012). Both the AcciMap and the ActorMap impose a
structure onto the socio-technical system associated with an acci-
dent, with indications of preconditions and the functions of the dif-
ferent system levels involved (Rasmussen and Svedung, 2000).
However, since their underlying model has already been discussed,
these two models do not offer further insight to improve the devel-
oping Offshore Helicopter Transportation System Model.

3.6.2.3. Systems Theoretic Accident Modelling and Processes (STAMP).
This is another control theory-founded model which encapsulates
assumptions about system control and structure. It prescribes two
hierarchical control structures (i.e., one for system development
and the other one for system operation) and the interactions
between them. Like with the previous model, safety (and con-
versely accidents) are deemed to emerge from the control func-
tions outlined (Hollnagel, 2012; Salmon et al., 2011), i.e.,
accidents occur when the control of safety-related constraints fails.

‘Project management’ in STAMP refers to the management of
safety and was incorporated as a new sub-part of the developing
system model, reflecting the tasks of safety officers (called ‘safety’)
within ‘helicopter operators’. ‘Manufacturing’, ‘manufacturing
management’ and ‘maintenance and evolution’ were incorporated
as sub-parts of the ‘aircraft/OEM’ part of the developing system
model. ‘Maintenance and evolution’ refers to the technical assis-
tance provided by the OEM, which is expected to enable product
evolution based upon the feedback of the helicopter operators.
‘Implementation and assurance’ was incorporated into the ‘aircraft
& systems maintenance’ sub-part and was labelled as ‘quality
assurance’.

3.6.3. Performance shaping factors
Performance shaping factors (PSFs) were developed in recogni-

tion that the performance of humans, as individuals and in a group,
to a significant extent is determined by a limited set of factors pre-
sent in the context in which human actions take place (Hollnagel,
2004). A review of the PSF literature allows for a list of the factors
that may affect human performance in offshore helicopter opera-
tions. Conversely, it is possible to identify which parts of the indus-
try could contribute to this shaping of performance and thus,
safety.

Subotic et al. (2007) reviewed 13 taxonomies of performance
shaping factors and summarised the factors into 14 groups. Similar
factors were proposed by Hollnagel (2004) and Annett and Stanton
(2000a). Typical factors include ‘training’, ‘support of the organisa-
tion’ and ‘conditions of work’, e.g., ‘ambient lighting’ and ‘glare’. All
factors are either not applicable given the purpose and stopping
rules of the present study or are already incorporated into the
developing Offshore Helicopter Transportation Industry System
Model.

The final version of the Offshore Helicopter Transportation Sys-
temModel is shown in Fig. 4. Each of the parts and sub-parts of the
model can be further expanded if necessary to understand the
dynamics of the whole industry or specific parts of interest. How-
ever, in order to launch an initial system to support identification

3.7. Validation of results

Kirwan and Ainsworth (1992) establish that using different
experts whilst developing and rechecking a task analysis is a useful
approach to ensure that it is reliable and valid. Additionally, it is
usually better to ask SMEs to review part of the analysis than
require them to review it in its entirety. These guidelines were
taken into account during the validation exercise conducted.

Validation was achieved by observation and recording of the
tasks undertaken by the pilots during over 50 h of flight simulation
training, followed by a replay of a sample of the recordings to five
very senior offshore helicopter instructors (with over 5000 flying
hours in their careers each and in leadership positions, e.g., chief
pilot) and an open group discussion on the correctness of the tasks
captured by the researchers.

A total of 159 videos were recorded, corresponding to various
parts of the training of 16 pilots from three different companies
and three nationalities (i.e., British, Dutch and Brazilian), under-
taken in three different helicopter types widely used in offshore
operations (EC225, EC155 and AS365N2).

The flight instructors were asked to review the tasks captured
during the task analysis exercise for each phase proposed and
use the video samples if needed to assure a common understand-
ing about the phases of flight to be reviewed. A sample of 14 video
recordings was used, corresponding to the phases of flight of Fig. 2,
except for pre-flight, taxi out and taxi in. The tasks of the pre-flight
phase are only executable in the actual aircraft and the other two
phases are of lesser importance to offshore operations (see Appen-
dix A). The senior pilots agreed that the tasks captured in the task
analysis reflected both real flight experience and the dynamics of
the simulator training exercises.

3.8. Use of the system model in hazard analysis

As mentioned in Section 2.2, between 1997 and 2011 world-
wide offshore helicopter accidents in the nighttime were found
to be associated with pilot performance (i.e., flight crew), MT air-
craft (potentially attributable to design) and region (i.e., country
of operation) (Nascimento et al., 2014a, 2013). The representation
of these associations overlaid onto the industry’s system model is
presented in Fig. 5 below, which generates a straightforward visu-
alisation of where the main problems leading to nighttime acci-
dents may lie.

200 F.A.C. Nascimento et al. / Safety Science 85 (2016) 194–204
By referring to the visualisation produced, stakeholders
involved with offshore helicopter safety management may priori-
tise interventions on pilot training, operational deployment of
mid-twin turbine helicopters, operational control and supervision
policies in specific countries, or consider restricting nighttime
operations to a greater extent. It also enables required further in-
depth hazard identification efforts to be noticed and targeted at
specific issues, such as elements of cockpit and automation rede-
sign that may be necessary (Nascimento et al., 2015).
4. Conclusions

This paper established that a novel process was required to
enable a holistic view of the safety-critical components of commer-
cial air operations to be established by means of a system model.
Furthermore, the absence of such view in the offshore helicopter
domain was identified. These gaps were addressed in this paper
by the meticulous development and careful implementation of a
novel process which generated a customised component-based
system model.

In order to ensure the necessary scientific rigour, the process
and the system model produced were subject to peer-reviews
and a final validation exercise. With assured validity, the novel

system model was used to describe how the components related
to each other with respect to a statistical analysis of accident
reports. This enabled a swift look at the industry for which compo-
nent connections should receive prioritised safety interventions
with regards to nighttime offshore helicopter operations.

The novel process is recommended for describing system mod-
els in other areas of aviation. Furthermore, for the first time the
Offshore Helicopter Transportation System Model was outlined
and used to guide safety management, hazard identification and
risks analysis in this domain. The system model may remain as a
reference for holistic safety management in the future.

However, it is important to use the system model flexibly and
remain open to the need to update it as more knowledge in the
field is accrued, more data are collected, operations change, or
specific problems require that parts of the system model are fur-
ther developed and understood. In all such cases, the systemmodel
supports safety interventions by anchoring the main concepts
related to the operation of offshore helicopters holistically.

for their knowledgeable and constructive comments.

. Introduction

Helicopters are essential for supporting offshore oil and gas
ctivities around the world. According to the International Asso-
iation of Oil and Gas Producers (OGP, 2009b),  there were 2.7
illion flights over the seas in 2007, accounting for over 9 mil-

ion passengers. Because the majority of operations involve ferrying
ersonnel, disruptions can lead to discontinuities in oil and gas pro-
uction flows, with potentially severe economic, political and social

mpacts (Mitchell and Braithwaite, 2008).
With over 85% of its production coming from offshore fields

Petrobras News Agency, 2010), Brazil is heavily reliant on heli-
opters for oil and gas activities. In 2008, nearly half a million people
ere transported in over 100 thousand flights, serving mainly the
ampos Basin (Gomes et al., 2009). Macaé is the main operations
ub, with fewer movements to this basin also occurring from Cabo
rio, Campos dos Goytacazes, São Tomé, Vitória and Rio de Janeiro.
here are over 80 fixed platforms in the Campos basin, employ-
ng more than 25,000 workers. Based on the volume of passangers
ransported, it is one of the largest non-military helicopter oper-

tions in the world (Menezes et al., 2010), ranking third after the
SA and the UK yet in 2000 (Williams, 2000). Future demands are
xpected; since 2007, oil and gas activities have been boosted by

∗ Corresponding author. Tel.: +44 207 594 2705.
E-mail addresses: f.a.c.nascimento@imperial.ac.uk,

.nascimento09@imperial.ac.uk, facnas@bol.com.br (F.A.C. Nascimento),
.majumdar@imperial.ac.uk (A. Majumdar), s.r.jarvis@cranfield.ac.uk (S. Jarvis).

001-4575/$ – see front matter © 2012 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
oi:10.1016/j.aap.2012.01.014
 Safety  initiatives  can  be derived  on  the  basis  of  these  results.
© 2012 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.

the discovery of major underwater reserves (Hawkes, 2010; Leahy,
2011; Sosa, 2010; Wheatley, 2010; Yapp, 2010). As a consequence,
rotary aviation has rapidly expanded in the Brazilian oil and gas
industry (Flores, 2010; Moody, 2009).

Regulatory changes have recently occurred in Brazil in order
to enable more intense flight schedules offshore, including greater
flexibility in the execution of nighttime operations. Until October
2008, such missions were restricted to flights that were related to
search and rescue (SAR) and the transportation of severe casualties,
a rule that was subsequently relaxed to allow for their training (ICA,
2008).

Worldwide, nighttime offshore helicopter operations have been
a cause of considerable concern to both operators and regulators.
Ross and Gibb (2008) estimated that the average accident rate
between 1995 and 2007 was as high as 8.4 per 100,000 flight hours,
which is over five times greater than that for daytime operations
during the same period. Fig. 1 shows the annual numbers of night-
time accidents and their five-year rolling average, as investigated
by the authors.

The increase in accidents observed in the 2003–2006 period
occurred as the worldwide annual hours flown by offshore heli-
copter remained fairly level, between 853,000 and 895,000 (OGP,
1999, 2001, 2003, 2004, 2005, 2006, 2007, 2009b). In 2004, the least
hours were flown and the greatest number of nighttime accidents
occurred (Ross and Gibb, 2008).
A further analysis undertaken by Nascimento et al. (2011)
showed that the worldwide fatal accident rate during 1997–2007
was 15 times greater for the nighttime than that for daytime. Addi-
tionally, the number of people who were fatally injured was  also

Fig. 1. Night offshore accidents by year, with a 5-year rolling average.
ource:  Ross and Gibb (2008).

ignificantly greater at night. Between 2008 and 2009, at least
nother five accidents have lent support to Ross and Gibb’s (2008)
nd Nascimento et al.’s (2011, 2012) analyses (AAIB, 2008a, 2009;
arnett, 2009; GCAA, 2010; OGP, 2009a).

A major area of concern for offshore helicopter flights involves
he approach and landing phases, especially under degraded visual
onditions (CAA, 2010a,c; Herrera et al., 2010; Nascimento, 2009;
ascimento et al., 2011, 2012). Because current technologies can-
ot offer auto-land capabilities to helicopters, the operation relies
n the pilot’s ability to fly the aircraft with reference to external
isual cues. As such, the pilots are ultimately responsible for provid-
ng a separation from obstacles and other aircraft. This scenario has
ed to numerous accidents during night or low visibility approaches
e.g., AAIB, 1988, 1996, 2003, 2008a,b, 2009; BASI, 1993; NTSB,
006, 2008; OGP, 2009a; RVTV, 2000).

Faced with the reduced availability of daylight hours in the
intertime, helicopter companies based at high latitude locations
ave accumulated a wealth of experience in such operations and
ave also adopted a variety of approaches to risk management.

n the North Sea, for example, safety-related research projects
ave been ongoing for many years, especially in the UK (e.g., CAA,
010c; Howson, 2006; Nascimento, 2009; Nascimento et al., 2011,
012; Talbot and Webber, 1988) and in Norway (e.g., Herrera
t al., 2009, 2010; Hokstad et al., 1999, 2001). This situation has
ed to major changes in many areas, including approach proce-
ures, aeronautical infrastructure, aircraft equipment, and helideck
esign and lighting. Despite this effort, safety concerns remain, as
emonstrated by Canada’s recent severe restrictions on offshore
ight operations (Canada-Newfoundland and Labrador Offshore
etroleum Board, 2010) and by the recommendation that such
ights should be minimized in Norway, especially to ships (Herrera
t al., 2010).

In contrast to this strategy, the regulator in Brazil has been
tudying the viability of increasing regular night operations, given
he increasing demand from the oil and gas industry (Quintão,
010). Whereas in 2007, the worldwide average monthly flight
ours for offshore helicopters were about 70 (OGP, 2009b), Brazil-

an helicopters flew approximately 120 h per month, limited only
y the regulatory framework that bans night flying for the ferrying
f passengers (Quintão, 2010).

A comparative analysis between the risks of nighttime oper-
tions in Brazil versus other regions of the world cannot be
stablished based solely on accident rates. This limitation is caused
y the small number of such accidents coupled with a lack of
ountry-specific information, which is required for such a calcula-
ion to be made in the public domain. For example, the OGP reports

o not present flying hours on a country basis, and some offshore
elicopter accident reports were still unavailable in the Brazil-

an National Accident Prevention Centre’s (CENIPA) online search
sis and Prevention 47 (2012) 64–74 65

engine when this investigation was undertaken. However, a qual-
itative analysis of relevant normative documents in Brazil against
those from countries renowned for their benchmarking role in the
development of safety for offshore helicopter transportation, i.e.,
those operating in the North Sea (Herrera et al., 2010; Howson,
2006; Humphries, 2009), highlights a series of shortcomings that
may  increase risk should Brazil opt for such operations more rou-
tinely. This argument is further expanded in the literature review
section.

As Brazil rapidly expands its offshore oil and gas activities, it
is important for the country to develop helicopter operations in
a sustainable manner (Sosa, 2010). Consequently, because it is
well established that night operations are of considerable con-
cern worldwide, the identification of potential safety shortcomings
in relation to offshore night flying is a priority in Brazil, thereby
enabling lessons from other offshore scenarios to be incorporated
and applicable risk management strategies to be devised.

This research was commissioned by The Lloyd’s Register Educa-
tional Trust Transport Risk Management Centre at Imperial College
London, in collaboration with six offshore helicopter operators in
Brazil. The objectives were as follows:

• Identify the major factors that affect safety during night opera-
tions offshore at Campos Basin, Brazil, as experienced by pilots.

• Assess how these factors may  interact to breach safety barriers.
• Relate such relationships to current knowledge state.
• Recommend safety mitigation methods for future nighttime off-

shore operations in Brazil.

2. Literature review

Nighttime visual approach segments offshore, particularly
those following airborne radar approach (ARA) procedures, have
previously been investigated. Using cognitive task analysis, talk-
through-based interviews (Kirwan and Ainsworth, 1992) and
Grounded Theory analysis (Strauss and Corbin, 1990), Nascimento
et al. (2012) utilized the expertise of thirty-three Northern North
Sea (NNS) helicopter pilots based in Aberdeen, Scotland and high-
lighted their concerns in three major areas:

• Decision-making conflicts (for example, pilots staying on instru-
ment flight rules (IFR) after the missed approach point (MAP), in
anticipation of reaching visual meteorological conditions (VMC)).

• Unexpected events caused by factors encountered at the desti-
nation (for example, near collisions with the platform metalwork
resulting from unknowingly misplaced obstacles close to the air-
craft’s flight path).

• The potential for visual spatial disorientation resulting from fac-
tors such as a lack of visual cues for depth and surface texture
perception during the deceleration maneuver prior to landing
(this was  perceived by crews to be related to the interplay
of seven factors: perceptual problems, attentional problems,
human–aircraft interface problems, Crew Resource Management
(CRM) issues, training problems, procedures and environmental
aggravators).

Numerous authors have also commented on the aforemen-
tioned areas of concern, and the major findings are highlighted
below.

2.1. Decision making
Pilot decision making in degraded visual environments has
been previously discussed: Stokes et al. (1997) and O’Hare and
Smitheram (1995) related the decision-making process to inner
