tates caused by the perception of either losses or gains, leading to
ore risk-taking or risk-averting behavior, respectively. Gibb and
lson (2008) and Helmreich and Khatwa (1998) identified flawed
ecisions in a series of approach-and-landing accidents, for com-
ercial airplanes and for the United States Air Force, respectively.

ocused on helicopters, the reports produced by the European Heli-
opter Safety Team (Masson et al., 2009) and the United States
oint Helicopter Safety Analysis Team (U.S. JHSAT, 2007, 2009) all
mphasized the need for better decision making in the presence of
nclement weather. Specifically related to the trade-offs between
afety and production needs in the Brazilian offshore helicopter
ndustry, Mata et al. (2006) identified that the earnings system for
ilots, which is partially based on wage-by-the-flight-hour, could
trongly motivate them to fly under adverse conditions.

.2. Unexpected events at the destination

Morrison (2001) investigated offshore helidecks in the North
ea and highlighted that the process of thermal effects, loose items,
rongly positioned cranes, obstructions and turbulence caused by

he layout of topside structures are all critical to helicopter opera-
ions. In this regard, Quintão (2010) mentioned that the Brazilian
tandard for helideck design in force when this research was under-
aken (Marinha do Brasil, 2009) was in need of major changes in
rder to comply with ICAO guidance and industry best practice
e.g., ICAO, 2009; CAA, 2010b). Comparative analysis between such
tandards revealed further need for change, for example:

The absence of turbulence criteria for aircraft controllability
around helidecks in Brazil, whereas research in this field has been
ongoing in the British sector of the North Sea for over 10 years.
A disregard for the ICAO-mandated helideck design standards
within the Brazilian regulations. Until recently, the Brazilian
indigenous lighting and marking schemes still followed the stan-
dards that were progressively changed in the North Sea since
1995 (for a historical perspective in helideck lighting schemes,
refer to Howson, 2006).

Moreover, Mata et al. (2006) and Gomes et al. (2009) identified
hat unclear flight maps that were used to reach offshore destina-
ions off Macaé airport, Brazil, were a cause of increased workload
or the pilots. In the USA, Hart (2006) noted that, between 1998 and
003, there were five helicopter accidents resulting from obstacle
trikes at offshore installations.

.3. Potential for visual spatial disorientation

In relation to the approach and landing phases, many authors
gree that the transition from instrument to visual rules, and its
pposite transition, are especially prone to misperceptions and
rrors (e.g., Braithwaite et al., 1998; Cheung, 2004; Cheung et al.,
995; Dunford et al., 1995; Gibb and Olson, 2008; Gibb et al.,
008, 2010; Kern, 2002; Schmidt, 1999; Tormes and Guedry, 1975;
oung, 2003). In helicopter operations, such concerns have also
een mentioned (e.g., Berbaum et al., 1991; CAA, 2002a,b,c, 2007;
heung et al., 1995; Masson et al., 2009; Nascimento et al., 2011,
012; Schmidt, 1999; Smith and Foster, 1995; Tormes and Guedry,
975; U.S. JHSAT, 2007, 2009). Indeed, the standard rule that
ategorizes flying conditions into either VMC  or instrument mete-
rological conditions (IMC) has previously been criticized, because

 range of perceptual states exists in-between, in which, while the
rescribed limits for VFR flights might have been met, there are

onetheless insufficient visual cues in the external environment
o allow for human spatial orientation. These conditions are often
alled degraded visual environments (DVE), and they render heli-
opter pilots to be especially susceptible to spatial disorientation
Fig. 2. Research methodology.

(CAA, 2007). Example conditions are blackouts (such as while fly-
ing over the sea at night) and whiteouts (flying over icy or snowy
terrain).

Previous research supports the expectation that offshore heli-
copter pilots flying night sorties in the Campos Basin will be
exposed to conditions that can lead to visual spatial disorientation,
that unexpected encounters could cause safety concerns close to
their destinations, mainly if aggravated by substandard platform
designs, and that decision-making problems may arise.

In summary, nighttime flights pose considerable challenges to
offshore operators, and the phase of flight that causes the greatest
concern is the visual segment of the approach. Several studies have
indicated that human factor issues are a key concern. Thus far, how-
ever, very few studies have attempted to collect data from current
operational pilots (e.g., Nascimento et al., 2011, 2012). In Brazil, this
concern seems especially relevant at present, because the oil and
gas industry are undergoing considerable expansion and nighttime
helicopter operations are considered for ameliorating production
bottlenecks. Therefore, in order to understand the elements that
cause the greatest difficulties to pilots in this specific operational
flight phase, a methodology was  proposed, as outlined below.

3. Methodology

The general methodology followed by this study is shown in

Fig. 2 and is based, in part, on the UK Civil Aviation Authority’s
(CAA) safety assessment methodology (CAA, 2010c),  in which the
identification of hazards and conflict scenarios should initiate any
attempt at safety assessment.

F.A.C. Nascimento et al. / Accident

Because the accidents in Brazil were too few to allow a com-
rehensive understanding of the situation to be drawn, the social
ciences’ principle of triangulation was used. This method deter-
ines that findings and conclusions should be based on different

ypes of sources or inputs. As such, interviews were found to be nec-
ssary, to compliment the studies based on the accident reports
entioned on the previous section. This triangulation approach
as previously applied in the offshore context to surmount the
roblems of limited and uncertain datasets by Vinnem (2010, 2011)
nd Herrera et al. (2010), for example. Because the literature has
een scarce on the subject, knowledge elicitation from profession-
ls in the domain, currently employed as such, was  necessary. The
ollowing sections outline each step of the methodology that was
evised.

.1. Subject matter expert (SME) consultation

In Brazil, in line with the Law of the Air in other countries
e.g., JAA, 2007), the responsibility for ensuring self-separation from
ther aircraft and obstacles during night visual flight segments lies
ith the pilot-in-command (ICA, 2008). With no procedures laid

ut by the regulator for this specific phase, there may  be consid-
rable variability in the techniques that are used by pilots across
ifferent helicopter operators. To address this variability, a stan-
ard operating procedure (SOP) evaluation sub-step was  devised

n order to determine whether a single research approach could
e undertaken across different companies and to determine which
ask description would then generate comparable data. Having
nalyzed the existing procedures that were laid out by the col-
aborating companies for nighttime approaches offshore, the last
autical mile to the helideck was deemed sufficiently similar across
he operators to smooth out eventual differences in pilots’ per-
eptions caused by their dissimilar instrument descent techniques.
ive experienced offshore helicopter pilots, who were also char-
ered air accident investigators (each with over 5000 flying hours
ccrued in Brazil), agreed that the final mile to the helideck would
uffice for the purposes of this research.

Subsequently, an individual semi-structured interview
pproach was chosen to ensure both participant privacy and
o optimize the time that was available. However, because of
perational constraints that were imposed on the participants,
wo interview sessions were conducted collectively, in which
ase participants were individually consulted beforehand for their
greement. In total, there were 22 individual interviews, one with

 and another one with 4 participants.
Demographic and open-ended interview questions were

evised. The open-ended questions used cognitive task analysis
alk-through principles (Kirwan and Ainsworth, 1992) and were
iscussed and refined with the same five SMEs referred to pre-
iously, before the main data collection commenced. The final
nterview questions are outlined in Appendix A.

Additionally, to challenge the document-analysis derived
ssumption that risk would be greater in nighttime offshore heli-
opter operations in Brazil rather than in the North Sea, for example,

 specific SME  consultation step was undertaken, whereby experts
ith operational experience in both scenarios were invited to com-
are them.

.2. Data collection
Pilots from five offshore helicopter operators, which were based
t the Macaé, Cabo Frio and Rio de Janeiro airports (all in Rio de
aneiro state), participated in this research. They were selected by
on-probability convenience sampling, given various constraints,
sis and Prevention 47 (2012) 64–74 67

including availability and individual circumstances. All of the inter-
views were recorded and transcribed prior to the analysis.

3.3. Data analysis

3.3.1. Line-by-line analysis
From the contents of the interviews, relevant sections of text

were extracted for further analysis. Based on Flanagan’s (1954)
technique, which has been applied by a number of authors (e.g.,
Butterfield et al., 2005; Jarvis, 2009; Schluter et al., 2008; Urquharta
et al., 2003), a criterion was laid out whereby the statements that
directly referred to factors that affect safety in offshore nighttime
approaches were to be extracted. Given this scenario, the line-by-
line analysis technique that was introduced by Strauss and Corbin
(1990) and applied by many authors (e.g., Huddlestone and Harris,
2006; Jarvis, 2009, 2010; Nascimento, 2009; Nascimento et al.,
2011, 2012; Partington, 2002) was  employed. Because this process
generated the dataset for analysis, there was  a need to ensure that
this extraction had been reliable.

3.3.2. Interview fidelity and extraction checks
To check the reliability of the extraction process, 19 interview

transcripts (over two-thirds of the total number of interviews
undertaken, accounting for more than 68% of all of the statements
that were extracted) were sent to a qualified individual to perform a
rating and a critique (a “rater”). This rater was  an aeronautical engi-
neer with a master’s level education in aviation safety, a holder of a
commercial pilot’s license and was experienced in helicopter oper-
ations. Although prescribed rules do not seem to exist for such a
check, Butterfield et al. (2005) suggested that, typically, a quarter
of the interviews would suffice. Other authors, though, were satis-
fied with smaller samples sizes (e.g., Bradley, 1992; Oldenburguer
et al., 2007; Schluter et al., 2008). This procedure also contributed
to ensuring interview fidelity, as checks were made for both leading
questions and for consistency.

3.3.3. Template analysis
The extracted statements were analyzed according to sug-

gestions by King (1998),  who defined a template analysis as an
approach to qualitative research that produces a list of codes (a
‘template’) that represent themes identified in textual data. It is
common practice to have some of the codes defined a priori, but
“they will be modified and added to as the researcher reads and inter-
prets the texts” (pp. 118). The template that was  developed by
Nascimento (2009) and Nascimento et al. (2012) was subsequently
used and modified accordingly, and the final version is presented
in the results section.

3.3.4. Categorization checks
To ensure that the new version of the template was reliable, it

was sent to another qualified rater for the independent categoriza-
tion of 75% of the statements, which were randomly selected. In
qualitative research, 25% would normally suffice (Butterfield et al.,
2005; Jarvis, 2009). This process led to changes in the labels and
definitions of some categories and to the assignment of some state-
ments into different categories. The process was  stopped when
agreement, as measured by Cohen’s Kappa, was either good or
excellent, according to the criteria laid out by Fleiss et al. (2003).
The second rater was a Brazilian helicopter pilot with experience
in offshore nighttime operations and a master’s degree in aviation
safety.
3.3.5. Story development and SME appraisal
The integration of categories in a meaningful manner was con-

ducted using the methods of both King (1998) and Strauss and
Corbin (1998), to whom the production of a coherent story assists

n presenting the phenomena under investigation. This story was
hen sent to three other SMEs for critique, all of whom were expe-
ienced offshore helicopter pilots working in Campos Basin, with
ach having over 5 years of experience as flight safety officers. As
entioned by Strauss and Corbin (1998, pp. 159), “They should be

ble to perceive it as a reasonable explanation [. . .]  even if not every
etail quite fits their cases”. Upon positive feedback from all three of
he SMEs, the story was considered to be a credible expression of
he current status for offshore nighttime operations in Brazil.

. Results

.1. Comparative analysis of the risks that are associated with
ighttime offshore helicopter operations in Brazil and the North
ea, by SMEs with experience in both scenarios

Specific legal frameworks determine that Brazilian and Euro-
ean licensed pilots shall only fly in their respective regions. This
ule prevented establishing a comparative risk analysis across fly-
ng regions based on pilot experiences. However, because some
ompanies operate integrated safety and operations departments
ith jurisdiction in both Brazil and the North Sea, some SMEs
ho are knowledgeable in both scenarios could be consulted. They
ere a former offshore helicopter pilot and a senior safety offi-

er involved in helideck certification in Norway and in the UK, an
xperimental test pilot based in the Aberdeen–Scotland area who
as previously hired for test flights in Brazilian offshore platforms,

 helicopter safety research professional employed by a British
egulator, four safety officers employed by offshore helicopter
perators in Brazil and two aeronautical consultants employed
y the largest Brazilian oil and gas company, all of whom agreed
hat the aeronautical infrastructure and regulations in Brazil would
eed considerable changes for ensuring safe nighttime offshore
elicopter operations.

.2. Demographics

Twenty-eight pilots (27 male and 1 female, 20 captains and 8
rst officers) were interviewed, representing over 10% of the esti-
ated 270 helicopter pilots who were eligible to fly nighttime

orties out to the Campos Basin at any one time. The interviews
ere on average 18 min  long. The major demographic features of

he participants are recorded in Table 1.
The likelihood of having to perform the role of the handling

ilot (HP) on the approach phase offshore is mainly determined
y which pilot has a clearer view when landing, while facing into
he wind as much as possible. According to the SMEs who were
onsulted, the prevailing wind conditions and the platform orien-
ations should provide equal chances for each pilot to execute the
pproach task. Therefore, comments by all of the pilots, regardless
f the differences in their operational experience, were deemed

aluable for the research. Nevertheless, differences in perception
aused by different levels of experience were not found in the data.

The aircraft on which the participants were type-rated and cur-
ent are shown in Table 2.
Sikorsky S92 1
a Some pilots were rated on more than one model of aircraft.

4.3. Template analysis results

The line-by-line analysis extracted 700 statements, which were
initially grouped into 72 categories and subsequently regrouped
into 13 higher order categories. The final template is presented
in Table 3 , with a comment on the higher order code in order to
enhance the understanding of the phenomena referenced.

The links between the higher order categories that are identified
through the iterative categorization process are shown in Table 4.
Such links led to the conceptual integration of categories in the
story presented next. The numbers in Table 4 correspond to specific
phrases highlighted in superscript brackets ‘[]’ in the story, which
typify the links that are identified.

4.4. Story

Pilots perceive that the problems that they face while flying
offshore nighttime approaches in southeastern Brazil result from
three main issues: visual spatial disorientation, destination issues
and flawed safety oversights.

4.4.1. Visual spatial disorientation
Because of illusory, absent or insufficient visual cues at the

missed approach point (MAP) of their descent procedures, pilots
could find that flying strictly using external visual references is
not truly feasible after that point. These perceptual constraints can
be aggravated by specific environmental conditions, such as rain,
drizzle, patchy low level clouds or a lack of celestial illumination.[1]

Against such perceptual limitations, pilots often employ two
strategies: ‘adequate attention allocation’ and/or an ‘extended
usage of aircraft capabilities’. The latter refers to the continued
use of IFR instrumentation and automation (the auto-pilot’s ‘upper
modes’) beyond the missed approach point.

‘Adequate attention allocation’ means that if external visual cues
are still deemed insufficient for a visually referenced flight after
it reaches the MAP, pilots may engage in a process of switching
between visual and instrument scan techniques while assessing
how supportive the external environment is for visual flying. This
practice, however, is perceived by pilots as requiring a wealth of
mental resources,[2] and, as such, there are concerns over its poten-
tial to contribute to flaws in the task execution.

An effective attention allocation strategy requires, in turn, ade-
quate CRM practices. Ideally, the non-handling pilot monitors the
aircraft flight instruments and calls out the parameters that deserve
immediate attention/intervention. When this task is completed, the
handling pilot can then, presumably, focus his/her visual scan at the

external environment for a longer period than at the cockpit instru-
mentation. This practice, however, requires a set of finely tuned
crew interaction procedures, the absence of which could result in
an increased attentional burden to the handling pilot,[3] as he/she

“When we have flights to Albacorab, we have to fill in a Yankee flight plan: you go to
a ‘Visu-ment’ – a cynical nickname for the practice of mixing visual and instrume
b Albacora is one of the fields within Campos Basin.

ust continue with internal and external cockpit scan techniques
t higher rates.

Some adverse operator states have been assessed as negatively
ffecting attentional capabilities[4] and CRM practices.[5] Stress,
atigue and dread could decrease pilots’ concentration for adequate
ttention allocation, whereas any excessive self-confidence on the
art of the pilots could impede proper CRM practices onboard.

The exploration of aircraft capabilities mainly concerns the
se of automation for as long as possible on the approach, such
hat the stabilization and tracking tasks do not add to the pilot
orkload. By keeping the autopilot’s upper modes engaged for a

onger time period, pilots can put more resources into monitoring
isual cues rather than actively flying the aircraft. Once closer to
heir destinations, visual cues should be sufficient for pilots to
evert to manual flying. This strategy, however, is constrained by
he autopilots’ capabilities,[6] given the speed that is required to
isengage its upper modes. A manually controlled flight phase is

 necessity because current technologies do not provide auto-land
r auto-hover for either passenger ferrying or for emergency
edical services (EMS) offshore helicopters. Other perceptual

roblems could also occur from high nose-up rotorcraft attitudes
t low approach speeds, obscuring the view ahead,[6] and from
neffective windscreen wipers,[6] which cause external visual cues
o degrade further.

In any case, unconstrained perceptual sets could translate into
andling difficulties,[7] often in the form of flawed height and/or
peed control, which pilots perceive as capable of leading to an
ccident.

.4.2. Destination issues
The destinations themselves pose additional problems to pilots,

rimarily because of misplaced unlit obstacles that they may  not
e aware of, improper helideck illumination that blends with other
ights on the structure, and/or by excessively lit platforms, poten-
ially causing visual impairment.[8]

Unknown obstacles and unclear helidecks consume addi-
ional attentional resources,[9] because pilots must maintain extra
in point, after which you have to be in visual conditions.”

n techniques.

vigilance while flying their aircraft. There are concerns about near
misses with obstacles that could have led to accidents.

In addition, some platforms offer few approach path options that
are free from turbulent airflow or obstacles, potentially leading to
aircraft controllability issues[10] and an extra attentional burden[11]

in terms of the search for obstacles.

4.4.3. Flawed safety oversight
The operational problems that are mentioned above are per-

ceived by pilots to stem from flawed safety provisions and
enforcement, emanating from a combination of insufficient infras-
tructure for properly supporting night flights together with
shortcomings in training.

4.4.3.1. Lack of infrastructure for night flying. Only two  certified
non-directional beacon (NDB) instrument approach procedures
exist to serve more than 80 platforms within the Campos basin.
Therefore, whenever pilots need to reach one of the remaining
78 or so platforms that are not fitted with an NDB, they are sup-
posed to execute an instrument descent procedure at either of
these two NDB-equipped platforms, reach their MAP  under VMC
and then proceed under visual flight rules (VFR) to their final des-
tination. However, the distances involved often preclude such a
procedure at night, because, in effect, the pilots are faced with
external black-out conditions.[12] Hence, pilots must blend instru-
ment and visual scan techniques during the navigation task toward
their intended destination[13] or revert to instrument flying with
no formal procedures outlined by the regulator.[14] Procedures for
this scenario are often developed at a company level or on the
basis of experience, with practical techniques passed on by senior
colleagues.[15]

Because there are no formal platform charts, pilots rely on
destinations’ layouts, their helideck location and obstacles to the
approach.[16] This procedure is believed to contribute to ‘close calls’
as well as to add to the attentional burden of having to maintain an
active external search for obstacles.[17]

sis and Prevention 47 (2012) 64–74 71

4.4.3.2. Training shortcomings. Training is deemed to be funda-
mental to both the attentional allocation strategy and the crew
interaction procedures that are mentioned in Section 4.4.1. How-
ever, training was  assessed by the pilots as being too infrequent for
developing the attentional[18] and crew interaction[19] skills that
are required by the task. This result arises from on-demand EMS
sorties as well as from service contractual clauses, both of which
imply that there are few opportunities for training and practicing
the approach task at offshore installations.

Training is also deemed to be an important element in devel-
oping the awareness of the various platform layouts, helideck and
obstacle locations. Again, it is assessed as too infrequent for such
purposes.[20]

Although deemed to be of paramount importance for both
avoiding spatial disorientation and for recognizing platform lay-
out, in-flight training is perceived as having declined in recent
years in favor of simulator-based training. This result however,
relies on allocating financial resources for hiring simulators abroad
and requires appropriate simulators to be available. As a conse-
quence, scenario-based training for offshore installations appears
to be kept to a minimum (annual line checks),[21] and even then, it is
often unreflective of the environment found in the Campos basin,
because few foreign platforms are replicated by existing simula-
tor databases. Additionally, unrealistic handling qualities appear
in some aircraft simulation models in low altitude–low airspeed
flying regimes.

Finally, from a managerial viewpoint, planning for training-only
sorties over the Campos Basin is a bureaucratic ordeal, because the
different stakeholders (e.g., the air traffic control services, the oil
companies that run the destinations, and the airports from which
the aircraft will operate) must be engaged and coordinated before
any such sorties can occur.[22]

5. Discussion

In common with Nascimento’s et al. (2012) findings for the
UK Northern North Sea scenario, pilots flying offshore nighttime
missions in Brazil also felt prone to spatial disorientation in the
visual portion of the task, because of a similar interplay of influ-
encing factors. This observation is also in accord with the wider
helicopter accident-based research (CAA, 2007; Masson et al., 2009;
Smith and Foster, 1995; Talbot and Webber, 1988; U.S. JHSAT, 2007,
2009), which emphasizes the hazards of the IFR-VFR transition in
degraded visual environments. Additionally, there has been prior
mention of the problems at the interface with the destination (Hart,
2006; Morrison, 2001; Nascimento, 2009; Nascimento et al., 2011,
2012).

While decision making was  mentioned as one of the emerging
higher order codes (Table 3), it was populated solely by a comment
from a single participant, and hence, it was not mentioned in the
story. Further discussions with the collaborating SMEs indicated
the possibility that few pilots would experience decision-making
conflicts under such circumstances, because they would tend to
opt for safer behavior by quitting approach attempts in such cases.
This result also led to filtering out the ‘external pressure’ code of the
template, because pilots seemed to ignore it, as shown by the low
frequency of comments and SME  input. Alternatively, it was specu-
lated that pilots may  not have felt keen on disclosing the existence
of decision-making conflicts during the course of the research. In
any case, exploring the differences in pilots’ decision-making atti-
tudes was  not part of the objectives of the present work.
The issues encompassed by the ‘flawed safety oversight’ heading
(‘training shortcomings’ and ‘lack of infrastructure for night fly-
ing’) seem to have been the focus of the participants’ concerns.
Indeed, inadequacies of the Brazilian helideck design standards

Fig. 3. Relationships betwe

Marinha do Brasil, 2009) that were in force during the course of this
nvestigation have previously been highlighted (Quintão, 2010).
uch inadequacies established a link between participants’ com-
ents on ‘destination issues’ and ‘lack of infrastructure for night

ying’. More recently, however, updated helideck design standards
ere published by the Brazilian regulator (Marinha do Brasil, 2011).
hereas such standards are expected to improve safety in night-

ime offshore helicopter operations, it is important to realize that
hey can only address a small subset of the areas that are in need
f intervention. As identified in the British and Norwegian sectors
f the North Sea, as well as in the Irish Sea (Nascimento et al.,
011, 2012), tackling human limitations to flying at night in the
ffshore environment requires a number of concerted initiatives
hat go beyond updated helideck design standards.

With regard to training, although the Brazilian regulation has
ecently been changed to account for the rehearsal of offshore
ighttime EMS missions (ICA, 2008), in practice, this scenario does
ot seem to be encouraged, given the interplay of two  conditions.
irst, pilots’ nighttime flying readiness can be achieved by per-
orming three approaches and take-offs within a 90-day period to
nshore installations (ANAC, 2010). Second, operators are required
o request the regulator’s permission 15 days in advance if they
ant to train offshore at night (ICA, 2008). As a consequence, plan-
ing for offshore nighttime training could prove to be an ordeal, as
ommented upon by the participants:

“You have to ‘leave no stone unturned’ to do some training. They
[the regulator and oil companies] won’t admit it, but this is the
truth. You need authorization from the regulator. You need autho-
rization from the airport authority. You have to arrange it with the
person on duty at the airdrome flight information service, because
this person will have to stay for longer than normal working hours
and might have already gone home. There is the team on the plat-
form for helicopter operations, which also has other assignments
and might not be on their rest times. [. . .]  In summary, you have
to arrange so many things that it becomes a really difficult thing. It
should be easier.”

Ultimately, scenario-based training may  be relegated to annual

imulator flights, with the drawbacks mentioned in Section 4.4.3.2
bove, together with the additional load of check rides where pilot
erformance is under evaluation, as opposed to skill-development
raining-only sorties.
ots’ major areas of concern.

Participants apparently perceive training and infrastructure as
latent conditions that set the stage for both visual spatial disori-
entation and issues with the destination. Although some problems
were previously mapped by other authors (e.g., Gomes et al., 2009;
Hart, 2006; Mata et al., 2006; Morrison, 2001), papers uncover-
ing the interplay between these factors seem absent, despite its
importance for effective safety promotion.

6. Conclusions

It  is well documented that offshore nighttime operations are a
cause of great concern and that, specifically, the approach phase has
been linked to many accidents. Few studies, however, have focused
on eliciting its hazards and subsequently proposing targeted inter-
ventions to enhance the safety of this flight phase.

Because offshore helicopter activities are expanding rapidly in
Brazil, this research aimed to critically investigate safety shortcom-
ings, to guide initiatives for the public benefit prior to the onset of
major problems.

This research raises the awareness of existing flaws in terms
of the infrastructure for nighttime flights. Specifically, these con-
cerns have the potential to lead to both spatial disorientation on the
approach and undesirable near misses when closer to the destina-
tion. Both of these scenarios increase the need for training, and it
is, therefore, of concern that training was perceived by crews to be
in decline. Flawed infrastructure and training shortcomings appar-
ently stem from inadequate safety oversight mechanisms. These
relationships are summarized in Fig. 3.

These conclusions have also been further endorsed by the pilots’
suggestions on how to improve safety, as queried during the inter-
views. Suggestions concentrated on providing more training and
fitting platforms with glide path indicators. The former would
tackle ‘training shortcomings’, whereas the latter, if proved fea-
sible, could improve the ‘lack of infrastructure for night flights’ to
some extent.

While such mitigation measures undoubtedly have cost impli-
cations, this research suggests that, unless investment in both

infrastructure development and training provision occurs, safety
could be in jeopardy for nighttime offshore operations in Brazil.
This strategy could be undertaken, for example, by improving the
safety regulations that govern offshore flight activity, with a view

oward smoothing the bureaucracy that is needed when arrang-
ng for training flights. Additionally, regulations should also ensure
hat the minimum infrastructure for night flying is put into place
oth en-route (e.g., by the installation of NDBs to a wider num-
er of platforms or by differentiating stations for DGPS-based
pproaches with vertical guidance) and at the destinations (e.g.,
y extrapolation of the ICAO guidance and industry best practices
or helideck design). Such an enhanced role again requires con-
iderable commitment and resources from the relevant authorities
nd stakeholders. However, given the expected increase in offshore
elicopter operations, this set of actions definitely appears to be an

mperative for Brazil.

ses of reliability. To the aviation safety officers who faithfully
acilitated the data collection for this project, your contributions
ere much appreciated. The authors are also thankful to all of the
ilots who voluntarily invested their time to share their expertise

aper for their valuable suggestions.

ppendix A. Open-ended interview questions

For this research, I am especially interested in the visual seg-
ent of offshore approaches at night. You should consider such a

egment as starting at a distance of 1.0 NM from the helideck and
nishing on the helideck landing.

1. Considering such a scenario, how would you conduct the visual
approach? Describe it please.

2. Considering your description, what difficulties might be expe-
rienced while flying this segment?

3. Can you recall cases in which you experienced difficulties or
saw difficult scenarios happening to other pilots? What hap-
pened?

4. Can you recall any other specific episode?
5. If you could do anything to make night visual approaches safer,

with no budget limits or time constraints, would you do some-
thing? What would it be?

6. Do you have anything that you would like to add, such as com-
ments or suggestions, before we finish?

E L S E V I E R  

Utilisation of principles from structural 
reliability in quantitative risk analysis: 

example from an offshore transport problem 

T h o m a s  Nilsen a'*, Ove T. G u d m e s t a d  a, Jan  I. Dalane  a, W e n c h e  K. Rettedal  a & Terje Aven  b 
aStatoil, N-4035 Stavanger, Stavanger, Norway 

bStavanger University College, Ullandhaug, N-4004 Stavanger, Norway 

The objective of this paper is to discuss the use of methods developed for calculation 
of the reliability of structures as a general tool for calculating probabilities within the 
context of quantitative risk analysis. By applying these methods the analyst is enabled 
to model the system, the uncertainties and the parameter correlations separately and 
systematically. This is ensured by flexible event and system modelling by logical 
combination of limit state functions and suitable uncertainty modelling by assigning 
marginal probability distributions and correlation measures. These properties might 
enable the analyst to include more knowledge in the analysis, compared to models 
traditionally applied in quantitative risk analysis. The subjectivistic theory of 
probability, which consistently allows subjective considerations to be included in 
the analysis, is adopted and provides the framework for the discussions. The paper 
concludes that methods of structural reliability represent a useful tool for calculating 
probabilities in a great number of situations in quantitative risk analysis. An example 
from an offshore towing operation sketches how these methods can open for stochastic 
modelling more in line with the characteristics of the actual system, compared to a 
typical event tree approach. © 1998 Elsevier Science Limited. 

Abbreviations: CoV = Coefficient of variation, ETA = Event tree analysis, FORM = 
First order reliability method, FI'A = Fault tree analysis, MSR = Methods of 
structural reliability, PLS ----- Progressive collapse limit state, QRA = Quantitative 
risk analysis, SORM = Second order reliability method.

Determination of environmental load factors for ISO 19902 code in
offshore Malaysia using FORM structural reliability method
Z. Nizamani a,n, V.J. Kurian b, M.S. Liew b

a Environmental Engineering Department, Faculty of Engineering and Green Technology, Universiti Tunku Abdul Rahman (UTAR), Kampar,
31900 Perak Darul Ridzwan, Malaysia
b Civil Engineering Department, Universiti Teknologi PETRONAS, Bandar Seri Iskandar, 31750 Tronoh, Perak Darul Ridzwan, Malaysia

a r t i c l e i n f o a b s t r a c t

Article history: Environmental load factor, used for the design of Jacket platform design in Malaysia, are based on
Received 21 May 2013 calibration of platforms located in Gulf of Mexico and North Sea. This load factor is assumed to be high
Accepted 27 September 2014 for locations where moderate climate governs i.e. regions lying near equator like Malaysia which is at 71

north of the equator. For Jacket platforms, safety can be achieved by taking into consideration of hazards
Keywords: produced by rare events of wave, wind and current. Environmental loads are not like live loads acting on
Jacket platform land-based structure but are more severe due to unpredictable weather conditions. These environmental
Structural reliability loads can act with unexpected severity on offshore structures. Material strength of tubular components
Resistance and load uncertainty and joints plays significant role against risk. The resistance can also be reduced because of damage to
Probability distribution Jacket, continuous action of wave forces causing fatigue and corrosion. Four platforms from three
Environmental load factor different regions of Malaysia were selected for the reliability analysis. Probabilistic techniques were used

to evaluate and estimate the load and resistance uncertainty. FORM method of reliability was used for
structural reliability analysis of Jackets. Modified environmental load factor is proposed for offshore
Malaysia considering component, joint and system reliability analysis based on calibration.

& 2014 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.

38 Z. Nizamani et al. / Ocean Engineering 92 (2014) 31–43

ISO LRFD (MS) stands for Malaysia with a load factor of 1.25. As the
ratio of We/G increases the reliability decreases. It can be seen that
with increase of We/G ratio, reliability decreases for all cases. ISO
(LRFD) code gave higher values as compared to API (WSD), which
shows the consistency of the ISO code.

The same trend was observed in case of axial compression as
shown in Fig. 5. Here also the ISO code gave higher reliability
index at different We/G ratios. It was found that metocean
parameters influenced this type of stresses more than the other
variables. Reliability for members under isolated bending stress
was not encountered during the Jacket analysis. Only those
members which showed high ratio of bending stresses as com-
pared to axial stress were selected. The results show the same
trend and ISO was again higher as compared to API. The ISO LRFD

Fig. 3. Axial compression resistance factor for components. (MS) came in between both codes for compression and bending
cases. During Jacket analysis, compression stress was the only
isolated stress present in components out of above three isolated
stresses.

5.8. Combined two stresses

Figs. 6 and 7 are based on reliability of combined two stresses
which were also found in actual member analysis. The code
equations were similar in API WSD and ISO LRFD but the only
difference was in safety factors. The ratio of axial to bending stress
used here was 0.5 based on actual values. The result show that the
ISO reliability was again higher, but in the case of compression and
bending both curves were close. At higher gravity load API gave
higher values. This study proposes a load factor of 1.25 which is in

Fig. 4. We/G ratio vs. reliability index – component in tension. between the curves. Combined stresses used for reliability analysis
was based on 50% for axial tension/compression and 50% for

Fig. 5. Reliability index – component in compression.
Fig. 6. Reliability index – component in Tens & Bend-SKO1.

was 1.18 which is same as provided by ISO and API. Therefore, no
change is proposed for offshore Malaysia as shown in Fig. 3.

5.7. Single stresses

Jacket members under pure axial tension were not found
during the analysis. For finding reliability against tensile stresses,
only members which were predominantly influenced by axial
tensile stresses and with minimum bending stresses, were
selected. The reliability was determined for the component, using
different environmental to gravity load ratios to find the effect of
API and ISO codes. The basic equations for API and ISO were found
to be similar i.e. depending on yield strength (Fy) except their
safety factors. Fig. 4 shows component reliability of member under
stress of axial tension. The ISO 19902 LRFD code, with a load factor
of 1.35 gave higher reliability index as compared to API WSD. Fig. 7. Reliability index – component in Comp & Bend in SKO1.

5.10. Effect of variation of environmental load factor

The effect of environmental load to gravity load variations for
ISO code equations is shown in Fig. 10. This shows variation in
reliability index with respect to change in environmental load
factor. With high We/G ratio the steepness reduced and thus
reliability decreased with high We/G ratio. Higher reliability was
achieved with increase in load factor as expected.

5.11. Sensitivity analysis

Sensitivity study indicated that environmental load para-
meters strongly influenced the reliability of Jacket (Gierlinski and
Yarmier, 1993) and the same trend was found in this study. All
random input variables do not have equal influence on reliability

Fig. 9. Reliability index for component – Comp, Bend & Hydrostatic.
index output. Sensitivity analysis can be used to quantify the
influence of each basic random variable (Chakrabarty and Bhar,
2006). Table 10 shows the sensitivity index for the variables used
in this research. The most important influence was made by
significant wave height, current, environmental load model uncer-
tainty, stress model uncertainty and yield strength. The same was
achieved for study in Mediterranean Sea (Theophanatos and
Wickham, 1993) and for ISO (BOMEL(a), 2003). ISO recommends
that most sensitive α values for resistance variables should have
value of 0.8 and for load variables 0.7 (ISO-2394, 1998). The
significant wave height of Malaysian regions is lower as compared
to GOM and NS which plays important role in reliability analysis as
shown in Table 10. This study was able to achieve the comparative
target reliability using a reduced load factor of 1.25.

Table 11 shows reliability index for this study compared with
ISO study. API WSD showed more consistency for all stresses. The
reliability index obtained in this study was comparable with ISO.

5.14. K-Joints

For axial stresses and in-plane bending ISO LRFD gave higher
reliability index as compared to API WSD. For OPB case, it was
API WSD which gave higher reliability index. With increase of
environmental load, the reliability decreased significantly. Fig. 12
shows the reliability index with respect to increasing We/G ratios,
for a typical tension/compression case of K-joint. The ISO LRFD
with a load factor of 1.35 gave higher reliability as compared to API
WSD. Here ISO LRFD was plotted with an environmental load
factor of 1.35 as shown by ISO 19902 code. ISO LRFD (MS) stands
for this region with a load factor of 1.25.

X joint in tension. The ISO LRFD gave higher reliability as
compared to API WSD. This study proposes 1.25 as environmental
load factor.

6. Conclusion

The main conclusions are listed as below:

recommended, as against 1.35 currently used by the codes.

on notional system reliability index proposed by Efthymiou
and Melchers respectively. The load factors were determined
for We/G ratio of 1.0. The load factor of 1.1 achieved with a
target reliability of 4.0 is recommended for offshore Malaysia
based on system reliability.

O conceito de Fatores Humanos
na aviação

DANIELA DE ALMEIDA MARTINS

LILIANA ANDOLPHO MAGALHÃES GUIMARÃES

RUY LANGE FILHO

LEONARDO DA VINCI RIBEIRO SIQUEIRA

DIRETORIA GERAL DE RECURSOS HUMANOS – DGRH – UNICAMP
FACULDADE DE CIÊNCIAS MÉDICAS – FCM - UNICAMP

VARIG LINHAS AÉREAS

Dados estatísticos recentes levantados pela Boeing mos-
tram que 62% dos acidentes aéreos envolvendo aero-
naves de grande porte são causados por erro humano

(MARQUES, 2004). Estão incluídos neste resultado todos os
fatores contribuintes conhecidos: falha de treinamento,
estresse, fadiga, desatenção, imprudência, imperícia, negligên-
cia, erro de julgamento, falha de planejamento, supervisão
deficiente, falta de coordenação entre a tripulação, falhas de
comunicação, operação indevida do equipamento e outros.

Do ponto de vista dos Fatores Humanos, não existe a
possibilidade de uma operação livre de erros humanos. Por ser
a falibilidade humana um fato inegável, diversas teorias foram
desenvolvidas para explicar as razões dos diferentes tipos de
erro, pois alguns deles podem ser causados por simples in-
compatibilidade física, como letras impressas que podem ser
confundidas quando muito pequenas, enquanto outros podem
ser causados por complexos fatores psicológicos ou por certos

203



tipos de estressores como fadiga e limites de tempo rígidos
(HELMREICH, 1998; FAA, 2002).

De maneira concordante, Helmreich, um dos mais im-
portantes estudiosos dos Fatores Humanos na aviação, afirma que,
dado que seres humanos operem sistemas complexos, erros ocor-
rerão e que, sob situações de estresse e/ou sobrecarga de trabalho
(ou trabalho monótono, ou sub-carga), a probabilidade de ocor-
rência de erro pode ser ainda maior (HELMREICH, 1998).

Portanto, o conceito de Fatores Humanos passou a ser
de fundamental importância na aviação, especialmente na pre-
venção de acidentes aéreos, aplicando-se também em outros
contextos.

Entretanto, por ter se tornado um termo de uso
abrangente, observa-se que o termo Fator Humano para o senso
comum tende a significar qualquer aspecto relacionado com
seres humanos. Em estudos no referido campo, o termo deve
ser operacionalmente definido, evitando-se diferentes enten-
dimentos, o que pode implicar em vários entraves, inclusive
de ordem metodológica.

A partir dessa preocupação, buscou-se nesse artigo
apresentar um breve histórico do conceito de Fatores Huma-
nos (FH), abordando as principais definições e descrevendo os
dois modelos teóricos de FH mais utilizados na aviação.

Breve Histórico

O campo de estudos dos FH, segundo a Federal Aviation
Association (FAA, 2002), tem suas raízes na aviação e em dados
e estudos de manutenção bélica. A 2ª Guerra Mundial foi o
embrião para a estruturação desse conhecimento em decor-
rência dos inúmeros acidentes ocorridos e da necessidade de
adaptar, então, veículos militares, aviões e demais equipamen-
tos bélicos, às características físicas e psicofisiológicas dos sol-
dados, especialmente em situações de emergência e pânico
(VIDAL, 1999).

204



Segundo a mesma fonte, o primeiro trabalho identifi-
cado na área de projeto de equipamentos e desempenho hu-
mano foi realizado durante esse período. Havia a preocupação
em eliminar certos acidentes relacionados ao projeto do cockpit1

e ao desempenho da tripulação. De fato, a maior parte dos tra-
balhos pioneiros relacionados ao projeto de equipamentos, trei-
namento, desempenho humano sob estresse, vigilância e outros
tópicos foram conduzidos e publicados no pós-guerra.

A FAA (2002) ressalta ainda que, durante esse perío-
do, o rápido aperfeiçoamento dos sistemas técnicos expôs o
equívoco de se ignorar a pessoa, isto é, o piloto, no sistema.
Erros humanos induzidos pelo sistema, como a leitura errada
de altímetros ou a seleção errada dos controles do cockpit, fo-
ram reduzidos ou eliminados através de uma melhor interface
entre piloto-cockpit.

Dessa experiência no pós-guerra, principalmente nos
EUA e na Inglaterra, os profissionais envolvidos em equipes
multidisciplinares (médicos, engenheiros e psicólogos) decidi-
ram continuar essa empreitada, voltando-se, com sucesso, para
a produção da indústria civil (VIDAL, 1999).

Nos EUA, segundo a FAA (2002), o campo de estudos
sobre os FH foi reconhecido em 1957, durante a fundação e o
primeiro encontro da Human Factors Society. Já na Europa, um
campo profissional paralelo, conhecido como Ergonomia, já vi-
nha se desenvolvendo havia ao menos uma década. Na Ingla-
terra, a Ergonomics Research Society, atualmente conhecida como
The Ergonomics Society, foi criada em 1947. Dentro desse movi-
mento nasceu a corrente da Ergonomia denominada de Enge-
nharia de FH (Human Factors Engeneering – HFE) (VIDAL, 1999).

Os profissionais americanos da área de FH tinham como
foco inicial os elementos relacionados ao desempenho huma-
no, que incluíam alguns componentes psicológicos. Já a cor-
rente da Ergonomia se concentrava mais nos aspectos
biomecânicos e biofísicos do trabalho. Entretanto, ambas bus-
cavam uma melhor conformação da interface entre pessoas e
sistemas técnicos (FAA, 2002).

205



Segundo Vidal (1999), os principais tratados de
Ergonomia, tendo como abordagem dominante a do HFE, fo-
ram produzidos nos anos 60, sendo os mais representativos os
de Woodson e Conover (EUA), em 1966, e Grandjean (Suíça),
em 1974.

Posteriormente, nos EUA, a Human Factors Society mu-
dou seu nome para a Human Factors and Ergonomics Society, fa-
zendo com que ambos os termos (FH e ergonomia) passassem
a ser utilizados de maneira alternada, como, por exemplo, o
faz a FAA (FAA, 2002).

Observa-se então que as principais ferramentas utili-
zadas em FH foram desenvolvidas entre a década de 40 e 50.
Dentre essas, a análise e a investigação de erros auxiliou os
engenheiros a enfocar a elaboração de projetos voltados para o
trabalhador. Já a análise da tarefa proporcionou a elaboração
de um procedimento sistemático para a compreensão e a pre-
dição de incompatibilidade entre operador-sistema que fosse
capaz de levar a erros (MAURIÑO, 2004, p. 13).

Segundo o autor acima citado, inúmeras pesquisas
quantitativas acerca das capacidades humanas físicas e men-
tais levaram ao desenvolvimento de uma vasta base de dados e
de princípios específicos de projetos. Dessa maneira, a notável
redução de acidentes aéreos nas últimas 4 décadas se deve, em
parte, à redução sistemática do erro do piloto, através da apli-
cação dos conceitos e das técnicas baseadas no estudo dos FH.

Conceito

Segundo a International Civil Aviation Organization
(ICAO, 2003, p.1-1), o elemento humano é “a parte mais flexí-
vel, adaptável e valiosa dentro do sistema aeronáutico, mas é também
a que está mais vulnerável às influências externas que poderão vir a
afetar negativamente o seu desempenho”.

Como a maior parte dos acidentes e incidentes aéreos
resulta de um desempenho humano menor do que o “ótimo”

206



necessário, surgiu uma tendência a atribuí-los meramente ao
erro humano. Entretanto, o termo “erro humano” é de pouca
ajuda para a prevenção de acidentes e incidentes aéreos. Ape-
sar de ele indicar ONDE o colapso do sistema ocorreu, ele não
oferece respostas precisas sobre COMO ele ocorreu.

De maneira geral, o conceito de Fator Humano tende
a ser identificado como um ramo da Medicina por conta da
tradição de estudos iniciais realizados na aviação, que o relaci-
onavam com os efeitos, nas pessoas, do ruído, do calor, do frio,
da vibração e da aceleração. Entretanto, seu alcance e signifi-
cado é muito mais complexo e amplo (FAA, 2002).

Na aviação, o estudo do Fator Humano abarca todos
os aspectos do comportamento e desempenho humanos: a to-
mada de decisões e outros processos cognitivos; o projeto dos
instrumentos e das cabines de pilotagem; as comunicações e o
suporte lógico dos computadores; mapas, cartas, manuais de
operações de aeronaves; check list, entre outros, transforman-
do-se numa ciência multidisciplinar por natureza (ICAO, 2003).

Para HAWKINS (1993), a atenção aos FH busca pro-
porcionar os melhores resultados possíveis da relação entre as
pessoas e as suas atividades, através de uma aplicação sistemá-
tica das Ciências Humanas, integrada aos conceitos da Enge-
nharia de Sistemas. Seus objetivos são a garantia da eficiência
do sistema que inclui a segurança, a eficiência e o bem-estar
do indivíduo, tendo como ponto de partida de análise as
interações entre os indivíduos, os grupos e as organizações às
quais pertencem, e as interações entre as organizações que cons-
tituem o sistema da aviação.

Para a ICAO (2003), o conceito de Fator Humano refe-
re-se ao estudo das capacidades e das limitações huma-
nas oferecidas pelo local de trabalho. É o estudo da interação
humana em suas situações de trabalho e de vida: entre as pes-
soas e as máquinas e equipamentos utilizados, os procedimentos
escritos e verbais, as regras que devem ser seguidas, as condições
ambientais ao seu redor e as interações com as outras pessoas.
Todos esses aspectos podem influenciar no comportamento no
trabalho de maneira a poder afetar a saúde e a segurança.

207



Na aviação, segundo a FAA (2002), esse conceito en-
volve um conjunto de cuidados médicos, pessoais e biológicos
para uma ótima operação da aeronave, a manutenção aero-
náutica e o controle de tráfego aéreo, o que compreende um
esforço multidisciplinar com o objetivo de gerar e compilar in-
formações sobre a potencialidade e a limitação humana, alian-
do estas informações aos equipamentos, sistemas,
procedimentos, tarefas, ambiente, treinamento e
gerenciamento, buscando assim alcançar a segurança e a
performance humana efetiva.

O ponto crucial acerca dos FH reside no fato de que as
pessoas não devem ser consideradas de maneira isolada de
outros componentes, dado que todos estão em interação, tor-
nando impossível que se mude um aspecto do sistema sem
considerar seus efeitos nos outros elementos.

Dessa maneira, na definição do FAA (2002), o Fator
Humano é o estudo do ser humano como parte central de qual-
quer sistema:

• identificando suas capacidades e limitações para, pos-
teriormente, adaptá-las conforme os demais compo-
nentes do sistema;

• quantificando o desempenho humano através de me-
didas como tempo, unidades de trabalho, segurança,
erro, e mudanças necessárias relacionadas a uma situ-
ação específica;

• projetando ou modificando os sistemas de acordo
com as necessidades identificadas, buscando o melhor
desempenho humano possível.

Como conseqüência desses procedimentos, a atenção
operacional aos FH pode elevar a eficiência, a produtividade e
a segurança no ambiente aeronáutico, o que se traduz em con-
trole de custo e segurança contínua (ICAO, 2003).

208



Dessa maneira, na prevenção e na investigação de aci-
dentes aéreos deve-se considerar o avião (equipamento), o ser
humano que o opera, projeta, fabrica e faz sua manutenção, o
meio e as condições em que a atividade se desenvolve (condi-
ções atmosféricas, cabine de pilotagem, meio social, familiar
e laboral em que vive e trabalha) – ou seja, o trinômio o
HOMEM – o MEIO – a MÁQUINA que constitui a base e o
objeto de toda a atividade de prevenção.

Principais modelos teóricos em FH

Um dos modelos que possibilita uma compreensão grá-
fica da necessidade de se ajustar os componentes envolvidos
na atividade aérea é o Modelo SHELL, desenvolvido inicial-
mente por Edwards, em 1972, modificado posteriormente por
Hawkins, em 1975 (ICAO, 2003), como abaixo:

S – simboliza Software (suporte lógico)
H – simboliza Hardware (equipamento, máquina)
E – simboliza Environment (ambiente)
L – simboliza Liveware (elemento humano)

FIGURA 1 – Representação gráfica do modelo SHELL
Fonte: ICAO (2003, p. 1-7)

209



O modelo SHELL é representado por um diagrama de
blocos (figura 1) baseado nas iniciais de seus componentes,
tendo o homem (liveware) como o elemento central. As
interfaces entre cada componente (S – H – E) são vinculadas
ao componente L (o elemento humano), devem adaptar-se e
ajustar-se a ele, e não envolvem as interfaces que se encon-
tram fora dos FH (máquina – máquina, máquina – ambiente,
suporte lógico – máquina).

O elemento humano (liveware) é o componente mais
crítico e flexível no sistema. Apesar das pessoas estarem sujei-
tas a consideráveis variações em termos de desempenho e so-
frerem muitas limitações, grande parte desses fatores são
atualmente previsíveis. As margens dos blocos desenhados (as
áreas de contato entre os componentes do modelo) não são
simples e retas, portanto, os outros componentes do sistema
devem ser cuidadosamente combinados entre si se algum
estressor ou colapso eventual tiver que ser evitado, pois uma
falha nessa combinação poderá ser uma importante fonte de
erro humano.

Segundo a ICAO (2003), para que se alcance esse en-
cadeamento é essencial que haja uma compreensão das carac-
terísticas do componente central – o homem – quanto a:

a) Tamanho físico e forma

b) Necessidades físicas

c) Características de input – O aparelho sensório humano
é capaz de coletar informações do mundo a sua volta,
capacitando as pessoas a responder aos eventos exter-
nos e a realizar as tarefas necessárias. Entretanto, to-
dos os sentidos estão sujeitos à deterioração por uma
razão ou outra.

d) Processamento de informações – Essa capacidade hu-
mana possui limitações relevantes. Projetos de instru-
mentos e de sistemas de alerta precários resultaram
freqüentemente de uma falha em considerar as

210



potencialidades e as limitações do sistema de
processamento de informações humano.

e) Características de output – Assim que a informação é
percebida e processada, mensagens são enviadas aos
músculos para iniciar a resposta desejada, que pode
ser desde um movimento objetivando o controle físico
até o início de alguma forma de comunicação.

f) Tolerância ambiental – Temperatura, pressão, umida-
de, barulho, hora do dia, claridade e escuridão podem
interferir no desempenho e no bem estar de uma pes-
soa. A altura, os espaços confinados e um ambiente de
trabalho monótono ou estressante podem influenciar
no desempenho humano.

A partir dos aspectos acima descritos, os demais com-
ponentes do modelo se relacionam e se adaptam a esse com-
ponente central como a seguir (FAA, 2002; ICAO, 2003):

• Elemento Humano – Máquina: é a interface mais
freqüentemente considerada e relaciona-se com os
ajustes da máquina ao corpo humano (assento, telas,
controles, etc). Entretanto, por uma característica na-
tural do homem de se adaptar aos desajustes, é possí-
vel encobrir-se uma deficiência nesse sistema, mas isso
não eliminará a sua existência. Erros podem advir da
má localização ou da identificação inadequada de bo-
tões e alavancas, por exemplo, o que justifica a impor-
tância da análise dessa relação desde o início do projeto
de um sistema.

• Elemento Humano – Software (Suporte Lógico): envol-
ve o ser humano e os aspectos não físicos do sistema,
tais como procedimentos, apresentação geral dos ma-
nuais e check lists, símbolos e programas de computa-
dor. Os problemas nessa interface não são objetivos e
são de difícil solução, podendo resultar da má inter-
pretação de mapas confusos, de documentação enga-

211



nosa ou composta por excessivos elementos, incluindo
a automação.

• Elemento Humano – Ambiente: foi uma das interfaces
que primeiro se reconheceu na aviação. As medidas
adotadas inicialmente visavam garantir a adaptação do
ser humano ao ambiente (capacetes, trajes de vôo,
macacão anti-G2, máscaras de oxigênio, entre outros).
Posteriormente, buscou-se adaptar o ambiente ao ho-
mem (pressurização e sistema de ar condicionado, iso-
lamento acústico). Hoje, as preocupações incluem
também as conseqüências das concentrações de ozô-
nio e de radiação em vôos de altitude, alteração do rit-
mo circadiano em viagens transcontinentais, erros de
percepção provocados por condições metereológicas,
aspectos organizacionais, o contexto político e econô-
mico da empresa aérea e suas restrições.

• Elemento Humano – Elemento Humano: é a interface
que envolve as relações interpessoais entre as equipes
de trabalho, sendo relevantes aspectos como lideran-
ça, cooperação, trabalho em equipe, interação de per-
sonalidades, relações de trabalho. A tripulação, os
controladores de tráfego aéreo, os engenheiros e téc-
nicos de manutenção e os demais profissionais
operacionais funcionam como um grande grupo, e a
influência grupal exerce importante papel na determi-
nação de comportamentos e de desempenho. É nessa
interface que atuam os programas de gerenciamento
de recursos de cabine, como, por exemplo, o CRM, in-
cluindo a cultura corporativa, o clima organizacional,
as pressões em funcionamento dentro da empresa, que
são capazes de afetar significativamente o desempe-
nho humano.

• Outro modelo de FH de grande relevância para a avi-
ação é o Modelo de Gerenciamento de Erros, desen-
volvido pelo inglês James Reason, que inicialmente
concebe a indústria aeronáutica como um sistema de
produção complexo, interativo e organizado. Nesse mo-
delo, Reason analisa o modo como os seres humanos

212



contribuem para as falhas desse sistema a ponto de
produzir um acidente aeronáutico.

• Dessa maneira, para o autor, um acidente é causado
por uma série de fatores múltiplos contribuintes, que
se formam através de uma cadeia de eventos (falhas
latentes em interação com falhas ativas) que rompem
as barreiras defensivas (REASON, 2000). As barreiras
defensivas seriam então os filtros desenvolvidos pelas
organizações com o objetivo de remover, minimizar
ou proteger-se de danos operacionais.

• Dentro desse enfoque, para se compreender os fatores
causais de um acidente dentro da cadeia de eventos
deve-se considerar inicialmente o tipo de falha
(REASON, 1995; 2000; 2002):

• FALHAS ATIVAS – São os atos inseguros de efeito ime-
diato, geralmente cometido por operadores, em conta-
to direto (na “linha de frente”) com o sistema (pilotos,
controladores de tráfego aéreo, entre outros). Podem
assumir diferentes formas: falha, lapso, perda, engano
e violações de conduta.

• FALHAS LATENTES – São os elementos patogênicos que
residem no sistema. Ficam latentes por muito tempo e
demoram a se manifestar, até que se combinam com
algum erro ativo que cria a oportunidade de ocorrência
de um acidente, dependendo das defesas existentes. Es-
tão ligados a decisões equivocadas ou falhas cometidas
por profissionais que não estão necessariamente presen-
tes nem no local nem na hora em que o acidente ocor-
re (fabricante, decisões gerenciais e manutenção). Essas
decisões estratégicas possuem o potencial de introduzir
os elementos patogênicos no sistema e, por sua caracte-
rística latente, podem ser identificadas e remediadas an-
tes da ocorrência de um evento adverso, o que permite
uma atitude pró-ativa no gerenciamento do erro. En-
tretanto, a maioria dos erros latentes só é descoberta
quando uma defesa falha.

213



REASON (2000) escolheu a imagem gráfica do “quei-
jo suíço” (figura 2) para explicar seu modelo, inspirado na
“teoria dos dominós” desenvolvida por Heinrich, que repre-
sentaria a trajetória do acidente através das camadas defensi-
vas do sistema.

FIGURA 2 – O modelo do “queijo suíço” de Reason
Fonte: Reason (2000, p. 769).

Segundo LIBERMAN (2004), a imagem de um mundo
“ideal” seria representada pelas sucessivas camadas defensivas
que permaneceriam intactas e assim impediriam a penetração
de possíveis acidentes. Entretanto, no mundo “real” as cama-
das de defesa apresentam fraquezas ou “buracos” que se movi-
mentam dinamicamente em resposta às ações dos operadores
do sistema.

Na imagem do “queijo suíço”, as falhas ativas causam
acidentes quando combinadas com rupturas nas camadas de
defesa. As falhas latentes são “janelas” nas defesas do sistema
que, ao se combinarem com falhas ativas, criam uma trajetó-
ria de oportunidades de acidente através de algumas ou de
todas as camadas protetoras do sistema. São estas janelas ali-
nhadas nas várias defesas que constituem um evento. Dessa
maneira, os caminhos das falhas ativas e latentes se juntam

214



para criar trajetórias completas ou parciais de oportunidades
de acidentes (REASON, 2000).

Para REASON (1995), as falhas humanas, mais do que
as técnicas, representam a maior ameaça a sistemas complexos
e potencialmente perigosos. Isso ocorre porque os problemas
relacionados aos FH são produto de uma corrente de causas na
qual os fatores psicológicos individuais (desatenção momentâ-
nea, esquecimento, entre outros) são os elos últimos e menos
gerenciáveis. Preocupações ou distrações são condições neces-
sárias para se cometer deslizes e lapsos. Além disso, sua ocor-
rência é quase impossível de se predizer ou controlar
efetivamente. O mesmo pode ser dito dos fatores associados ao
esquecimento. Dessa maneira, estados mentais que contribu-
em para o erro são extremamente difíceis de serem gerenciados;
podem acontecer para as melhores pessoas a qualquer tempo.

Além disso, as pessoas nunca agem isoladamente. Seus
comportamentos são moldados pelas circunstâncias. O mesmo
vale para os erros e violações. A probabilidade de um ato inse-
guro ser cometido é altamente influenciada pela natureza da
tarefa e pelas condições do trabalho. Isso, por sua vez, é produ-
to dos fatores organizacionais. Ganhos significativos em segu-
rança podem ser alcançados através de pequenas modificações
nos equipamentos e no local de trabalho. Por outro lado, trei-
nar as pessoas para trabalhar em equipe custa pouco e traz
avanços relevantes para o desempenho humano na aviação.

A partir do acima exposto pode-se entender que o aci-
dente (ou o quase-acidente3) ocorre dentro de um determina-
do contexto, que não pode ser desprezado na análise dos fatores
contribuintes, partindo assim de uma perspectiva global e
sistêmica da situação, considerando todas as variáveis
(REASON, 2002).

A alta incidência da chamada ‘falha pessoal’ nas con-
clusões das investigações quanto às causas dos acidentes aero-
náuticos mostra, claramente, que a identificação do Fator
Humano é mais complexa do que se imagina.

A crescente literatura sobre a cultura de segurança e a
proliferação de instrumentos desenvolvidos para mensurá-la

215



(WIEGMANN et al., 2003) aponta para a importância das vari-
áveis organizacionais como condições latentes importantes para
a ocorrência dos erros.

Para WIEGMANN & SHAPPELL (1997), as diversas clas-
sificações de erros utilizadas na indústria aeronáutica enfatizam
o papel organizacional assim como as variáveis individuais. As
últimas apresentam graves desvantagens para a prevenção de
acidentes, pois focalizam os erros nos indivíduos, que são cul-
pados por falhas como o esquecimento, a falta de atenção ou a
fraqueza moral.

Numa perspectiva de análise sistêmica, FOGARTY
(2004) atesta que os modelos descritivos sobre as causas de
acidentes sugerem que o indivíduo erra por causa de elemen-
tos organizacionais patogênicos latentes, capazes de criar con-
dições nas quais as fraquezas humanas são expostas
desnecessariamente. Dessa maneira, aceita-se conceitualmente
que é a interação de variáveis organizacionais e individuais
que leva ao erro.

Além disso, segundo MAURIÑO (2004), com o desenvol-
vimento do conceito e da compreensão do Fator Humano, evo-
luiu-se para a noção de que acidentes são causados através de
falhas no sistema em lugar do mau comportamento individual.

Dessa maneira, a evolução do conceito de FH transfor-
mou as relações de trabalho na aviação, trazendo uma nova
consciência acerca da importância das relações entre Homem
– Meio – Máquina e dos fatores organizacionais envolvidos, o
que culminou na criação de um modelo de treinamento co-
nhecido como CRM (Crew Resource Management).

216
