típicas” (SEVÁ, 2000). Caracteriza-se, muitas das vezes, como se os trabalhadores 

compartilhassem de um dialeto, pois o linguajar não se refere apenas às situações de 

anormalidades, mas também às siglas dos cargos, aos níveis salariais, às áreas de 

trabalho, aos documentos internos expedidos, sejam técnicos ou da área de pessoal.  

  A contribuição dos trabalhadores para a produção do conhecimento offshore não 

é reconhecida formalmente, muito pelo contrário, é encoberta pelos procedimentos, 

como se estes fossem suficientes para a garantia do pleno funcionamento do sistema 

produtivo. O saber operário, enquanto um mecanismo de transformação do sofrimento 



 218 

em prazer, implica na vontade dos trabalhadores em atuarem juntos, de suplantarem 

coletivamente as contradições e de dar continuidade à produção de um know how 

offshore.  

 Desse modo, torna-se fundamental que um trabalhador não se coloque como 

mero executante, que use o seu conhecimento prático, suas habilidades tácitas, suas 

interpretações e técnicas em termos práticos e concretos. 

 “(...) o pessoal de dezoito, vinte anos de casa, pessoal nasceu com a 

plataforma. Pessoal que nasceu lá praticamente. Saiu de um tubo lá de 

óleo, de gás. Primeiro poço que furou saiu os caras”.  

Esta fala só vem ratificar a representação do grau e da intensidade da experiência 

dos trabalhadores mais antigos, pois muitos deles fizeram parte dos pioneiros das 

plataformas da Bacia de Campos. 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 



 219 

CONCLUSÃO 

 
 
 A construção da identidade do trabalhador offshore próprio da Petrobras foi 

moldada ao longo do processo histórico da empresa. A sua face bifacial, pública e 

privada, está presente em sua estrutura e nos processos organizacionais, nas narrativas 

dos trabalhadores e na identidade destes homens. Essa ambigüidade intrínseca conferiu-

lhe características que ora remetiam exclusivamente à racionalidade empresarial, ora a 

uma atuação típica das organizações de Estado. 

Aos traços de uma gestão autoritária, hierarquizada, militarizada, que perpassou 

toda trajetória da empresa, desde sua criação, se associam a uma identidade moldada a 

partir da campanha “O Petróleo É Nosso”, de cunho nacionalista e que permaneceu 

presente, principalmente, na voz das lideranças do movimento sindical. Hoje, sentem-se 

frustrados pela interrupção dessa história de conquistas, demonstrando um total 

descrédito pelas lideranças atuais, identificando-as somente com os interesses da 

empresa, desconsiderando os anseios da categoria.    

O trabalhador offshore, como pioneiro da indústria petrolífera offshore brasileira, 

vai gradativamente assumindo a face de uma identidade de herói nacionalista, do 

guerreiro que trava lutas cotidianas no enfrentamento das intempéries climáticas e na 

complexidade dos riscos técnicos e coletivos inerentes a atividade produtiva offshore. 

Ele se auto-representa como um verdadeiro “bandeirante do mar” que em suas aventuras 

durante 14 dias, descobre e produz o tão almejado “ouro negro”, cobiçado por tantos. 

A década de 90 deixou marcas profundas nessa identidade. Os ideais políticos 

governamentais assolaram a gestão e as práticas organizacionais. Os planos de 

incentivos às demissões voluntárias e de estímulo às aposentadorias foram medidas 

largamente utilizadas. Tentativas de redução do período de folgas dos trabalhadores 

offshore, de 21 dias para 14, retornando ao regime anterior à Constituição de 1988, 

foram seguidamente colocadas na mesa de negociação em troca de indenizações e 

tinham como propósito “comprar o descanso, o lazer, os dias de convivência com a 

família, os filhos, os amigos etc”. Esse ciclo histórico, de desnacionalização, de 

desestatização e de desemprego em massa foi afastado, pelo menos por enquanto, 

enquanto um fantasma que circundava as expectativas dos trabalhadores.  



 220 

No novo milênio, o sonho longínquo dos pioneiros, da década de 70, de alcançar 

a auto-suficiência brasileira em petróleo, torna-se realidade. O início da produção da 

plataforma P-50, no campo de Albacora Leste, na Bacia de Campos, em 21 de abril 

deste ano, ratifica os traços de pioneirismo e vanguardismo que permanecem marcando 

a história da empresa e dos trabalhadores offshore.  

 Os traços dessa identidade são apropriados pela empresa como estratégia para 

criar novos desafios a serem superados, elevando conseqüentemente sua produtividade, 

suas riquezas e o seu lucro. A auto-suficiência como lema que aglutina e integra é forte 

o suficiente para envolver não somente aqueles que estão, há tanto tempo, nesse 

processo, como também os “borrachos” que facilmente assimilam e internalizam o 

conteúdo ideológico da linguagem empresarial.  

 O trabalhador, sujeito histórico que participa e assume a liderança do movimento 

sindical, da mesma forma que trava lutas políticas e ideológicas diante do poderio do 

capital, também se sente prisioneiro de um regime de efeito totalizante, que o aliena, 

que o controla e que não o valoriza, nem o reconhece. Sofre com as conseqüências da 

jornada de trabalho, o turno de revezamento, o confinamento, a distância de terra, de 

contatos com sua rede social de relações e, que não somente trabalha no arquipélago de 

aço da Bacia de Campos, mas que também neste vive por 14 dias.   

Pessanha (2001) afirma que como professor na Escola Técnica Federal de 

Campos, muito contribuiu para a formação dos jovens estudantes, na época, que 

ansiavam pelo sonho em trabalhar e pertencer à classe de trabalhadores até então mais 

bem remunerada e valorizada na sociedade local (Campos). A sua experiência mostra o 

quanto esses “desbravadores do nosso tempo”, ainda permanecem não-visíveis aos 

olhares dos estrangeiros a essa realidade. Esse reconhecimento pode vir de pequenos 

atos simbólicos, de uma gestão menos autoritária, de estímulos a permanecer se 

aperfeiçoando, se capacitando, estimulando a formação de uma competência técnica e 

de um know how que fazem a diferença diante da imprevisibilidade dos processos e 

sistemas.  

Diante dos inúmeros procedimentos operacionais, estes não são suficientes para 

minimizar os riscos e evitar os acidentes, algumas vezes com conseqüências 

irreversíveis. Estes procedimentos estão na esfera do trabalho prescrito, o trabalho real 



 221 

depende do saber offshore, do conhecido “feeling” da execução, da experiência 

profissional na área que também não é valorizada. 

Hoje, esses “heróis-pioneiros” com mais de 40 anos de idade, se sentem, ainda, 

meros desconhecidos heróis ou vítimas de toda complexidade imposta pela condição de 

trabalho offshore. Nessa ambigüidade de suas vidas, o guerreiro, em sua luta diária, 

busca estratégias para se proteger dos riscos dos processos e da organização do trabalho 

que invadem seu corpo e sua mente.   

Nesse sentido, o trabalhador, até na mais simples tarefa que executa, pode ser 

desafiado a realizar escolhas diante das determinações prescritas. Nesse sentido, a 

atividade não pode ser entendida apenas pelo que o trabalhador faz, mas também o que 

não se faz, “o que se busca fazer sem conseguir, o que poderia ser feito, o que há para 

refazer e até o que se faz sem querer”. “O homem é, a cada minuto, pleno de 

possibilidades não realizadas”. (ATHAYDE; BRITO, 2003:242)  

O processo de terceirização que invadiu a empresa e o trabalho offshore deixou 

traços marcantes, uma vez que a área de manutenção, por exemplo, nas plataformas foi 

praticamente terceirizada. Esse saber acumulado que se perdeu ao longo do tempo, hoje 

se existir, está na memória dos trabalhadores terceirizados. Assim, essa experiência terá 

que ser acumulada, interpretada e reinterpretada pelos “borrachos” que estão chegando, 

caso novas ondas e correntes marítimas não levem a empresa para outras rotas.  

O trabalhador vive conflitos e tensões, de forma fragmentada e intermitente, 

entre o mundo marítimo e a vida terrestre. A identidade que se constrói no espírito de 

aventura é comprimida pelas exigências da disciplina dos procedimentos operacionais. 

O homem parcializado no mundo offshore, pois não lhe é possível desempenhar outros 

papéis, deseja e sonha com o dia de retorno ao convívio com a família e amigos. Sonhos 

realizados, outros frustrados, desejos saciados, outros ainda por fazê-lo, comemorações, 

eventos, tempo marcado pelos acontecimentos: esse não é o tempo offshore. O tempo é 

limitado e determinado pela “escala”, pela dúvida permanente entre ficar mais um 

pouco ou pra toda vida.  

Nessa vida segmentada e intermitente, esses trabalhadores “como fumaça 

costumam sumir e reaparecer” (PESSANHA, 2001). Diante dos riscos e do 

enfrentamento do medo desenvolvem estratégias coletivas que minimizam o sofrimento 



 222 

e os alienam sob o efeito totalizante do território offshore. Solidariedade e 

companheirismo alimentam corpos e mentes daqueles que, por vezes, vivem no limite 

de suas forças físicas e psíquicas. 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 



 223

SAFETY PERFORMANCE INDICATORS – 2013 DATA 

Report No. 2013s 
(July 2014) 
 
OGP DATA SERIES 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 

International  Association  of  Oil  &  Gas  Producers



  
  
  
  
  
  

 

ublications 

P 

 

 

Global experience 
 
The International Association of Oil & Gas Producers has access to a wealth of technical 
knowledge and experience with its members operating around the world in many different 
terrains. We collate and distil this valuable knowledge for the industry to use as guidelines 
for good practice by individual members. 

 

Consistent high quality database and guidelines 
 
Our  overall  aim  is  to  ensure  a  consistent  approach  to  training,  management  and  best 
practice throughout the world. 

The  oil  and  gas  exploration  and  production  industry  recognises  the  need  to  develop 
consistent databases and records in certain fields. OGP’s members are encouraged to use 
the guidelines as a starting point for their operations or to supplement their own policies 
and regulations which may apply locally. 

 

Internationally recognised source of industry information 
 
Many of our guidelines have been recognised and used by international authorities and 
safety and environmental bodies. Requests come from governments and non-government 
organisations around the world as well as from non-member companies. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 

Disclaimer 

Whilst every effort has been made to ensure the accuracy of the information contained in this publication, 
neither OGP nor any of its members past present or future warrants its accuracy or will, regardless of its 
or their negligence, assume liability for any foreseeable or unforeseeable use made thereof, which liability 
is hereby excluded. Consequently, such use is at the recipient’s own risk on the basis that any use by the 
recipient constitutes agreement to the terms of this disclaimer. The recipient is obliged to inform any 
subsequent recipient of such terms. 

 

Copyright notice 

The contents of these pages are © The International Association of Oil and Gas Producers. 
Permission is given to reproduce this report in whole or in part provided (i) that the copyright of OGP 
and (ii) the source are acknowledged. All other rights are reserved. Any other use requires the prior 
written permission of the OGP. 
These Terms and Conditions shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of England 
and Wales. Disputes arising here from shall be exclusively subject to the jurisdiction of the courts of 
England and Wales.

Scope of reporting and data validation

Safety performance indicators – 2013 data 
 

1. Summary 

 
 
 

The OGP safety performance indicators report 
summarises  the  safety  performance  of  contributing 
OGP member companies for 2013. 

The key performance indicators (KPI) used to benchmark 
safety   performance   are:   number   of   fatalities,   fatal 
accident and incident rates, total recordable injury rate 
and lost time injury frequency. 

Third party fatalities are not included in this report. 
 
 
 

1.1 General 
 
 

·  The safety performance of contributing OGP 
member companies in 2013 is based on the analysis of 
3 771 million work hours of data. 

·  Submissions  were  made  by  50  of  the  62  operating 
company OGP members. 

·  The data reported cover operations in 110 countries.

Safety performance indicators – 2013 data 
 

2. Overall results 

 
 
 
 

In this section the primary indicators used to measure 
contributing OGP member companies’ safety 
performance are;   the number and nature of fatalities, 
total  recordable  injury  rate  (TRIR),  fatal  accident  rate 
(FAR), fatal incidents per 100 million work hours, and 
lost time injury frequency (LTIF). 

Third party incidents are not included in this report.

company & contractors

Onshore Company 

0.94  (1.58)   (41% lower)  
Offshore 1.68  (0.89) (89%  higher) Overall FAR* 

 

4 
   

The number of fatal incidents per 100 million work hours   
is  a  measure  of  the  frequency  with  which  fatal  incidents   

occur, in contrast to the FAR which measures the frequency   

of   fatalities.   Accordingly,   for   company   and   contractor 2  

fatalities, the number of fatal incidents per 100 million work  
 

 
hours will be less than or equal to the FAR.  Comparison of  

 
FAR and number of fatal incidents per 100 million work  

 
hours gives an indication of the magnitude of the incidents  

 
in terms of lives lost. 0 

·  Overall the number of fatal incidents per 100 million 2004       2005       2006       2007       2008       2009       2010       2011       2012 2013 

work hours has decreased by 19% compared with last *FAR: Fatal Accident Rate 
(Number of fatalities per 100 million hours worked) 

year and is the lowest on record (43 fatal incidents in 
 

2013, 52 fatal incidents in 2012).

onshore & offshore

Total recordable injury rate (TRIR) 

The  number  of  recordable  injuries  (fatalities  +  lost 
work day cases + restricted work day cases + medical 
treatment cases) per 1 000 000 hours worked.

Of the 1627 reported lost work day cases resulting in at least 
one  day  off  work,  1312  incidents  (81%)  were  contractor- 
related  and  315  (19%)  were  company-related  (1356  and 
343 respectively for 2012). The lost work day case category 
was provided for all of the Lost Work Day Cases reported, 
although 5% of the cases were categorised as ‘Other’. 

The pie chart shows the percentage of LWDCs within each 
of the reporting categories for 2013. 

·  The  greatest  number  of  incidents  was  reported  as 
‘Struck by’ (367 cases accounting for 23% of LWDCs), 
(2012 results showed 408 cases accounting for 24%). 

·  ‘Caught in, under or between’ accounted for 21% of the 
total reported cases (21% in 2012). 

·  In comparison with 2012, the 2013 results were very 
similar. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 

Lost work day case (LWDC) 

A   Lost   work   day   case   is   an   incident   resulting 
in  at  least  one  day  off  work.  Fatal  incidents  are 
not included.

therefore reduced to 2,008 million work hours, 53% 
of all reported hours (see Appendix A).

The   varying   ratio   of   fatalities   to   lost   time   injuries   to 
recordable injuries for 2012-2013 challenges the traditional 
notion of recordable injuries and lost time injuries overall as 
a precursor to fatalities as shown in the incident triangles. In 
some incident categories however such as “confined space”, 
“assault or violent act” and “water related, drowning”, the 
ratio will be higher as shown in the tables below.

2.11   Causal factors analysis 
 

The allocation of ‘causal factors’ to fatal incidents and high 
potential  events  was  requested  as  part  of  the  2013  data 
submission. This request was first made in 2010, therefore a 
comparison of four years of data is possible. 

To standardise the response an OGP list of causal factors 
and a glossary was provided to the member companies as 
part  of  the  OGP  User  Guide.  The  causal  factors  list  is 
divided into two sections: 

·  People (Acts) classifications usually involve either the 
actions of a person or actions which were required but 
not carried out or were incorrectly performed. There 
are four major categories of actions, with an additional 
level of detail under each of the major categories. 

·  Process   (Conditions)   classifications   usually   involve 
some type of physical hazard or organizational aspect 
out of the control of the individual. There are five major 
classification  categories,  with  an  additional  level  of 
detail under each of the major categories. 

  
  
  
  
  
  
  
  
  
  
  
  
  
  
  
  
  
  
  
  
  
  
  
  
  
  
  
  
  
  
  
  
  
  
  
  

2-20 ©OGP 



 
Safety performance indicators – 2013 data

materials/products

·  Process (conditions):  Tools, Equipment, Materials & Products:  Inadequate/defective tools/equipment/materials/ 

products

2012, 2011 and 2010.

·  People (acts):  Inattention/Lack of Awareness:  Improper decision making or lack of judgment 
·  Process (conditions):  Organisational:  Inadequate work standards/procedures 
·  Process (conditions):  Organisational:  Inadequate supervision 
·  Process (conditions):  Organisational:  Inadequate hazard identification or risk assessment

2.12  OGP Life-Saving Rules 

OGP has released a set of life-saving rules (OGP Life-Saving Fig 2.12.1: OGP Life-Saving Rules applicable to 2013 fatal incidents 
per cent of total 2013 incidents 

Rules, OGP report № 459, released Feb 2012), intended for 
 

use by the oil & gas industry to mitigate risk and reduce 
Insufficient  information 

fatalities. Each OGP Life-Saving Rule consists of a simple 
to  assign  a  rule  14% Core  rules  16% 

icon   and   descriptive   text,   providing   clear,   simple   and  
consistent communication about risks in the workplace.  

These Rules were developed by using the fatal incident and  
 

high  potential  event  data  from  the  1991  to  2010  Safety 
Performance Indicators reports to identify the events and No  appropriate 

rule  19% 
activities  that  are  the  highest  risk  and  therefore  provide  
clear instructions on how to avoid them. The Life-Saving  
Rules are split into 8 ‘core rules’ and 10 ‘supplemental rules’.  
Assessment  of  the  applicability  of  the  OGP  Life-Saving  

Rules to fatal incident descriptions for 2013 data has shown Supplementary 
that at least 67% of the fatal incidents reported are covered rules  51% 

 
by the OGP Life-Saving Rules and may have been prevented 
by the adoption of this system. Insufficient information was Fig 2.12.2: OGP Life-Saving Rules applicable to 2013 fatal incidents

3.5 FAR, TRIR and LTIF – 5-year rolling averages by region 
 
 
 

The  five  year  rolling  average  is  calculated  by  summing  the  total  number  of  incidents  of  the  five  previous  years, 
and dividing by the sum of the work hours for these years. For example, the five year rolling average for 2013 is 
calculated by: 

(Number of injuries in 2009+2010+2011+2012+2013) 

(Total work hours in 2009+2010+2011+2012+2013) 

The  number  series  involved  in  the  calculation  is  frame  shifted  along  by  one  each  year,  e.g.  2011  will  calculate 
from 2007-2011. 
   
   

Fig 3.5.1: FAR 5-year rolling average  
In order to smooth out variability in the annual values for 

per 100 million hours worked [Data from B-8]  
the regional TR

3.8 Incident triangles by region 
  

In this section the relative numbers of types of occupational Lost time injuries 
injury  are  shown  in  the  form  of  ‘incident  triangles’.  The 
ratios  have  been  corrected  to  account  for  the  absence,  in Fatalities and lost work day cases. 

some data submissions, of medical treatment cases. Recordable injuries 

Fatalities,  lost  work  day  cases,  restricted  work  day 
cases  and  medical  treatment  cases  where  medical 
treatment cases are reported for the data set. 

Ratio of lost time injuries to fatalities 

The number of lost time injuries divided by the total 
number of fatalities (LTI / fatalities). 

Ratio of total recordable injuries to fatalities 

The number of recordable injuries divided by the total 
number of fatalities (recordable injuries/fatalities).

4. Results by function 

 
 
 

In  this  section  the  safety  performance  within  different 
functions performed in the E&P industry is presented. 
Functions are defined as ‘exploration’, ‘drilling’, 
‘production’, ‘construction’ and ‘unspecified’. The 
category ‘other’ is no longer in use. See the Glossary 
of Terms at Appendix E for definitions. 
 
 
 
 
 

4.1 Fatalities by function 
 

The distribution of company and contractor fatal incidents 
and fatalities between the functions is shown for both 2013 
and 2012. 
 
 
 

2013 2012 
Fatal Fatal 

 incidents Fatalities incidents Fatalities

© OGP 4-1 



 
International Association of Oil & Gas Producers 
 
 
 
 
 

 

4.2 Fatal accident rate (FAR) – 5-year rolling average 
 
 
 

The five year rolling average is calculated by summing the total number of incidents of the five previous years, and 
dividing by the sum of the work hours for these years. For example, the five year rolling average for drilling FAR 
for 2013 is calculated by: 

(Number of fatalities in drilling function 2009+2010+2011+2012+2013) 

(Total work hours in drilling 2009+2010+2011+2012+2013) 

The number series involved in the calculation is frame shifted along by one each year, e.g. 2011 will calculate from 
2007-2011, inclusive. 
 

% of 2013 work hours   % of 2012 work hours   % of 2011 work hours   % of 2010 work hours   % of 2009 work hours 

Exploration 3 2 2 2 2 
Drilling 15 18 19 15 10 
Production 35 33 35 28 26 
Construction 25 25 24 25 30 
Unspecified 23 22 20 29 32 
   
   

Fig 4.2.1: Fatal accident rate 5-year rolling average  
per 100 million hours worked [Data from B-12]  In order to smooth out variability in the annual fatal accident 

7 rate  values  5-year  rolling  averages  are  presented.  These

Construction 

All major construction, fabrication activities and also 
disassembly, removal and disposal (decommissioning) 
at  the  end  of  the  facility  life.  Includes  construction 
of   process   plant,   yard   construction   of   structures, 
offshore   installation,   hook-up   and   commissioning, 
and removal of redundant process facilities.

with office personnel who’s work hours and

and gas industry. The database is 2% larger than it was in

·  73% of the hours reported were associated with onshore 

Overall                 2760 436          1 010 110   3 770 546 
activities, 27% with offshore activities. 

·  110 countries are represented in the database, 3 more 
than  in  the  2012  database.  Countries  are  listed  in 
Appendix D. 

·  50 companies contributed data, of which 49 companies 
contributed contractor statistics, though not in every 
case for each country of operation. 

·  Of the 50 companies, 47 had contributed data in 2012 
which accounted for 95% of the database in 2012 and 
96%  of  the  database  in  2013.  43  of  the  companies 
submitting 2013 data had also provided data in 2011. 

·  23 of the companies contributed 90% of the hours. 7 
companies  between  them  covered  51%  of  the  hours, 
and the largest contributor accounted for 12%. 

·  22% of the reported work hours were related to company 
personnel and 78% were related to contractors. 

A summary of the key elements of the database is shown in 
the table at the end of this section.

‘Unspecified (as a work function)’ is used for the entry of 
data  associated  with  office  personnel  whose  work  hours 
and  incident  data  cannot  be  reasonably  assigned  to  the 
administrative  support  of  one  of  the  function  groupings 
of    exploration,    drilling,    production    or    construction. 
Corporate  overhead  support  function  personnel  such  as 
finance  or  human  resources  staff  may  be  examples  where 
work hours cannot be specifically assigned to a particular 
function.  All  other  data  that  are  not  separated  out  by 
function are reported as ‘unspecified’.

SAFETY PERFORMANCE INDICATORS – 2013 DATA
Report No. 2013s 
(July 2014)

OGP DATA SERIES

I n t e r n a t i o n a l  A s s o c i a t i o n  o f  O i l  &  G a s  P r o d u c e r s



P ublications

Global experience

The International Association of Oil & Gas Producers has access to a wealth of technical 
knowledge and experience with its members operating around the world in many different 
terrains. We collate and distil this valuable knowledge for the industry to use as guidelines 
for good practice by individual members.

Consistent high quality database and guidelines

Our overall aim is to ensure a consistent approach to training, management and best 
practice throughout the world.
The oil and gas exploration and production industry recognises the need to develop 
consistent databases and records in certain fields. OGP’s members are encouraged to use 
the guidelines as a starting point for their operations or to supplement their own policies 
and regulations which may apply locally.

Internationally recognised source of industry information

Many of our guidelines have been recognised and used by international authorities and 
safety and environmental bodies. Requests come from governments and non-government 
organisations around the world as well as from non-member companies.

Disclaimer

neither OGP nor any of its members past present or future warrants its accuracy or will, regardless of its 
or their negligence, assume liability for any foreseeable or unforeseeable use made thereof, which liability 
is hereby excluded. Consequently, such use is at the recipient’s own risk on the basis that any use by the 
recipient constitutes agreement to the terms of this disclaimer. The recipient is obliged to inform any 
subsequent recipient of such terms.

The safety data submission process is used for the collection 
of data relating to safety performance, process safety 
performance and motor vehicle crashes. The OGP safety 
database has built-in data validation requirements and 
each company data submission is validated by the OGP 
secretariat and the workgroup (Safety Committee Sub-
Committee, Task Force or Network) responsible for the 
data set in accordance with the OGP data collection and 
reporting procedure. Any communication with reporting

Safety performance indicators – 2013 data

1.  Summary

The OGP safety performance indicators report 
summarises the safety performance of contributing 
OGP member companies for 2013.
The key performance indicators (KPI) used to benchmark 
safety performance are: number of fatalities, fatal 
accident and incident rates, total recordable injury rate 
and lost time injury frequency.
Third party fatalities are not included in this report.

1.1	 General

•	 The safety performance of contributing OGP 
member companies in 2013 is based on the analysis of 
3 771 million work hours of data. 

•	 Submissions were made by 50 of the 62 operating 
company OGP members. 

•	 The data reported cover operations in 110 countries.

Safety performance indicators – 2013 data

2.  Overall results

In this section the primary indicators used to measure 
contributing OGP member companies’ safety 
performance are;  the number and nature of fatalities, 
total recordable injury rate (TRIR), fatal accident rate 
(FAR), fatal incidents per 100 million work hours, and 
lost time injury frequency (LTIF).
Third party incidents are not included in this report.

The number of fatal incidents per 100 million work hours 
is a measure of the frequency with which fatal incidents 
occur, in contrast to the FAR which measures the frequency 
of fatalities. Accordingly, for company and contractor 2
fatalities, the number of fatal incidents per 100 million work 
hours will be less than or equal to the FAR.  Comparison of 
FAR and number of fatal incidents per 100 million work 
hours gives an indication of the magnitude of the incidents 
in terms of lives lost. 0

•	 Overall the number of fatal incidents per 100 million 2004 2005 2006 2007 2008 2009 2010 2011 2012 2013

work hours has decreased by 19% compared with last *FAR: Fatal Accident Rate  
year and is the lowest on record (43 fatal incidents in          (Number of fatalities per 100 million hours worked)

2013, 52 fatal incidents in 2012).

Of the 1627 reported lost work day cases resulting in at least 
one day off work, 1312 incidents (81%) were contractor-
related and 315 (19%) were company-related (1356 and 
343 respectively for 2012). The lost work day case category 
was provided for all of the Lost Work Day Cases reported, 
although 5% of the cases were categorised as ‘Other’. 
The pie chart shows the percentage of LWDCs within each 
of the reporting categories for 2013. 

•	 The greatest number of incidents was reported as 
‘Struck by’ (367 cases accounting for 23% of LWDCs), 
(2012 results showed 408 cases accounting for 24%).

•	 ‘Caught in, under or between’ accounted for 21% of the 
total reported cases (21% in 2012).

•	 In comparison with 2012, the 2013 results were very 
similar.

Lost work day case (LWDC)
A Lost work day case is an incident resulting 
in at least one day off work. Fatal incidents are 
not included.

2.11	 Causal factors analysis

The allocation of ‘causal factors’ to fatal incidents and high 
potential events was requested as part of the 2013 data 
submission. This request was first made in 2010, therefore a 
comparison of four years of data is possible.  
To standardise the response an OGP list of causal factors 
and a glossary was provided to the member companies as 
part of the OGP User Guide. The causal factors list is 
divided into two sections:

•	 People (Acts) classifications usually involve either the 
actions of a person or actions which were required but 
not carried out or were incorrectly performed. There 
are four major categories of actions, with an additional 
level of detail under each of the major categories.

•	 Process (Conditions) classifications usually involve 
some type of physical hazard or organizational aspect 
out of the control of the individual. There are five major 
classification categories, with an additional level of 
detail under each of the major categories. 

2-20 ©OGP



Safety performance indicators – 2013 data

2.11.1	 Fatal incident causal factors

Causal factors are divided into two separate groups, People The causal factors assigned to fatal incidents are shown below. 
(Acts) and Process (Conditions), see OGP User Guide The highlighted content indicates the top 10 causal factors 
(Report 2013su) and Glossary for details. assigned to fatal incidents in 2013 compared with 2012, 2011 

•	 34 of the 43 fatal incidents reported were assigned and 2010. Seven of the ten were the same for all four years.
causal factors (47 of 52 in 2012) Additional information on the fatal incidents reported 

•	 222 causal factors were assigned for the 43 fatal incidents by region can be found on the OGP Safety Zone website: 
•	 Between 2 and 16 causal factors were assigned per http://info.ogp.org.uk/Safety/. The information 

incident (between 1 and 11 in 2012). provided includes a narrative description of the incident, 
the corrective actions and recommendations and the causal

products
The following 5 causal factors appear consistently in the top ten for both fatal incidents and high potential events for 2013, 
2012, 2011 and 2010.

•	 Process (conditions):  Organisational:  Inadequate training/competence 
•	 People (acts):  Inattention/Lack of Awareness:  Improper decision making or lack of judgment
•	 Process (conditions):  Organisational:  Inadequate work standards/procedures 
•	 Process (conditions):  Organisational:  Inadequate supervision
•	 Process (conditions):  Organisational:  Inadequate hazard identification or risk assessment

2.12  OGP Life-Saving Rules
OGP has released a set of  life-saving rules (OGP Life-Saving Fig 2.12.1: OGP Life-Saving Rules applicable to 2013 fatal incidents
Rules, OGP report № 459, released Feb 2012), intended for per cent of total 2013 incidents

use by the oil & gas industry to mitigate risk and reduce 
fatalities. Each OGP Life-Saving Rule consists of a simple Insufficient information 

to assign a rule 14% Core rules 16%

icon and descriptive text, providing clear, simple and 
consistent communication about risks in the workplace.
These Rules were developed by using the fatal incident and 
high potential event data from the 1991 to 2010 Safety 
Performance Indicators reports to identify the events and No appropriate 

activities that are the highest risk and therefore provide rule 19%

clear instructions on how to avoid them. The Life-Saving 
Rules are split into 8 ‘core rules’ and 10 ‘supplemental rules’.
Assessment of the applicability of the OGP Life-Saving 
Rules to fatal incident descriptions for 2013 data has shown Supplementary 
that at least 67% of the fatal incidents reported are covered rules 51%

by the OGP Life-Saving Rules and may have been prevented 
by the adoption of this system. Insufficient information was Fig 2.12.2: OGP Life-Saving Rules applicable to 2013 fatal incidents
provided to be able to assign a rule for 6 of the incidents. per cent of total 2013 incidents

Dropped objects 2.3% Gas test 2.3%

Line of fire – Journey management 2.3%
safe area 32.6%

Suspended load 2.3%

3.5	 FAR, TRIR and LTIF – 5-year rolling averages by region

The five year rolling average is calculated by summing the total number of incidents of the five previous years, 
and dividing by the sum of the work hours for these years. For example, the five year rolling average for 2013 is 
calculated by:

(Number of injuries in 2009+2010+2011+2012+2013)
(Total work hours in 2009+2010+2011+2012+2013)

The number series involved in the calculation is frame shifted along by one each year, e.g. 2011 will calculate 
from 2007-2011.

Fig 3.5.1: FAR 5-year rolling average In order to smooth out variability in the annual values for 
per 100 million hours worked [Data from B-8]

4.  Results by function

In this section the safety performance within different 
functions performed in the E&P industry is presented. 
Functions are defined as ‘exploration’, ‘drilling’, 
‘production’, ‘construction’ and ‘unspecified’. The 
category ‘other’ is no longer in use. See the Glossary 
of Terms at Appendix E for definitions.

4.1	 Fatalities by function

The distribution of company and contractor fatal incidents 
and fatalities between the functions is shown for both 2013 
and 2012.

4.2	 Fatal accident rate (FAR) – 5-year rolling average

The five year rolling average is calculated by summing the total number of incidents of the five previous years, and 
dividing by the sum of the work hours for these years. For example, the five year rolling average for drilling FAR 
for 2013 is calculated by:

(Number of fatalities in drilling function 2009+2010+2011+2012+2013)
(Total work hours in drilling 2009+2010+2011+2012+2013)

The number series involved in the calculation is frame shifted along by one each year, e.g. 2011 will calculate from 
2007-2011, inclusive.

Unspecified
Unspecified is used for the entry of data associated 
with office personnel who’s work hours and 
incident data cannot be reasonably assigned to 
the administrative support of one of the function 
groupings of exploration, drilling, production or 
construction. Corporate overhead support function 
personnel such as finance or human resources staff 
may be examples where work hours cannot be 
specifically assigned to a particular function.

© OGP 4-13



International Association of Oil & Gas Producers

and gas industry. The database is 2% larger than it was in

•	 73% of the hours reported were associated with onshore Contractor 2 123 159 (56.3%) 826 531 (21.9%) 2 949 690

activities, 27% with offshore activities. Overall 2 760 436 1 010 110 3 770 546

•	 110 countries are represented in the database, 3 more 
than in the 2012 database. Countries are listed in 
Appendix D.  

•	 50 companies contributed data, of which 49 companies 
contributed contractor statistics, though not in every 
case for each country of operation. 

•	 Of the 50 companies, 47 had contributed data in 2012 
which accounted for 95% of the database in 2012 and 
96% of the database in 2013. 43 of the companies 
submitting 2013 data had also provided data in 2011.

•	 23 of the companies contributed 90% of the hours. 7 
companies between them covered 51% of the hours, 
and the largest contributor accounted for 12%. 

•	 22% of the reported work hours were related to company 
personnel and 78% were related to contractors.

A summary of the key elements of the database is shown in 
the table at the end of this section.

‘Unspecified (as a work function)’ is used for the entry of 
data associated with office personnel whose work hours 
and incident data cannot be reasonably assigned to the 
administrative support of one of the function groupings 
of exploration, drilling, production or construction. 
Corporate overhead support function personnel such as 
finance or human resources staff may be examples where 
work hours cannot be specifically assigned to a particular 
function. All other data that are not separated out by 
function are reported as ‘unspecified’.

Journal .I Occupational and Organiptional Ps_vchology (1998), 71, 289-304 Printed in Great Britain 289 
0 1998 The British Psychological Society Reference Copy 

only 
Organizational controls and safety: The 

varieties of rule-related behaviour 

James Reason, Dianne Parker* and Rebecca Lawton 
Department of Psychology, Universio o jMancbester, Mancbester M 13 9PI,, UK 

Companies operating in hazardous environments employ a variety of organiz- 
ational controls both to  manage the enterprise as a whole and to minimize their 
risks. This paper critically reviews the use of procedures, rules and regulations to 
restrict individual behaviour to those courses o f  action considered safc and 
productive. In particular, it discusses the psychological, situational and organiz- 
ational factors influencing compliant and non-compliant behaviour (i.e. violations 
and circumventions of safe operating procedures). Ten kinds of rule-related 
behaviour are identified. The paper concludes by arguing for the extended usc 
of  social and self-controls to compensate for the inevitable limitations o f  
prescriptive procedures in guiding safe behaviour. 

In  almost every organization, there is a tension bctween the natural variability of 
human behaviour and the needs of the system to ensure a high degree of 
predictability and regularity in the activities of its members. So fundamental is this 
conflict that it has been argued by many theorists that the manner of its resolution 
is the major influence in shaping the character of the organization as a whole (see 
Katz & Kahn, 1978). 

For those systems that operate under hazardous conditions, thcre is the 
additional requirement t o  limit human action to pathways that are not only efficient 
and productivc, but also safc. The most obvious difficulty in thc traditional pursuit 
o f  organizational safety is that success is counted b y  the absence of something- 
fatalitics, lost time injuries, damage to assets and other negative outcomes. Thc 
feedback necessary to  attain safety goals is thus discontinuous and indirect: 
discontinuous bccause recordcd accidents, incidents and near misses are compar- 
atively rare events; indirect because these data only rcflect moments o f  unsafety 
rather than the system’s intrinsic resistance to its operational hazards. Moreover, 
since thcre is a large chance component in accident causation, these nega- 
tive outcome data yield unrcliable information regarding the true state o f  an 
organization’s safety. 

This paper cxamincs the issue of organizational control in relation t o  safcty, 
focusing o n  the use of rules and proccdures as one means by which an organization 
seeks t o  produce predictability and regularity in the txhaviour of its members. This 
*Request\ f o r  reprints. 



290 ldn1e.r Reason et al. 
is an arca that has received relatively little attention in the literature, and the 
empirical basis is still weak. Problems with the use of prescriptive procedures are 
discussed and a classification o f  rule-rclated behaviour is proposed as a means of 
understanding the ways in which rule quality, the potential outcomes of behaviour 
and the motives of individuals intcract in relation to the rules. In conclusion, it is 
argued that simply providing and enforcing prescriptive rules and procedures is n o t  
suficient to foster safe behaviour in the workplace, and supplementary methods o f  
organizational control are discussed. 

Organizational controls 
I lopwood (1 974) identified three types of organizational controls: administrative, 
social and self-controls. To these, advances in technology require that we add 
a further control: technical controls that include engineered safety features. 
Administrative controls have becn defined as '. . . those mechanisms, techniques 
and proccsscs that have been consciously and purposefully designed in order t o  try 
t o  control the organisational behaviour(s) of  other individuals, groups and 
organisations' (Johnson 8( Gill, 1993, p. 21). Administrative controls may involve 
control o f  process o r  of output. Process controls are attempts to standardize the 
work process using prescriptive rules and procedures, often backed up by the 
monitoring of bchaviour and by sanctions for non-compliance. In contrast, output 
controls entail a feedback control process based upon comparing output measures, 
including performance, with organizational objectives, and adjusting outputs 
whenever nccessary. This type o f  control (e.g. job appraisal) is often necessary 
when tasks are complex and unpredictable. While process controls such as rules 
and procedures are the focus of this paper, the other types o f  control suggestcd by 
Hopwood will be examined in greater detail later. 

On the nature and proliferation of safety rules and procedures 
Employee surveys, interviews, group discussions and observations carried out in a 
variety of industries (oil production and exploration, rail transport, commercial 
aviation and medicine) havc shown the ubiquity o f  violations of organizational 
safety rules (c.g. Chapman, 1982; Cushing, 1994; Ilorning & Smith, 1991). 
Moreover, Free (1 994) showed that, in the railway industry at Icast, safety rules 
differ from othcr kinds of procedural guidance in at least two important respects. 
First, in the manner of their creation and development and, second, in the way 
that they are perceived by the workforce. Each o f  thcse aspccts is considered 
I,elow. 

Safe operating procedures are continually being amended to prohibit actions that 
havc been implicated in some recent accident or  incident. Over time, these 
additions become increasingly restrictive, often reducing the range of permitted 
actions to far less than those necessary t o  get the job done under anything but 
optimal conditions. However, the combination o f  latent conditions, local triggers 
and othcr active failures (Reason, 1990; Reason, Parker & Free, 1994) which leads 



O p n i ~ a t i o n oclontrols and sufijty 29 1 

to  an  accident rarely rccurs in precisely the same form. Thus the workforce quickly 
lcarns that thc violation (deviation from written rules o r  procedures) of individual 
rules in isolation generally carries n o  penalty. Furthermore, the separation in 
time and space of the individual violation from its consequent failures might 
sewe to reinforce a bclief in the innocuous nature o f  violations. Indeed, workcrs 
often discover that such violations lead to an easier and more efficient way of 
working. 

Ironically, then, one of the effects of continually tightening up safe working 
practices is to  increasc the likelihood o f  deliberate deviations from thcse practices: 
in other words, encourage violations. ’The scope of allowable action shrinks to such 
an cxtent that procedures are either violated routinely o r  on those occasions when 
operational nccessity demands it. Whereas crrors, being unintentional deviations 
from a planned course of action o r  in the case of mistakcs incorrect plans, arise 
from various kinds of informational underspecification (Reason, 1990), many 
violations are created by procedural overspecification. 

Ncverthelcss, although the commission of these isolated proscribed acts may be 
mostly inconscqucntial, the fact that they were implicated in some past cvent 
indicates that thcy cun increasc thc risks for thcir perpetrators, cven though they do  
not usually result in a bad outcome. 

One  of the morc obvious ways in which such violations incrcase risk is in thcir 
effccts upon thc subsequent likelihood of committing an error, often because thc 
perpetrator is less familiar with the physical regime. Driving a car at a 100 mph on  
the motorway is not in itself sufficient to cause an accident. However, since this 
specd will almost certainly be highcr than the driver’s norm, therc is a greater 
likelihood o f  misjudging the braking distance o r  the handling characteristics of the 
vehiclc. If a driver always travcls at 100 mph o n  the motorway, i.e. the violation is 
routine, increascd accidcnt risk may nevertheless arise not necessarily from higher 
error rates, but from the fact that the margins for tolerating an error are now 
considerably rcduced. By violating the safe speed limit the conscqucnces o f  a 
misjudgment are likely to be morc damaging than if the mistake had been 
committed whilst obeying the traffic laws. Thus, violations can havc two important 
consequcnccs: they can incrcase the probability of a subsequent error, and they can 
also incrcase the likelihood that it will have a bad outcomc (Free, 1994; Parkcr, 
Reason, Manstead & Stradling, 1995). 

In many hazardous technologies, the important issue is not whether to violate, but 
when to  violatc. Nearly all hazardous operations routinely involve thc commission 
of  actions that lie outside the prescribed boundaries, yet are regardcd as acceptable 
practice within the work-group. Most experienced workers think they know 
approximately whcre the ‘cdgc’ bctwecn safety and disastcr lics and d o  not excecd 
it, except in cxtrcme circumstances. 

Violations of safety rules gain considcrable significance for a company in the 
aftermath o f  a serious accidcnt. Faced with issues of lcgal liability, the organization 
may seeks ways of distancing thc actions of involvcd employees from the stated 
aims and practices of the company. This is readily done when thc implicated unsafe 
acts are clearly in violation of some safety rule or  procedure, allowing the company 
to  argue that the responsibility for the accident lay with the pcrson o r  people who 



292 James Reason et  al. 
committed these proximal violations. This approach may further compound the 
cynicism of workers who view safety procedures more as a means of protecting 
the company in the event of an accident than as a workable way of preserving the 
health and safety of those in direct contact with hazards (Free, 1994). 

Should safety rules permit the achievement of personal as well as organizational 
goals, there would be little need for external enforcement. However, there is often 
a conflict between individual and organizational goals (as embodied in rules and 
procedures) making enforcement necessary. For example, employees may cut safety 
corners in attempting to achieve a bonus, although the organization requires that 
safety be the first consideration. Alternatively an organization may wish to meet a 
deadline, but may only be able to do so with less than strict adherence to the safety 
rules. Furthermore, in some situations there may be a conflict between two 
simultaneously held organizational goals, only one of which is specified in the rules 
(e.g. a conflict between adhering to  safety rules and achieving productivity goals). 
Thus a meaningful evaluation of rule-related behaviours requires an assessment of 
the interaction between individual and organizational safety goals. 

The bases for a behavioural taxonomy 
In seeking to clarify the varieties of rule-related behaviours that occur in 
organizations, four distinctions may be identified: (u) between psychologically 
rewarding and unrewarding behaviour; (b) between violations and compliant 
behaviour; (c) between correct and incorrect actions; and (d ) between ‘good’ and 
‘bad’ rules. 

I+ychological(y rewurdzny and unrewurding behaviour 
Error has been defined as ‘the failure of planned actions to achieve their desired 
ends’ (Reason, 1990). There are three elements to this definition: a plan or intention 
that incorporates both the goal and the means to achieve it, a sequence o f  actions 
initiated by that plan, and the extent to which these actions are successful in 
achieving their purpose. I.ogically, actions may bc unsuccessful in achieving their 
purpose because the actions failed to go as planned, or  because the plan itself was 
inadequate to achieve the intended outcome. For our present purposes, i t  is 
sufficient t o  define psychologically rewarding behaviour as that which achieves the 
personal goals of the actor, which may or  may not be consistent with the goals of 
the organization. So,  for example, for one individual deviating from a rule may be 
psychologically rewarding as it gives rise to a scnse of excitement o r  thrill; for a 
fellow worker this samc deviation may produce psychologically unrewarding 
feelings of guilt and worry. 

Ll’iolalions and compliant behaviour 
Violations arc deviations from safe operating procedures, standards o r  rules. Such 
deviations can be either intentional o r  unintentional (i.e. speeding without being 



O~qani~ationucol  ntrols and safety 293 
aware o f  cither the speed o r  the local restriction). The latter arc caws in which 
ignorance of the procedure exists or  the individual is unaware that they are violating 
the procedure and, thereforc, for all intents and purposes thesc may bc defined as 
errors rather than violations. Three major categories o f  deliberate safety violation 
have been distinguished: routine, optimizing and situational violations (Reason, 
Parkcr & Free, 1994). In each casc, the decision not to  abide by safc operating 
procedures is shaped by both organizational and individual factors, though the 
balance of these influences varies from one type o f  violation to  anothcr (Free, 
1994). 

Routine violations typically involve corner-cutting-taking the path of least 
effort between two task-related points. Eventually, these short cuts can become an 
habitual part o f  the person’s bchavioural repertoire, particularly when the work 
environmcnt is one that rarely sanctions violations o r  rewards compliance. Such 
routine violations are also promoted by ‘clumsy’ procedures that take little account 
of behavioural economics (Rattmann & Klumb, 1993). 

Optimizing violations reflect the fact that human actions serve a variety o f  goals, 
and that some o f  these are quite unrelated to thc functional aspects of the task. 
Thus, a drivcr’s functional goal is to get from A to B, but in the process he or  she 
can optimize the j o y  of speed or  indulge aggressive instincts. ’Tcndencics to 
optimize non-functional goals can bccomc a part of an individual’s performance 
style. They are also characteristic of  particular dcmographic groups such as young 
malc drivers (Meadows, 1994). 

Whereas routine and optimizing violations are clearly linked to the attainment of 
personal goals, situational violations have their primary origins in particular work 
situations. Here, non-compliance is secn as essential in order to get the job done. 
These violations arc commonly provokcd by organizational failings with regard t o  
the site, tools or  cquipmcnt. In  addition, they can also providc an easier way of 
working. In this way these violations can become routinized. 

In terms o f  the taxonomy, compliant behaviour is behaviour that involves the 
following of formal rules and procedures, irrespective of whethcr o r  not the rule is 
a good one, o r  applicable in the circumstances. Compliance will not always achieve 
the organization’s safety goals. When the rule is bad, or  inappropriate, compliance 
is not desirablc. 

(,orreci umd incorrect uctiom 
The terms correct and incorrcct relate to how closely an individual’s appraisal of 
the local dangers approximates to  the reality. In  other words, the distinction relates 
to the accuracy of hazard perception. Recognizing a situation as hazardous and/or 
a rule as inappropriate is likely to lead to the adoption of self-protective behaviour. 
A correct action, therefore, is one taken on the basis of an adequate hazard 
evaluation. Psychologically rewarding actions need not necessarily be correct 
actions with rcgard to the accuracy of the hazard assessmcnt. Driving at 100 mph 
could achieve the personal goals of arriving sooner and of expcricncing the thrill of 
speed, but it is clcarly not correct given the increased risks associatcd with a 
relatively unknown physical regime and its reduced error margins. Similarly, 



294 James Reason et al. 

- 
Figure 1. Classifying the categories of rule-related behaviour. 

compliance is not automatically correct, nor a violation incorrect. When a safety 
procedure is appropriate, then the safety goals o f  an organization are assumed to be 
achieved when employees are correct in their choice of actions. 

Appropriate and inappropriate rules 
A fourth distinction relates to the appropriateness o f  the rules to  particular local 
circumstances. For any given situation, there may be good rules, bad rules or  no 
rules. A good rule is one that is appropriate to the circumstances, and a bad rule is 
one which is inappropriate. A 'no rule' situation is one for which no procedures are 
available. 

A 10-way classification of rule-related behaviour 
O n  the basis of these four distinctions, it is possible to describe the varieties of 
rulc-related behaviour in the form of a flow diagram. Figure 1 shows the 10 forms 
of rule-related behaviour which are possible given the four distinctions outlined 
above. Looking at the left-hand side of the diagram, the first two are concerned 
with situations where there are no  rules and where the individual must improvise in 
order to perform a task. In this situation, if the improvisation works out it is said 
to be correct. Alternatively, if the improvisation does not work out, the behaviour 
is defined as a mistake. In the case of the former, the behaviour is correct in terms 
of hazard appraisal and psychologically rewarding in terms of achieving personal 
goals. 

Where a rule or procedure is available, the right-hand path is followed. The  first 
question concerns the appropriateness of the rule for the situation. Having 



O~p’n i~a t ioncaoln trols und sufety 295 
determined whether the rule is appropriatc for the situation, the next decision point 
concerns whether compliance with or deviation from the rule occurs. Whether the 
ensuing bchaviour is classified as correct in terms of hazard appraisal is a function 
o f  both the appropriateness of the rule and whcther or  not i t  is followed. Whcre the 
rulc is inappropriate, compliance is assumed t o  be incorrect in terms of hazard 
appraisal and deviations are held to be correct. O n  the other hand, when a rule is 
appropriate for the situation, then compliance with the rule is correct and deviance 
incorrect. Finally, the question of personal goals is addressed. While for most 
individuals complying with an appropriate rule and getting the job done safely may 
accord with their need for non-threatening situations, for some, whose personal 
goals might bc thrill and excitement, this coursc of bchaviour would not bc 
psychologically rewarding. Thus, achieving pcrsonal goals is not necessarily 
associated with thc ‘correct’ form o f  behaviour o r  the behaviour favoured by the 
organization, 

liach o f  the 10 forms o f  rule-related behaviour highlighted in Fig. 1 is discussed 
in morc detail below. 

The 10 types of rule-related behaviour 
Those categories of rule-related behaviour that achieve the goals of both thc 
organization and thc individual will be considered first as thc aim of any 
organization should be to encourage these forms of bchaviour. Correct compliance 
(type 10) occurs when bchaviour is both correct (i.c. the actions were based upon 
an adequate hazard appraisal) and psychologically rcwarding (i.c. the actions 
fulfilled personal goals). In this case, performance conforms t o  appropriate safety 
rulcs (e.g. a train driver bringing the train to a stop at a rcd signal). Correct 
violations (typc 4) are correct and psychologically rewarding behaviour achieved by 
deliberately deviating from inappropriate rules o r  procedures (e.g. a surgeon who 
chooses not t o  put on  a mask for an operation wherc research evidence suggests 
there is minimal risk of cross-infection). Correct improvisations (type 2) involve a 
correct and psychologically rewarding course of action taken in the absence o f  
appropriatc procedures. 

The nest three categories o f  Ixhaviour to  be considered, misvcntions, 
rnispliances and mistakes, occur when neither the goals of thc organization nor 
thc individual are achieved. Misventions (type 7, a blcnd of ‘mistake’ and 
‘circumvcntion’) are behaviours that arc neither correct nor psychologically 
rcwarding and that involved a deviation from appropriate safety rules (c.g. a 
wclder using oxyacetylene welding equipment next to  a furnacc with an incrcased 
risk of cxplosion). Mispliances (type 5, a blend of ‘mistake’ and ‘compliance’) are 
bchaviours that are neither correct nor psychologically rewarding and that 
involve mistaken compliance with inappropriate or  inaccurdtc operating rules 
(c.g. a doctor who prescribes analgesics strictly according to the protocol, 
causing some patients unneccssary suffering, when in fact some flcxibility in 
dosage is permissible). Mistakes (type 1) occur when an unsuccessful outcome 
results from an incorrect plan o f  action carried out in the  absence of suitable 



296 /umes Reason et al. 
procedures. It should also be noted that Fig. 1 deliberately blurs the sharp 
distinctions hitherto maintained between errors and violations (Reason et ui., 
1994). This modification of our earlier theorizing acknowledges in part the 
validity of Dougherty’s (1 995) criticisms of the error-violation distinction. 
Although i t  is meaningful to make a distinction between errors and violations in 
terms of intentionality, there are some circumstances in which the individual 
intends to violate a rule, but does so on the basis of erroneous judgments of the 
likely consequences. Thus, while misventions, mispliances and mistakes all 
involve errors of judgment, the first of these, misvention, also involves 
intentional violation of the rules. Therefore it is clearly not possible to treat 
mistakes and violations as mutually exclusive. 

The four remaining types of behaviour occur when the goals of the 
organization and the individual are at odds. These are the incorrect but p!ycho- 
logicakj reuwding violution (type 8)  of good rules (i.e. routine and optimizing 
violations); the correct but ps_ychologicul~unrewarding compliance (type 9) with good 
rules; the correct but psycholoRical~yu nrewarding viobtion (type 3) of bad rules; and the 
incorrect but psychologiculb rewurdinx compliance (type 6) with bad rules. In the first 
case (type S), which we refer to previously in the discussion as routine or  
optimizing violations, personal goals are successfully achieved despite faulty 
hazard assessments. The individual succeeds in saving time or  reducing boredom 
by deliberately ignoring a rule designed to maximize safety (e.g. a machine 
operator wiring open a safety device such as the ‘dead man’s handle’, rather than 
holding it down for long periods of time). lncorrect but psychologically 
rewarding violations are the breed stock from which occasional misventions 
emerge. Though often inconsequential in themselves, they create the conditions 
that promote dangerous misventions, particularly overconfidence in personal 
skills and an underestimation of the hazards. 

Correct but psychologically unrewarding compliances (type 9) are unlikely to be 
long lived because one of the primary motives of individuals is to save time and 
minimize effort (Battman & Klumb, 1994; Schonpflug, 1986). An example o f  this 
sort o f  compliance might be when a steel erector wears an uncomfortable hard hat 
in hot weather. In cases such as this, where following the rules leads t o  boredom, 
requires greater effort, or  leads to psychological dissatisfaction, violating may 
become too tempting a prospect. 

The correct but psychologically unrewarding violations (type 3) of inappropriate 
rules occur when an individual recognizes that the accepted procedure is not 
appropriate for the current situation, and s o  elects not to follow it, but feels 
uncomfortable violating a company rulc. This person prefers the security of 
compliance and may become anxious when he or  she finds that a violation is 
necessary (e.g. an operator in the chemical industry who feels uncomfortable starting 
a centrifugal pump in the way informally approved o f  by his colleagues which, 
although successful, neverthcless deviates from the method stated in the procedure). 

Finally, incorrect but psychologically rewarding compliance (type 6) arises when 
the individual chooses the easy option and avoids stress by deciding to comply with 
a rule in spite o f  judging it to be inappropriate in the current situation (c.g. when 
it is ‘more than my job’s worth’ not to  comply).
