Factors influencing rule-related behaviours 
Figure 1 indicates that the fivc main factors shaping the occurrence of these ten 
kinds of rule-related behaviour are the availability of a procedure, the appropriate- 
ness of the procedure, whether o r  not it was followed, the correctncss of the 
chosen course of action and the outcome of the action in terms of thc achicvernent 
of personal goals. Each of these factors will be considered below. 

1. Wus u procedure available? The problem of requisite vuriety 

As detailed earlier, the generally additive way in which safety rules are accumulated 
and the associatcd conflicts that arise between their enforcement and getting the 
job done affect the utility of thc rules. In addition, there is the basic control 
problem of rcquisitc variety noted by Ashby (1956). This relates to the fact that in 
virtually all productive activities carried out in potentially hazardous circumstances, 
the variety of possible unsafe behaviours is very much grcater than that of the 
required productive behaviours. While it is feasible, though not necessarily 
desirable, to devise rules to cover all aspects of production, it is not feasible to  
cover all aspects of safe production. The variety of rules developed to govern safe 
behaviour will always be less than the possible variety of unsafe situations. I t  is 
impossible to  anticipate all combinations of dangers and their related accident 
scenarios. Wholly safe behaviour can never be controlled entirely by prescriptive 
controls. There will always be situations that are either not covered by the rules o r  
in which thc rules are locally inapplicable. In short, there will always be ‘bad rule’ 
situations and ‘no rule’ situations. 

This docs not imply that organizations should give up the attempt to  formulate 
safety rules. No t  only are such rules important for guiding safe behaviour in 
relation to  identified and understood hazards, they also constitute an important 
record o f  an organization’s lcarning about its opcrational dangers. Howevcr, thc 
rcyuisite variety problcm mcans that this compendium of safe opcrating procedures 
will nevcr be wholly comprehensive nor universally applicable. 

rhrlier, four kinds of organizational control were identified: administrative 
controls, technical controls, social controls and self-controls. It is now clear that 
administrative process controls are both a flawed and a restricted means of 
rcgulating safe behaviour-though, for many organizations, thcy rcmain thc 
instrument of choice. In order to achieve improvcd regulation of safe behaviour, it 
is necessary to consider the possibilities offcred by other kinds of organizational 
control. Such considerations are especially important when it comcs to identifying 
and controlling the factors that dctermine the correctness and success o f  
performance. 

2. Wu.c the procedure uppropriute for the situution? 
It is not uncommon for organizations to develop and formalize procedures with 
littlc considcration for thc practicalities o f  working with thcm in evcryday 



298 James Reason et al. 
situations. Organizations are often reluctant to introduce alternative methods o f  
control becausc they find the formal nature of rules reassuring-they allow the 
responsibility for safety to be delegated to  the people who d o  the job. Summarizing 
a number of studies examining managers’ and workers’ perceptions of their 
responsibilities in relation to safety, Glendon (1 987) notes that managers and 
supervisors feel that they are responsible for safety, while workers are responsible 
for accidcnt prevention. So, for example, while it is the rcsponsibility o f  managers 
to  provide rules and safety equipment, the role of the workers in accident 
prevention is to follow the rules and wear the safety equipment. Procedures may 
also be a popular form o f  organizational control becausc they can be written and 
issued and therefore closure seems possible, but with other forms of organizational 
control, e.g.  disciplinary systems, training, supervision, the process is ongoing. In 
summary, organizations like rules. 

A further problem leading to inappropriate procedures is that they are often 
written for the ideal situation, yet work situations are rarely ideal. ‘Time pressures, 
equipment failures o r  unavailability of equipment, poor planning, etc. mean that 
sometimes following the rulc becomes problematic or  simply impossible. When 
rules are inappropriate, alternative courscs of action tend to be used to  achieve the 
same ends. The employee in this situation needs to be aware of the organizational 
goals implicit in the rules, and to choose a course of action that meets these 
objectives. Thc skills and initiative required to solve problems is very different from 
the qualities required to follow the procedures methodically, and therefore an 
over-reliance on rules and procedures can cause problems for organizations whcn 
inappropriate rules surface or  unusual situations arise. As Perrow (1 967) has 
suggested, certain activities readily lend themselves t o  being proceduralized, while 
others d o  not. Tasks which are routine, repetitive and easy to perform lend 
themselves to rules, while tasks that are non-routine, poorly structured and 
unpredictable d o  not lend themselves to proceduralization as there are too many 
exceptional cases. 

3. Wus ~ k puro cpclurt. follonwd? 
If all rules were good and if what the organization rcquircd was blind obedience to  
the rules, compliance should be almost always guaranteed given strict discipline, 
supervision and training. Matters are rarely that simple. First, employees must know 
all of the rules and procedures o r  at least know of their cxistence so that they can 
refer t o  them when necessary. Where large numbers of rules have accumulated this 
may require initial training, refresher training, testing and retesting. The rules must 
be understood by all and therefore writing them requires careful consideration of 
the cnd-users. Thought must also be given t o  the way in which the information is 
t o  be presented, e.g. pictorially or  in the form o f  a checklist. 

Sccond, therc will always be a few individuals who enjoy doing the wrong thing 
or  taking risks and therefore givcn a set of perfect rulcs, compliance is not assured. 
In a laboratory experiment Zeitlin (1 994) explorcd the distinction betwecn faulty 
communication and risky decisions in the failure to follow safety instructions. He 
concludes that ‘if a worker places high value o n  his or  her time, convenicnce, 



Otpnixational controls and s a j t y  299 

self-imagc, status among peers, and other factors and if he or  she estimates thc 
probability of being injured by disregarding an instruction as sufficiently low, then 
it is likely that the instruction will be ignorcd despite its clarity of presentation’ 
(p. 179). Thus even good rules may bc violated, and not only by risk takers and 
sensation seekers. 

4 .  Was peforntunce correct? 
Whereas the factors affecting the effectiveness and scope of prescriptive rulcs are 
primarily systemic in origin, those relating to  correct and incorrect performance 
may also he influenced by the belicfs, attitudes and abilities of the individual and 
the work-group. 

Several factors are likely to promotc correct performance. ’Thcse include accurate 
hazard perception, appropriate task training and the ability to lcarn from experi- 
cncc. At the group levcl, many factors, including leadcrship, work overload, 
role conflict and pcrson-cnvironment fit, appropriate supervisory example and 
guidance, adequate safety briefings, planning for non-routine contingencies, effec- 
tive resource management, the sharing of an appropriate situational awareness, 
open and unambiguous communications and coordinated teamwork, play a role in 
achieving correct and safe outcomcs (see Harrell, 1990; Helmreich, Butler, Taggart 
C(c Wilhem, 1994; Sherry, 1991). 

Rules that combine safety with an appropriate economy of productive effort, a 
justified faith in the system on  the part of individuals, high morale and a personal 
satisfaction in performing tasks ‘the right way’ are likely to promote correct 
compliance. Correct violations, on  the other hand, will demand many of  the 
features identified by Westrum (1993) as characterizing generative organizations. In 
such contexts individuals arc trained and empowered to  respond to  local con- 
ditions, contexts, having what Westrum calls a ‘licence to think’. The system is open 
to ncw ideas, with information flowing freely and openly. Errors and incidents are 
widely rcported, analysed and acted upon. Responsibility is shared, as the system 
frecly admits and profits from its mistakes. The system rates substance higher than 
form, and can reconfigure itself to cope with new situations. This lcads to  a 
‘reform’ rather than a ‘repair’ culture. The key attribute of such organizations is 
requisite imagination. According to Westrum (1993), an organization with such 
imagination is ‘able to make use of information, observations or  ideas wherever 
they exist within thc system, without regard for thc location of the person o r  group 
having such information, observations or  ideas’ (p. 402). In large part, both correct 
violations and correct improvisations cmcrgc from an enabling organizational 
culture. As Wcstrum (1993) elegantly expressed it: ‘Culture is to an organisation 
what personality is t o  an individual, and every organisation dcvelops a charactcristic 
way of doing things’ (p. 401). 

Just as there are many common factors shaping correct performance, so there are 
several factors common to  the production of incorrect performance in the shape of 
misventions, mispliances and mistakes. The relative effects of various error 
producing factors have been mapped most precisely by Williams (1988), and may be 
reduced to seven broad categories: high workload, inadequate knowledge, ability o r  



300 James Reason et  al. 
experience, poor interface design, inadequate supervision or  instruction, stressful 
environment, mental state (fatigue, preoccupation, distraction, anxiety, etc.) and 
change. Departures from routine and changes in the circumstances in which actions 
are normally performed constitute a major factor in the making of absent-minded 
slips of action (Reason, 1990). 

Misventions arc the occasional by-product of what are usually psychologically 
rewarding hut incorrect violations of sensible safety rules. As indicated earlier, the 
mere act o f  violating good rules increases the probability of subsequent errors by 
taking people into regions where the physical characteristics of the task are often 
less well understood, and it is often these consequential errors rather than the 
violation itself that result in bad outcomes. 

Several recent studies have examined the personal and situational factors 
promoting violations, and summaries of this work can be found elsewhere (Free, 
1994; Reason e t  a/., 1994). There is also a large literature examining individual 
differences in accident liability which is certainly relevant (see Hansen, 1988, 1989 
for a review), but beyond the scope of the current discussion (which is concerned 
with an organizational rather than an individual level of explanation). Whereas error 
producing conditions adversely affect information processing, violation promoting 
factors exert their main influence on  attitudes, beliefs and group norms (Parker, 
Manstead, Stradling, Reason & Aaxter, 1992). Tendencies to violate are also 
markedly affected by age and gender: the young violate more than older people, and 
males of all ages violate more than females (Reason, Manstead, Stradling, Baxter eCr 
Campbell, 1990). N o  such patterns are found for error tendencies. In  addition, 
many operators see bending the rules as the hallmark of skill and experience (Free, 
1994). The same clearly does not apply to  errors. 

Mispliances are likely to  be associated with the personal characteristics o f  rigid 
compliance (‘rules must be obeyed at all times’), mature age and female gender, 
inexperience, low morale and fear of sanctions. Other factors are group norms that 
stress unthinking obedience and not ‘rocking the boat‘, and an authoritarian system 
that is intolerant of non-compliance under any circumstances. 

Finally, improvisations in the absence of applicable rules will be prey to all the 
many error producing factors associated with knowledge-based processing (Reason, 
1990). Since trial-and-error learning is one of the principal ways of coping with 
novel situations, i t  is to be expected that a large number of mistakes will occur 
under these conditions. Whether o r  not these improvisations result in a safe 
outcome is often a matter of good luck as well as sound judgment (Haynes, 
1992). 

5. Was the behaviour p!ychological!y rewardiq? 

What is deemed psychologically rewarding txhaviour for one individual may not be 
regarded as so by another. For some employees achieving maximum pay for 
minimum effort may be the ultimate goal of work. For others work is more of a 
social event and the norms of the group may be a strong influencing factor. For yet 
others, security and safety may be perceived as the reward for working methodically 
and blindly following the rules. Given the variety of factors that motivate 



Orgunixational controls and sajety 30 1 

individuals at work, it is likely that all the forms of rule-related behaviour will result 
in the achievement of personal goals for some individuals. The  organization can 
affect individual motivations by various means, thus encouraging those behaviours, 
e.g. correct compliance and correct violations, that assure the achievemcnt of the 
organizational objectives. The organization might foster group norms that accord 
with organizational goals. An organization can also influence individual motivations 
by rewarding desired behaviour. Providing a stimulating work environment that 
meets individual needs for interest and excitement at work may also prevent 
potcntially dangerous behaviours, such as incorrect but psychologically rewarding 
violations. In general, thereforc, organizations should strive to maximize the 
concordance of individual and organizational goals. 

Summary and remedial implications 
A number of  problems associated with the use of safety rules have been idcntified, 
including the additive way in which they accumulate leading to a range o f  
perrnissiblc actions that are often less than that neccssary to get the job done. 
Difficulty can also arise when rules are not enforced because they conflict with local 
production goals. It has been shown that prescriptive process controls will always 
lack the requisite variety neccssary to guarantee safe behaviour, even if the 
workforce were scrupulously compliant. Moreover, safcty rules and procedures can 
never be wholly comprehensive nor universally appropriate. Thcre will always be 
situations in which no rules are available o r  in which variations in thc local 
circumstances negate thc applicability of the available rules. 

In terms o f  remediation, the above analyses suggcst that the burden o f  directing 
safe hehaviour must be distributcd morc evenly over the available control 
mechanisms. ‘The way in which this might be achieved can be illustrated by a 
reconsideration o f  the issue o f  administrative controls. The distinction between 
process and output controls suggested by Johnson & Gill (1993) does not tell the 
whole story. These two types of control actually characterize the extremes of a 
continuum, with process control relying wholly upon direct guidance from 
centralized management (via rules and procedures), and output control from which 
thesc detailcd directives are effectively absent, at least at the local level. Output 
control-the continuous adjustment of outputs to match the system’s 
goals-depends primarily on two other modes of organizational control: social (or 
group) controls and sclf- (or individual) controls. It is within these areas that thc 
main remedial implications of the present analysis lie. 

The immcnse variety of potentially hazardous situations requircs that much o f  
the tine control of safe behaviour is delivered at thc level of the individual work- 
group. This doctrinc was applied successfully in a military context through the 
Gcrman systcm of Az$rqpysten/,  which allowed, indeed relied on, low-lcvel 
commanders to fulfil organizational goals, with o r  without specific orders. 
Translatcd from military to industrial safety terms, this means selecting and training 
supervisors to provide on-the-spot action control when safcty proccdures are either 
not applicable or  not availablc. Supervisors need to bc ‘sitewise’ both t o  the local 



302 jumes Reason et al. 
production demands and to  the range of obvious and less obvious hazards. Equally 
important is a personal authority derived both from the respect of the workforce 
and the support of management. The latter, in turn, demands that safety ranks high 
in the list of corporate goals. Top-level commitment to safety is the sine qua non of 
effective behavioural control. 

The adoption of this approach would be beneficial in bringing the goals of the 
supervisor into line with those of the organization, thus reducing the occurrence 
of behaviours in which the individual chooses a course of action that is psycho- 
logically rewarding in terms of his or  her own point o f  view, but does not 
accord with organizational objectives (e.g. types 3 and 9). It would be nai’ve to 
think that supervisors arc any less prone to deviate from rules than other 
workers. In  fact, given that production pressures may be most salient to this 
group, the tcmptation to violate safety rulcs in order t o  save time and effort may 
be evcn greater. 

Not  all hazardous activities are carried out in supervised groups. When 
individuals are relatively isolated, the onus shifts from social to self-controls. 
Crucial among these are techniques designed to enhance hazard awareness and 
risk perception. These are measures that seek to promote correct rather than 
merely psychologically rewarding behaviour. A number of hazard evaluation 
programmes are being developed o r  are already in place (see Glendon & 
McKenna, 1995). The importance of such programmes should be strongly 
endorsed, and their wider application encouraged. Whereas an understanding 
of the limitations of administrative process controls is a necessary step forward 
at the organizational level, improved hazard evaluation holds the key to 
safer-more ‘correct’-performance at the level of self-control. In  addition, 
technical controls may be used to eliminate the opportunity for workers to  
behave in ways which may be unsafe. 

However, as Wagenaar (1 992) has pointed out, risk appraisal training is of little 
value once the incorrect actions have become habitual. When this happens, people 
are not taking risks deliberately, they are running them in a largely automatic and 
thoughtless fashion. To be effective, such training must occur in the initial phase of 
employment and then be consolidated and extended by on-the-spot supervisory 
guidance. By the same token, it is mainly through local supervisory interventions 
that long-established patterns of incorrect behaviour can be modified. 

The idcas presented in this paper arc the result of a mixture of theory and 
field study observations. The usefulness of the suggested typology is currently 
being tested in a study of thc use of procedures in the health service, a context 
in which a population o f  highly trained professionals carry out routine jobs, as 
well as deal with the unexpected, in a demanding work environment. This study 
will provide the opportunity to assess the types of administrative controls 
employed, as well as the situations in which social, self- and technical controls 
are used to greater effect. 

The task o f  the human factors specialist is not to reduce violations of safe 
operating procedures willy nilly. Rather, it is to aid organizations to develop a 
customized portfolio of controls that is best suited to guiding safe and productive 
behaviour in their particular sphere of operations.

Passenger and pilot risk minimization in offshore helicopter transportation
Fubin Qian a,n, Irina Gribkovskaia a, Gilbert Laporte b, Øyvind Halskau sr. a

a Molde University College, Postboks 2110, N-6402 Molde, Norway
b Canada Research Chair in Distribution Management, HEC Montréal, 3000 chemin de la Côte-Sainte-Catherine, Montréal, Canada H3T 2A7
a r t i c l e i n f o

Article history:
Received 20 December 2010
Accepted 6 November 2011

This paper was processed by fatalities. A mathematical model is proposed and a tabu search heuristic is applied to this problem.
Associate Editor Yagiura.

Three routing policies are considered: a direct routing policy, a Hamiltonian routing policy, and a

Available online 22 November 2011

Pickup and delivery routing problem

Tabu search heuristic
esponding author. Tel.: þ47 97435197; fax:
ail addresses: qianfubin@hotmail.com (F. Qian
bkovskaia@himolde.no (I. Gribkovskaia),
aporte@cirrelt.ca (G. Laporte), oyvind.halskau

016/j.omega.2011.11.003
a b s t r a c t

In the offshore petroleum industry, employees are transported to and from the offshore installations by
helicopter, which represents a major risk. This paper analyzes how to improve transportation safety by

general routing policy. Extensive computational experiments are conducted on instances derived from
real data in order to assess and compare these policies under a travel time, a passenger risk and a
combined passenger and pilot risk objective. Several management insights can be derived from this
study. In particular, our results show that passenger transportation risk can be reduced by increasing
travel time at the expense of pilot risk. This can be achieved through a reduction of the average number
of passengers onboard by applying either a Hamiltonian or a general routing policy. Our methodology
can also be used to derive an equitable distribution of risk between passengers and pilots, considering
that pilots fly much more frequently than passengers.

& 2011 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
1. Introduction

Helicopter has been the main way of transporting personnel to
and from offshore installations for more than 60 years. Kaiser [18]
states that Humble Oil and Refining and Kerr-McGee began using
helicopters to transport workers to offshore facilities as early as
1948. Travel by helicopter is more comfortable and less harmful
in terms of travel sickness and tiredness as compared to travel by
ship. However, helicopter transportation is perceived by many
offshore workers to be an uncomfortable and risky part of their
work. They experience heaviness and weightlessness during take-
off and landing, heavy noise, strong vibrations, and even some-
times incidents or accidents. Vinnem et al. [32] claim that the
hazards associated with helicopter transportation of personnel
are among the main risks experienced by offshore employees.
Table 1 presents a comparison of average passenger fatality rate
per billion passenger-kilometers by transportation mode, in
which the offshore helicopter transport holds the highest risk
among all public transportation modes.

Helicopter accidents are frequently reported. For example, a fatal
accident took place on April 1, 2009 when a helicopter carrying
personnel from BP’s Miller platform crashed into the North Sea,
killing all 16 people onboard. European offshore helicopter data
þ47 71214100.
),

@himolde.no (Ø. Halskau sr.).

ll rights reserved.
indicate that there have been 23 fatal and major injury accidents in
the offshore oil industry from 1968 to 2000 [25]. A recent summary
report from the Helicopter Safety Study 3 (HSS-3), undertaken by
SINTEF Trondheim, shows that among 28 OGP1 offshore accidents
from 2000 to 2005, 22 occurred during the take-off or landing phase.

In helicopter transportation, a flight consists of a sequence of
connecting stages that starts from an onshore heliport, visits
some offshore installations to pick up or/and deliver some
passengers, and usually ends at the same heliport. Helicopter
routing can be viewed as a vehicle routing problem with pickups
and deliveries (VRPPD). In this problem the aim is to design a
set of minimum cost routes for several vehicles performing
pickups and deliveries at customer locations, subject to capacity
constraints and other side constraints [5,1].

Several studies on helicopter routing for passenger transporta-
tion have been published, with a focus on minimizing transporta-
tion cost in terms of travel distance or time [11,9,28–30]. Moreno
et al. [24] and Menezes et al. [22] seek to minimize the flight costs,
the number of flights, and the total number of offshore landings in
order to improve flight safety. The objective function in the
optimization model uses weights to balance these multiple goals.

Qian et al. [26] introduced a risk measure for passenger
transportation by helicopter and proposed a risk objective in
terms of the expected number of fatalities. They modeled the
problem as an integer linear program which was solved by CPLEX
1 The International Association of Oil & Gas Producers.



F. Qian et al. / Omega 40 (2012) 584–593 585
for small instances. The purpose of this paper is to derive insights
into passenger transportation safety by comparing the solutions
obtained from optimizing risk or cost objectives under different
helicopter routing policies. This study differs from traditional
operational research studies because of its focus on learning as
opposed to optimization [23] and on planning in a high risk
environment (see, e.g., [27]). Our focus is therefore on analysis,
as opposed to algorithmic design. Comparative analyses are
performed on 120 instances derived from real-life settings
through the application of a tabu search heuristic. We also carry
out experiments in relation to the equity aspect of risk distribu-
tion between passengers and pilots.

The remainder of this paper is organized as follows. In Section 2,
some routing policies and perspectives on reducing transportation
risk are presented. A mathematical model to minimize the risk,
together with a summary of the tabu search heuristic, are provided
in Section 3. Computational results based on instances derived from
a real-life setting are presented in Section 4, followed by conclusions
in Section 5.

2. Passenger transport risk and routing policies

Risk is defined as the combination of the probability of an
event (the equivalent term ‘accident frequency’ is used in the
safety context) and its consequences (The International Organiza-
tion for Standardization/the International Electrotechnical Com-
mission Guide (ISO/IEC) 73). Using this definition, risk is often
defined as an expected consequence; see Erkut and Verter [8] and
Zografos and Androutsopoulos [33] who used this definition in
the context of hazardous materials transportation.
Table 1
A comparison of average passenger fatality rates per billion passenger kilometers
by transportation mode 1995–2004 (from UK Offshore Public Transport Helicopter
Safety Record (1977–2006)) [31].

Transportation mode 1995–2004 average

Air 0.01
Rail 0.4
Car 2.8
Offshore helicopter 6.3

Fig. 1. Five possible solutions for the helicopter rout
The risk for passenger transportation is defined as the
expected number of fatalities for persons, which include both
passengers and pilots. As in Qian et al. [26], risk is decomposed
into take-off and landing risk (TLrisk) and cruise risk (Crisk):

Risk¼ TLriskþCrisk ¼ PTL  f TL  pTLþPFH  f C  pC , ð1Þ

where PTL is the total number of person take-offs and landings, f TL

is the probability of an accident during a combined take-off and
landing operation, defined as the mean number of take-off and
landing accidents per million pairs of take-offs and landings, pTL is
the probability of a fatal outcome for an individual person
involved in a take-off and landing accident; PFH is the total
person flight hours, f C is the probability of an accident during
one cruise hour, defined from statistics as the mean number of
cruise accidents per million flight hours, pC is the probability of a
fatal outcome for an individual involved in a cruise accident. The
PTL measure is a summation of persons exposed to pairs of take-
offs and landings over all flight stages. The PFH measure is a
summation over all flight stages of the number of persons on
board, multiplied by the flight hours of each flight stage. Hokstad
et al. [17] define the risk of offshore passenger transport as the
product of accident frequency and the average consequence of an
accident without classifying the risk into take-off and landing risk
and cruise risk in helicopter transportation.

We implement and compare three routing policies: a direct
routing policy (D), a Hamiltonian routing policy (H), and a general
routing policy (G). Under the direct routing policy, each installa-
tion is served directly from the heliport so that the number of
routes is equal to the number of installations. The term ‘Hamilto-
nian’ refers to the fact that each installation is visited exactly once
for the combined pickup and delivery within a tour. In a general
solution, each installation is allowed to be visited twice if
necessary, once for delivery and once for pickup, and these visits
to an installation may take place in two different flights.

Fig. 1 illustrates how risk for passengers and for pilots varies in
five solutions under the three routing policies for a problem with
two installations 1 and 2 to be served from heliport 0 by a helicopter
with two pilots and transportation capacity for 19 passengers
onboard. Suppose it takes 1 h to travel between the heliport
and each installation, and 0.9 h between the two installations.
ing problem with two installations to be served.



586 F. Qian et al. / Omega 40 (2012) 584–593

Table 2
The impact of routing policy on risk and travel time.

Routing policy Solution Pax Risk Pilot Risk Total risk Travel Time

D (a) 33.22 12.08 45.30 4.0
H (b) 47.46 8.89 56.35 2.9

(c) 50.31 8.89 59.20 2.9
G (d) 41.76 11.74 53.50 3.8

(e) 56.00 11.74 67.74 3.8
The numbers shown in parentheses are the pickup demand pi and
the delivery demand di, respectively. The numbers in the square is
the number of onboard passengers at each flight stage. The accident
frequencies f TL and f C are determined according to historical data as
in Qian et al. [26]. The frequency f TL of take-off and landing accidents
is 0.65 per million pairs of take-offs and landings. The frequency f C

of cruise accidents is 0.86 per million flight hours. The probabilities
pTL and pC are assumed to be one.

The risk distribution between passengers and pilots is presented in
Table 2 for five solutions. ‘Pax’ is used to represent passengers. For
simplicity, in this table and everywhere in this paper, all reported risk
values are the actual values multiplied by 106. In solution (a) resulting
from the direct routing policy, the passenger PTL is 10þ6þ4þ2¼22
and the passenger PFH is 101.0þ61.0þ41.0þ21.0¼22.0.
Using formula (1) for passengers, we compute the passenger risk as
33.22. The pilot PTL is 2þ2þ2þ2¼8 and the pilot PFH is
21.0þ21.0þ21.0þ21.0¼8.0, which results in pilot risk of
12.08. Solution (a) has a total risk of 45.30 and a travel time of 4.0 h.
The Hamiltonian solutions (b) and (c) have the same risk of 8.89 for
pilots, and the same travel time of 2.9 h. In these solutions the routes
are constructed in reverse directions; the difference in total risk show
that trip direction may affect the passenger risk. The risk for
passengers and the total risk are smaller in the general solution (d),
compared with the Hamiltonian solutions. The Hamiltonian solutions
have the lowest travel time, while the passenger risk and total risk
are relatively high in comparison with the solutions (a) and (d).
Solution (e) is dominated by solution (d).

Three players are involved in offshore helicopter transporta-
tion: the helicopter operator, the pilots, and the passengers. From
an operating cost point of view, the operator will choose the
Hamiltonian solution (b) or (c) because of low costs resulting
from the minimum travel time; pilots will also prefer these
solutions since the lowest risk for themselves. With solution (a),
the risk for passengers and the total risk are minimized. The
decision maker will choose this solution if safety is prioritized.
However, it may not be realistic to serve each installation directly
when fleet size is insufficient. In such cases, it may only be
possible to choose between Hamiltonian and general solutions.

Proposition 1 presents an interesting property of a general
solution when only passenger PTL is minimized.

Proposition 1. If only passenger PTL is minimized in a general
routing strategy, there exists an optimal solution in which each route
starts pickup operations after finishing all delivery operations.

Proof. Assume installations 1, . . . ,n are served in a route and each
has a delivery demand di and a pickup demand pi. The helicopter
flyinPg the route starts its first flight stage from the heliport with
all n

i ¼ 1 di passengers onboard. Assume the helicopter follows a
sequence ðv1, . . . ,vnÞ of installations to perform all deliveries, in
which vi denotes the installation visited on the i-th position on a
delivery route ðv1, . . . ,vnÞ. The PTL from these deliveries will be

Thus, there exists an optimal solution with respect to PTL visiting

If for a given route the sequence ðv1, . . . ,vnÞ with non-increas-

i

we obtain a Hamiltonian optimal solution by visiting each instal-
lation only once in this sequence. Proposition 1 provides a rule for
determining an optimal passenger PTL solution for a single flight,
but it does not apply to the passenger PFH minimization problem.
For example, assume two installations 1 and 2 are served from
heliport 0, installation 1 has one passenger to be delivered, and
installation 2 has two. Neither installation has a pickup demand.
The travel time between 0 and 1, 0 and 2, 1 and 2 are 0.2, 0.7 and
0.6 h, respectively. The solution with minimum PTL is (0, 2, 1, 0),
whereas the solution with minimum PFH is (0, 1, 2, 0).

Risk management problems arise in a number of transportation
settings beyond helicopter routing. Erkut and Verter [8] present an
overview and analysis of path selection for the transportation of
hazardous materials between origin and destination pairs under
several definitions of risk. They summarize the five risk models
suggested in the literature; these are expected consequence,
population exposure, incident probability, perceived risk, and con-
ditional risk. Hazardous materials distribution problems are also
defined as bi-objective vehicle routing problems in Zografos and
Androutsopoulos [33], in which the authors minimize transporta-
tion risk in terms of expected consequence and cost. Giannikos [12]
considers four objectives: the total operating cost, the total per-
ceived risk, the equitable risk distribution among population
centers, and the equitable risk distribution of disutility caused by
the operation of the treatment facilities, for locating treatment sites
and routing hazardous wastes.

Equity of risk distribution between passengers and pilots is an
important issue in this paper, given that pilots have to fly
helicopters significantly more frequently than offshore workers
as passengers. In our model, it is appropriate to handle equity
through weight adjustments in the objective function because both
passenger risk and pilot risk are measured on the same scale. For
further discussions on equity issues in more general settings, see
Mandell [20], Marsh and Schilling [21] and Eiselt and Laporte [7].
3. Solution methodology

This section presents the mathematical model we have devel-
oped, and the tabu search heuristic we have applied to solve the
helicopter routing problem.

3.1. Mathematical model

The mathematical formulation presented in this section is an
extension of the model proposed for helicopter routing problem
in Qian et al. [26]. The helicopter routing problem in Qian et al.
[26] is viewed as an application of Multi-Vehicle Hamiltonian



F. Qian et al. / Omega 40 (2012) 584–593 587
One-to-Many-to-One Pickup and Delivery Problem with Com-
bined Demands (1–M–1 9PD9 m), which is usually called VRPPD
(see [1] for a classification scheme and survey of pickup and
delivery problems). Indeed, each installation receives a delivery
originating at a common heliport and sends a pickup quantity to
the heliport, which makes it a case of this problem.

Gribkovskaia et al. [13] have introduced the concept of general
solution for the VRPPD which encompasses known solution
shapes such as Hamiltonian, double-path and lasso. In a general
solution, each customer can be visited once or twice. If a customer
is visited once, a pickup and a delivery are performed simulta-
neously. If a customer is visited twice, the two operations are
performed separately. Gribkovskaia et al. [13,14] show that a
general routing strategy may result in cost savings for the single
vehicle routing problems with pickups and deliveries. Hoff et al.
[16] have implemented a general routing strategy to the VRPPD
and have shown that such solutions outperform other solution
shapes in terms of cost.

The helicopter routing problem can be defined on a directed
graph G¼ ðV ,AÞ, where V ¼ f0, . . . ,ng is the node set, A¼ fði,jÞ :
i,jAV ,ia jg is the arc set, and T ¼ ðtijÞnþ1,nþ1 is a travel time
matrix defined on A. Node 0 corresponds to the depot (the
onshore heliport), and the remaining nodes correspond to custo-
mers (the offshore installations). The offshore installations are
served by a homogeneous fleet composed of m helicopters, each
having the same capacity Q, where m is a decision variable. Each
installation i has a number pi of workers to be picked up and
brought back to the onshore heliport, and a number di of workers
to be delivered to that installation from the onshore heliport. We
denote by g the number of pilots who fly the helicopter.

To obtain a general solution, the original graph is extended by
duplicating each node. We associate two nodes i and iþn to each
installation i. Node i has a delivery demand di and no pickup,
while iþn has no delivery but a pickup demand pi. If nodes i and
iþn are visited in succession in a route, the delivery and pickup
operations are effectively performed by one visit to installation i.
Otherwise, the two operations are performed separately by the
same vehicle or by two different vehicles [16]. We define the
exten8ded travel time matrix T ¼ ðt ijÞð2nþ1Þð2nþ1Þ, where

>>< tij if irn and jrn,
tin,j if iZnþ1 and jrn,

t ij ¼>>: ti,jn if irn and jZnþ1,
tin,jn if iZnþ1 and jZnþ1:

W(e also define the following variables:

1 if a helicopter travels directly from i to j ði,j¼ 0, . . . ,2n; ia jÞ,
xij ¼ 0 otherwise,

lij¼the number of passengers on a helicopter traveling from i

vij ¼ the delivery load on a helicopter from i to j ði,j¼ 0, . . . ,2n; ia jÞ:

The mathem0atical model is then stated a1s follows:

In this formulation, the objective function consists of three
parts. The first two parts are the passenger risk (4a) and the pilot
risk (4b), respectPively. The passenger risk is a wPeighted sum of
passenger PTL¼ i,j ¼ 0,...,2n, lij and passenger PFPH¼ i,j ¼ 0,...,2n,tijlij. The

i a j 7 n i a j 7 n

pilot rPisk is a weighted sum of pilot PTL¼ i,j ¼ 0,...,2n,gxij and pilot
i a j 7 n

PFH¼ i,j ¼ 0,...,2n,gtijxij. Note that gxij is the number of pilots in a
i a j 7 n

helicopter traveling from iPto j. The third part (4c) is a weighted
total travel time equal to i,j ¼ 0,...,2n,tijxij as in the classical Vehicle

i a j 7 n

Routing Problem (VRP). The three non-negative parameters A1, A2

and A3 control the relative importance of the three components of
the objective function.

Constraints (5) ensure that the number of routes lies in
between a lower bound m and an upper bound m, where m is
the minimum number of routes required to meet all the pickup
and delivery demands and mrn. Constraints (6) and (7) mean
that at each node of the extended graph a single service (pickup or
delivery) is performed. Constraints (8) balance the outflow and
inflow of the pickup load for each node. These constraints also
eliminate subtours. Constraints (9) stipulate that each helicopter
has no initial pickup load onboard when leaving the depot.
Constraints (10) define the lower bounds for the pickup flow on
the arcs. Corresponding to constraints (8)–(10), constraints
(11)–(13) are used to control the delivery loads. Constraints (14)
state that the onboard load of a helicopter equals the pickup load
plus the delivery load; the load fluctuation will depend on the
sequences of the visits. Constraints (15) ensure that the capacity
constraint will not be violated along a route. Constraints (16)
impose binary and non-negativity restrictions on the variables.

In this formulation, a Hamiltonian solution can be obtained by
imposing the following constraints which force pickup and
delivery operations to be performed simultaneously:

xi,iþn ¼ 1, i¼ 1, . . . ,n: ð17Þ



588 F. Qian et al. / Omega 40 (2012) 584–593
It is straightforward to include in this model upper bounds on
the flying time and on the number of take-offs and landings of a
helicopter. We did not consider such constraints in our analysis
because they would have led to the introduction of additional
parameters. Recall that our aim is to study the trade-offs between
cost and risk under the same operating rules which should be
kept as simple as possible.

3.2. Unified tabu search algorithm

The model just described cannot be solved exactly for large
values of n. We therefore apply a heuristic for its solution. This
section presents a summary of the unified tabu search algorithm
(UTSA) adapted to the helicopter routing problem with an
objective made up of safety and cost components.

The UTSA was developed by Cordeau et al. [2] for three
variants of the VRP: the Periodic VRP, the Periodic Traveling
Salesman Problem and the Multi-Depot VRP. It was later applied
to the VRP with time windows, the Periodic VRP with time
windows and the Multi-Depot VRP with time windows [3], and
their extensions to problems with route duration constraints [4].
The UTSA was recently adapted to solve the single vehicle routing
problem with pickups and deliveries [13] and the single vehicle
pickup and delivery problem with capacitated customers [14].

Starting from an initial feasible or infeasible solution, the UTSA
works with a penalized objective function f ðsÞ ¼ cðsÞþaqðsÞ, where
c(s) is defined by (4a), (4a) and (4b), or (4c), q(s) is the total
capacity violation of solution s, and a is a positive parameter
which is updated at each iteration: initially set at 1, it is divided
by 1þd if the current solution is feasible or multiplied by 1þd is
the current solution is infeasible.

To each solution s is associated an attribute set BðsÞ ¼ fði,kÞ :
customer i is visited by vehicle k}. The neighborhood N(s) of
solution s is defined by all solutions that can be reached from s by
removing customer i from route k and inserting it into another
route k0. A transition from solution s to a new solution s0ANðsÞ is
called a move, which can also be interpreted by the removal
attribute (i,k) from the set B(s) and the inclusion of attribute ði,k0Þ
in the set Bðs0Þ, ðkak0Þ.

Continuous diversification is achieved by penalizing the attri-
butes most frequently added to the solution when evaluating a
move. Let MðsÞDNðsÞ be an admissible subset of neighbor solu-
tions which can be reached by a non-tabu move from s, or which
are feasible and meet the aspiration criterion. Let rik0 be the
frequency at which attribute ði,k0Þ has been present in the
solution. Any solution s0AMðsÞ such that f ðs0ÞZ f ðsÞ is penalized

pffiffiffiffiffiffiffi
by a term pðs0Þ ¼ g nmcðsÞrik0=l, where g is used to control the
intensity of diversification and l is the iteration counter. The
selection of the best solution s0AMðsÞ is based on the penalized
objective f ðs0Þþpðs0Þ.

Every time a new best solution is encountered, an intra-route
re-optimization is performed in an attempt to identify a better
solution nearby by altering the sequence of vertices in each route.
The algorithm terminates when a fixed number Z iterations is
reached, where Z is a user-controlled parameter.
4. Comparative analyses

We have performed several computational experiments in
order to compare the three routing policies under various objec-
tives. Our purpose by doing so is to determine how the choice of a
specific routing policy and of an objective function affects
passenger and pilot risk. We also provide insights on equity
issues relative to passenger and pilot risk. More specifically, we
compare the direct, Hamiltonian and general routing policies.
We also compare three objectives: passenger risk ðA1 ¼ 1, A2 ¼ A3

¼ 0Þ, total risk ðA1 ¼ A2 ¼ 1, A3 ¼ 0Þ, and travel time ðA1 ¼ A2 ¼

0, A3 ¼ 1Þ. The direct routing policy yields the same solutions
under the three objectives and is only applied for m¼n. This is
why we use these solutions as bounds for the solutions under
Hamiltonian and general routing policies. Section 4.1 describes
two sets of test instances. Sections 4.2 and 4.3 deal with the
Hamiltonian and the general routing policies, respectively, and
focus on passenger risk analysis. Section 4.4 provides a compar-
ison of passenger and pilot risk in both flight stages under the
three routing policies. We study trade-offs between the mini-
mization of passenger risk and the minimization of pilot risk in
Section 4.5.

The experiments were performed by applying the UTSA
heuristic. The algorithm was coded in C using Pelles for Windows,
version 5.00.8, and run on a personal computer with an
inter(R) Core(TM)2 Duo CPU E8400 @ 3.00 GHz, 2.99 GHz, and
3.25 GB of RAM, with the operating system Microsoft Windows
XP Professional Version 2002, Service Pack 3.

The UTSA works with four user-controlled parameters, i.e. d, g ,
y, Z. The two parameters d and g are randomly selected in the
range (0,1). The parameter y is an integer randomly selected in
pffiffiffiffiffiffiffi

½1, mn. The randomly selected values of these parameters are
updated at each iteration, as in Cordeau et al. [3]. The algorithm
was executed five times with Z¼ 105 for each instance, and the
best results out of the five runs are reported.

The UTSA heuristic was tested on two sets of randomly
generated instances to examine its effectiveness. We have created
10 instances with n¼10 and 10 instances with n¼15 by randomly
selecting a depot and customers from a 100100 square with
integer coordinates. Euclidean distances were used to calculate
travel times. The optimal solutions were obtained by CPLEX 9.0. In
each of the five runs the UTSA heuristic for all instances yields
optimal Hamiltonian solutions for the three objectives. The UTSA
generates in each run optimal general solutions with the passen-
ger risk objective for all instances with n¼10. Among these
instances, the UTSA heuristic identifies optimal general solutions
with the total risk objective for seven instances, and the average
gap of the best solutions of the five runs (measured by (best
heuristic solution – optimal solution)/optimal solution) is 0.02%.
These results indicate that the proposed heuristic is capable of
generating high quality solutions and its performance is stable
over the runs. CPLEX fails to give optimal general solutions for the
instances with n¼15. For these instances, we obtain the lower
bounds by solving the linear relaxation of the mathematical
formulation. However, these results are very uninformative since
the average gap between heuristic solutions and the correspond-
ing lower bounds is nearly 20%.

4.1. Test instances

We have generated two sets of instances based on geographi-
cal data associated with the installations at two offshore opera-
tion regions in the North Sea. These two sets of instances are
referred to as the Flesland instance set and the Sola instance set.
Each Flesland instance consists of 34 installations served from
Flesland heliport, Bergen. Each Sola instance is based on a
network of 37 installations served from Sola heliport, Stavanger.
In the Sola instances, the installations are located much further
from the heliport than in the Flesland instances. The locations of
the installations and of the heliports are represented in latitude
and longitude coordinates, which are used to determine the flying
time in hours based on an average speed of 250 km/h.

The helicopter fleet is assumed to be homogeneous. Each
helicopter can accommodate 19 passengers and is operated by
two pilots. The delivery amounts di of the installations are



F. Qian et al. / Omega 40 (2012) 584–593 589

uniformly distributed integers on the interval [a, b]. As in Dethloff
[6], the pickup amount pi is given by the formula pi ¼ ð0:5þxÞdi,
where x is uniformly distributed on the interval [0, 1], and further
rounded up or down to the nearest integer. If the pickup amount
pi happens to be greater than or equal to the helicopter capacity
19, it is reset to pi19. By varying the interval [a, b], we have
generated three groups of instances within each instance set.
Within the Flesland set, we have created the F5, F10, and F15
instance groups with [a, b]¼[1, 5], [1, 10], and [1, 15], respec-
tively. Similarly we have created instance groups S5, S10, and S15
within the Sola set. With 20 instances in each group within two
sets, we have generated a total of 120 instances. Regarding the
number of routes, we have observed that when m is not fixed a
priori, most solutions tend to be made up of direct trips to every
installation under a risk minimization objective and are Hamilto-
nian under a cost minimization objective. When m is free, it is
also difficult to calibrate the vehicle fixed costs. To avoid this
complication and to ensure fair comparisons between the various
routing policies, we have therefore used the minimum number of
routes required in each instance, as determined by the demands.
The accident frequencies f TL and f C , together with the probabilities
pTL and pC , are given in Section 2.

4.2. Comparative analysis for the Hamiltonian routing policy

Computational results for Hamiltonian solutions are summar-
ized in Tables 3 and 4. Statistics for each group of instances are
reported for the three objective functions, i.e. the travel time (line
Travel Time), the risk for passengers (line Pax Risk), and the total
risk including passenger risk and pilot risk (line Total Risk). These
are values for the components and the elements of the objective
function (4), and include the average number of passengers
onboard during a flight stage (column Pax onboard), the passenger
PTL (column Pax PTL), the passenger PFH (column Pax PFH), the
risk for passengers (column Pax risk), the risk for pilots (column
Pilot risk), and the average flying time per flight (column Flying
hours). Table 3 provides these values computed for each objective
function, while Table 4 presents the percent deviations of the
values obtained with the Pax Risk and the Total Risk objectives
from the values obtained with the Travel Time objective.

Overall, the same conclusions apply to all groups of instances.
As can be seen from Table 3, the most important part of the total
risk is the passenger risk, most of which is the take-off and
landing risk, proportional to the passenger PTL value. The first

observation is the result of the large average number of passen-
gers onboard. The second reflects the fact that passenger PTL in a
Hamiltonian solution is the product of Pax onboard and total
number of take-offs and landings (equal to nþm), while passen-
ger cruise risk is proportional to passenger PFH, approximately
equal to the product of Pax onboard and Flying hours.

It is easy to see that for all objective functions the average
flying time and risks increase with demands, and are larger for the
Sola instances, in which installations are located much further
from the heliport. When minimizing risk, as opposed to travel
time, solutions tend to have fewer passengers onboard and longer
flying times, resulting in lower risk for passengers and slightly
higher risk for pilots. Flying times and pilot risk are only slightly
larger if passenger risk is minimized as opposed to total risk. We
can conclude that minimizing passenger risk benefits passengers
at the expense of pilots and flying time.

Table 4 illustrates that under risk minimization objectives the
average number of passengers onboard decreases while the
average flying time increases with respect to the values observed
under a travel time minimization objective. The consequences of
an accident are proportional to the average number of passengers
onboard who could be potentially involved in an accident.
The total risk is reduced because of the much smaller conse-
quences of take-off and landing accidents, but this effect is
somewhat offset by the increase in the cruise accident probability
due to the longer flying times. Table 4 shows that the decrease in
passenger risk is more significant than the increase in pilot risk. It
also shows that the increase in flying times and pilot risk is larger
for instances with small demands. Solutions obtained under the
Total Risk minimization objective yield slightly lower increase in
flying times and pilot risk, compared with solutions in which only
the Pax Risk is minimized.

In these computational results, we have also observed a new
feature of Hamiltonian solutions under risk minimization objec-
tives. Fig. 2 depicts three Hamiltonian solutions for a small
instance, where the flights representing direct services to the
installations are not drawn for the sake of legibility. An interest-
ing observation is that the Travel Time solution has one flight
with three visited installations (TRS, ASC and HEI), while neither
of the other two solutions has a flight with more than two visited
installations. This means that the solutions with risk minimiza-
tion objectives have less variability in terms of number of
installations visited during the same flight. The standard devia-
tion was used to measure the variability of the number of
installations visited in a flight in Hamiltonian solutions for the
two tested sets of instances. Over the Flesland instances, the
average standard deviation of the number of installations visited
in a flight is 0.986 for Travel Time solutions and 0.905 for Pax Risk

4.3. Comparative analysis for the general routing policy

The general routing policy was implemented under the objec-
tives of minimizing Pax Risk and Total Risk, in order to assess the
impact on transportation risk of allowing a second visit to the
installations. Computational results are presented in Table 5.
The average deviations of the reported statistics with respect to
those obtained under the Travel Time objective (which are the
same as for the Hamiltonian routing policy) are shown in Table 6.

The observations made in Section 4.2 for the Hamiltonian routing
policy also hold for the general routing policy, except that the
variations in values are now more important. As can be seen from
Table 5, compared to Table 3, under the general routing policy the
average number of passengers onboard is much less, while flying
times are longer. This results in a much lower risk for passengers
and in a significantly higher risk for pilots, while the total risk is
reduced compared with the Hamiltonian routing policy.

The reduction in the average number of passengers onboard
compared with Travel Time solutions (illustrated in Table 6) is so
remarkable that both passenger PTL and passenger PFH decrease,
leading to a considerable decrease in passenger risk. On the other
hand, the important increase in flying times leads to a substantial
Fig. 2. Travel Time solution (top left), Pax Risk solution (top right), and Total Risk so
increase in pilot risk. Since passenger risk dominates pilot risk,
the overall risk decreases although pilot risk increases more than
passenger risk decreases in percentage.

In Pax Risk solutions, all installations are visited twice, except
those at which the helicopters perform their last delivery and
their first pickup. This yields two pairs of take-offs and landings at
each installation, and longer travel times. This does not very
much affect passenger risk (as the number of passengers onboard
is low), but considerably increases pilot risk. Under this policy,
passenger risk is reduced considerably at the high expense of pilot
lution (bottom) under the Hamiltonian routing policy on an illustrative instance.



F. Qian et al. / Omega 40 (2012) 584–593 591
risk and flying time. In Total Risk solutions, pickups and deliveries
are often performed during the same visit, resulting in fewer take-
offs and landings and in shorter travel times. The Total Risk
solutions have more passengers onboard and a slightly higher
passenger risk, but pilot risk is to a large extent smaller than in
Pax Risk solutions. Thus, under the general routing policy the
minimization of Total Risk reduces pilot risk remarkably without
deteriorating passenger risk significantly compared with the
minimization of Pax Risk.

4.4. Comparative analysis of three routing policies

The average values of the passenger and pilot risks are
presented in Table 7 for the two flight phases and for the three
routing policies.

Computational results with variable equity factor p.

F10 1 486.42 153.23 639.65 39

As was shown by Qian et al. [26] for the uncapacitated problem,
the direct routing policy provides lower bounds for passenger risk,
both in the take-off and landing phase and in the cruise phase, and
is best for passengers. However, it provides upper bounds for pilot
risk in both flight phases for Hamiltonian solutions. In turn, Travel
Time solutions under the Hamiltonian routing policy yield lower
bounds for pilot risk and upper bounds for passenger risk, and are
best for pilots.

Under both Hamiltonian and general routing policies, mini-
mizing passenger risk is best for passengers and worst for pilots,
while minimizing total risk is slightly worse for passengers
and better for pilots, particularly for the general routing policy.
Take-off and landing risk dominates cruise risk for passengers and
pilots under the Hamiltonian and general routing policies. The
difference between take-off and landing risk and cruise risk for
passengers is larger under the Hamiltonian policy than under the
general policy. The opposite is true for pilots. Hamiltonian
solutions yield a lower pilot risk than general solutions do. In
contrast, general solutions obtained with the Pax Risk objective
are the best for passengers at the expense of higher pilot risk.
However, when the total risk is minimized, the general policy
provides the next best passenger risk values and a remarkably
reduced pilot risk, thus yielding the lowest total risk values and a
fairer distribution of risk between passengers and pilots.

All risks are higher for the Sola instances, with a more important
contribution of the cruise risk. This can be explained by the longer
travel distances from the heliport to the installations.

4.5. Equitable distribution of risk between passengers and pilots

The results presented in the previous sections are based on the
computation of a single solution, which neglects the time dimen-
sion of the problem with respect to the risk distribution between
passengers and pilots. In the Norwegian offshore petroleum
Pilots

k Total TLrisk Crisk Total

.82 460.62 88.40 79.91 168.31

.66 735.26 58.96 29.16 88.12

.57 701.82 58.96 30.49 89.45

.62 632.66 101.79 42.39 144.18

.32 639.78 81.49 35.48 116.97

.59 610.37 96.20 125.42 221.62

.57 908.63 64.33 45.70 110.03

.29 48.50 13.35 14.50

.93 46.10 15.42 12.10

.78 46.00 15.61 12.00

.88 71.80 6.59 29.60

.69 52.40 14.28 15.30

.04 51.60 14.82 14.60

.65 51.00 15.47 13.90



592 F. Qian et al. / Omega 40 (2012) 584–593
industry, offshore workers usually start their shift by taking a
helicopter to an installation and return to their base two weeks
later. They will have a four-week holiday before starting a new
shift. This means that an offshore worker will take two helicopter
trips in six weeks. In contrast, the pilots may fly an average of
3.288 h per day (1200 h in any 365 consecutive days2). In six
weeks, a pilot may fly up to 138 h, while a passenger only flies
between 1 and 4 h in two trips. To achieve an equitable distribu-
tion of risk between passengers and pilots, we introduce a
multiplier p ranging from 1 to 150 and acting as an equity factor
for the pilot risk (4b) in the model, i.e. A2 ¼ pA1. We also let the
algorithm to determine the number of flights for each instance,
instead of using the minimum number of flights as in the previous
sections because we did not want the analysis to be constrained
by the operating cost. The upper bound m is set equal to the
number of installations in each instance. The computational
results for these experiments are reported in Table 8 for 10 of
F10 and 10 of S10 instances under a general routing policy.

In the solutions without penalty ðp¼ 1Þ, most installations are
served directly as can be seen from the ‘Number of flights’ column
for both F10 and S10 instances. In solutions with penalty (p¼ 50,
100, and 150), Hamiltonian routes are constructed to simulta-
neously perform a pickup and a delivery at each installation,
except for one installation which is served twice by two flights in
one S10 instance. The number of installations served in each flight
is larger on average in the solutions with penalty, since the
number of flights is reduced by about half, as compared with
the solutions without penalty.

In both the F10 and the S10 instances, pilot risk is reduced
radically when p¼ 50. The reduction of pilot risk is due to the
lower number of pairs of take-offs and landings (column Number
of TLs) and to shorter travel time (column Travel time). In
addition, the average number of passengers onboard (column
Pax onboard) is doubled, which results in a massive increase in
passenger risk (column Pax risk).

With penalty value over 50, pilot risk decreases only slightly,
but the increase in passenger risk leads to a total risk increase. No
notable changes in risks and other solution characteristics were
observed when the penalty value exceeded 100.
5. Conclusions

We have considered a helicopter routing problem arising in
the transportation of employees to offshore installations from an
onshore heliport. The problem was modeled with the objective of
risk minimization for passengers and pilots, expressed as the
expected number of fatalities during take-off and landing and
cruise phases of a flight. With passenger risk and total risk
minimization objectives, three routing policies were implemen-
ted using tabu search, and compared on real-based data instances.

Results of comparative analyses show that the direct routing
policy is optimal for passengers, while the Hamiltonian routing
policy is best for pilots. The most important part of the total risk is
the risk experienced by passengers during the take-off and land-
ing phase. Transportation risk can be reduced at the expense of
pilot risk and extra travel time by reducing the number of
passengers onboard. The lowest total risk for the capacitated
helicopter routing problem can be achieved under a general
routing policy in which a second visit to the installations is
allowed. A penalty mechanism was applied to achieve an equi-
table distribution of risks between passengers and pilots.
2 http://www.pprune.org/rotorheads/401851-flight-time-duty-time-limita
tions.html. Last accessed November 17, 2010.

 v 
 



 

Resumo da Dissertação apresentada à COPPE/UFRJ como parte dos requisitos 
necessários para a obtenção do grau de Mestre em Ciências (M.Sc.) 

 

PLANEJAMENTO DE REDE LOGÍSTICA DE TRANSPORTE AÉREO DE 

PESSOAS PARA ATIVIDADES DE EXPLORAÇÃO E PRODUÇÃO DE 

PETRÓLEO EM BACIA MARÍTIMA UTILIZANDO PROGRAMAÇÃO INTEIRA 

MISTA 

 

Nathália da Silva Sena 

Julho/2011 

Orientador(es): Virgílio José Martins Ferreira Filho  

Laura Silvia Bahiense da Silva Leite 

                          

 

Programa: Engenharia de Produção 

 
As descobertas de petróleo dos últimos anos, principalmente na camada do pré-

sal da Bacia de Santos, apontam para um grande crescimento da exploração e produção 
de petróleo no Brasil. Conseqüentemente, haverá aumento da demanda por 
movimentação de passageiros para as sondas de perfuração e plataformas de produção. 
Este transporte se tornará também mais complexo devido ao aumento da distância 
média entre os campos e a costa brasileira.  

O helicóptero é o modal mais utilizado para este fim.  Este é o meio de 
transporte de maior eficiência devido à rapidez e segurança, porém apresenta custos 
elevados. Logo, a otimização da rede logística de transporte de passageiros para 
trabalho em atividade offshore torna-se um tema economicamente relevante. 

O objetivo desta dissertação é o aprimoramento do modelo de otimização 
utilizado atualmente para planejamento de rede logística de passageiros da Petrobras. 
Este modelo provê aos gestores informações mais precisas para auxiliá-los nas tomadas 
de decisão com relação ao planejamento da infraestrutura logística. Decisões estas que 
envolvem dispêndios consideráveis e que uma vez tomadas não são facilmente 
alteradas. 

O modelo aperfeiçoado proposto apresenta menor nível de incertezas nos 
resultados gerados de localização de aeródromos, distribuição da demanda e frota. Os 
cenários rodados no estudo de caso apresentaram tempo de processamento aceitável, 
mesmo tendo um aumento considerável de complexidade. As revisões no modelo 
possibilitaram utilizá-lo também para a definição do perfil ótimo de frota, gerando 
oportunidades de redução de custos para a empresa.   

 vi 
 



 

Abstract of Dissertation presented to COPPE/UFRJ as a partial fulfillment of the 
requirements for the degree of Master of Science (M.Sc.) 
 

LOGISTIC NETWORK PLANNING FOR PEOPLE AIR TRANSPORT TO WORK 

IN PETROLEUM PRODUCTION AND EXPLOTATION USING MIXED INTEGER 

PROGRAMMING 

 

Nathália da Silva Sena 

July/2011 

Advisors: Virgílio José Martins Ferreira Filho  

Laura Silvia Bahiense da Silva Leite 

                  

 

Department: Industrial Engineering 
 

The oil discoveries of recent years, especially in the pre-salt Santos Basin, show 
a large increase in petroleum exploration and production in Brazil. Thus, crew transport 
demand  for drilling rigs and production platforms will increase. This transport will also 
become more complex due to increased average distance between the fields and the 
Brazilian coast. 

The helicopter is the most used modal for this purpose. This is the mean of 
transportation with the highest efficiency due to speed and safety, but it has high costs. 
Therefore, optimization of crew transport logistic network becomes an economically 
relevant issue. 

The objective of this thesis is to improve the optimization model currently used 
in Petrobras for planning of the crew transport logistics network. This model provides 
more accurate information to managers to assist the decision making regarding the 
planning of the logistics infrastructure. Such decisions involve considerable 
expenditures, and once made are not easily changed.  

The proposed model shows lower level of uncertainty in the outputs of location 
of airfields, demand allocation and profile of the fleet. The scenarios generated in the 
case study presented acceptable processing time, despite a considerable increase in 
complexity. The revisions in the model allowed its use to define the optimal profile of 
the fleet creating cost saving opportunities for the company.

 xv 
 



 

1. INTRODUÇÃO 

 

Neste capítulo apresenta-se o problema estudado nesta dissertação, os objetivos 
e contribuições do trabalho e, por fim, a estrutura desta dissertação. 

 

1.1 RELEVÂNCIA DO TEMA 

A definição da rede logística de atendimento às unidades marítimas de 

exploração e produção de petróleo, que contempla escolha da localização dos 

aeródromos, alocação da demanda a estes aeródromos e determinação do perfil da frota, 

torna-se cada dia mais impactante no custo logístico total, e conseqüentemente no custo 

de extração do óleo. Algumas causas são: 

 Crescimento acelerado das reservas de petróleo brasileiras ( 

 Figura 1); 

 Aumento das distâncias dos campos petrolíferos em relação à costa; e, 

 Aumento da dispersão das unidades marítimas (distância relativa entre estas 

unidades) (Figura 2). 

13.000 

57% de  
12.000 crescimento  

em 9 anos 
11.000 

10.000 

9.000

7.000
