3.2. Operating environment

High temperatures inside the cockpit: The helicopters [flying in
Campos Basin] do not have air conditioning systems in order to
avoid ‘‘unnecessary’’ weight.

Excessive vibration: ‘‘(y) The start and also leading indicator of
equipment malfunctions is almost always vibration. If the Captain
keeps flying with the aircraft vibrating more than usual, he will do
harm to the whole helicopter. (y)’’

Distance from hangar and offices to equipment: When there are
no available spots in front of the company’s hangar, the aircraft
park at the far end of the airport, about a half mile away. The
crews have to walk up to 8 min.
3.3. Economic environment

Personal financial constraints in flight operation decisions: Part of
pilots’ pay comes from hours effectively flown. ‘‘(y) there are
some Captains that forget about Safety in order to fly more (y)’’
‘‘(y) there are cases-with older aircraft-where the pilot knows
that the aircraft should stop flying because of excessive vibration.
But he doesn’t do that and leaves the problem to be solved during
the other 15 days [when it won’t impact his income]. (y)’’

Budget constraints in maintenance operations: Conflicts among
pilots, mechanics and inspectors were reported. If the Captain
records an aircraft problem in the Aircraft Log Book, the aircraft
must be grounded until it is repaired and the repair inspected. The
company may be fined by the regulator for not grounding an
aircraft with [formally] pending maintenance issues. So some-
times pilots do not write equipment problems or malfunctions in
the Aircraft Log, and report them informally—directly to a
mechanic—and keep flying under the Minimum Equipment List
(MEL) rules while they await favorable maintenance conditions:
parts availability and inspection timing. This can go on until the
pilot cannot stand it anymore and decides to report it officially.
Besides Safety, there is also a conflict with mechanics.
3.4. Flight infrastructure issues

The visual flight plan form is inadequate: The form uses an A4
format which is problematic because it is almost twice the size of
the clipboard used during flight (A5 landscape). The form presents
both pre-flight and in-flight information, and because of the way
this information is distributed, simply folding the form is not
sufficient. While some pilots resort to transcribing from one area
of the form to another, enabling a simple fold (see Fig. 4), other
pilots fold the form in complex ways (which they refer to as
origami), and others are constantly manipulating the form during
flight. The same pilot might adopt different flight plan use
strategies, depending on the number of legs of the flight (number
of offshore platforms he/she has to land on). This problem
compounds as the number of flight legs increases, as there is
more data to represent and there are more operations to do in
less time.

Flight region maps: The maps of the offshore air space provided
by the companies do not provide the entire set of data
pilots would like to have. In order to overcome this shortcoming,
most of them buy an unofficial map—generated by specific
specialized software—produced by an individual pilot. Besides
better information presentation (colors, better design and
layout), this map has useful information not available on the
official map, such as air traffic control system and production unit
(platforms and ships) radio frequencies, and latitude and long-
itude scales.

Congested radio communications: For much of the flight from
Macaé to the oil fields the rigs are out of radio range. Near the
platforms, drill-ships and support vessels there is a large number
of aircraft trying to communicate with them and the three
embedded air traffic controllers. At this point, when communica-
tion is crucial, it turns out to be difficult to be performed. The
frequent solution used by the pilots is to call several platforms in
rapid succession, allowing only a token interval for them to reply,
and then handle the replies as they come in, at which point they
may request a shift to another radio frequency and do all the



ARTICLE IN PRESS

316 J.O. Gomes et al. / Reliability Engineering and System Safety 94 (2009) 311–319

Fig. 4. A sample visual flight plan form.
talking over this alternate channel. There is a recommendation to
communicate with the platforms to exchange landing information
when 20 min out and then again when 5 min out, to allow for
safety preparations there, but as on many-legged flights the legs
can be very short, the crews anticipate the calls, which may have
to be remade should any delays occur. These two strategies
increase the number of communication exchanges in the air traffic
control system.



ARTICLE IN PRESS

J.O. Gomes et al. / Reliability Engineering and System Safety 94 (2009) 311–319 317
3.5. Operations issues

Flight schedules: The interval between two consecutive flights
of a same aircraft is 30 min. Within this interval, the pilots must
walk from the helicopter to the office (depending on the landing
position it may take almost 10 min), fill in a report, check the next
flight’s information, register the flight plan, walk back to the
helicopter and do a safety briefing with the passengers. In order to
be able to accomplish all those tasks, there is almost no time left
to rest.

Lack of standardized identification of the production units: ‘‘(y)
There is identification on the deck, but it should be more visible
and easier to make out. This is a great problem with small ships.
Each ship has a different place where the identification is located,
which makes it more difficult, and you have to pay more
attention. (y)’’ ‘‘(y) I have already landed on a wrong ship.’’

Ship operations: Small ships are difficult to land on. ‘‘(y) you
land on ships and platforms, it is a security area, you have fuel
issues and there are engineers [passengers] on board. It requires
specific training. You are not landing on the top of a building.
There are limit conditions for landing on ships that we call pitch
and roll, the ship’s motion. It is very hard, even your body must
adapt to it (y)’’

Rapid changes in flight planning: ‘‘(y) I leave with everything
set up, planned, and then I go to the platform, board some
passengers, etc. And, while flying, the radio informs that there is a
flight plan change. I usually leave with everything in my mind.
I have to take off from here and then communicate on this radio
frequency, etc. When it changes I find myself completely lost. OK,
so where should I go now? What should I do? Everything changes
in your mind. In my opinion, it increases the stress level a lot (y)’’

Limited contingency fleet: ‘‘(y) there should be a rescue aircraft
because the helicopter is fast. If you come by ship the guy may die
on the way to the hospital. In one case some people suffered an
accident at 11:30 am and were rescued 1 h later. People got hurt,
not much, but hurt! They reached shore 5 h later! What if
someone were seriously injured? Someone could have died (y)’’

Pilot/co-pilot coordination: ‘‘(y) that has happened to me.
I tried to help verbally, telling the pilot how to do ity And then
the pilot got angry and said:-shut your mouth, I want to learn by
myself. That is stressful. He was not respectful and that was the
end of the flight for me. And that happened at the beginning of the
15 day tour. I began waking up in the morning and thinking to
myself: damn, I have to go and fly with that guy! Then you have to
wait for him to calm down. Meanwhile, instead of helping the
captain, one stops doing it. Then one doesn’t follow the right
procedures, doesn’t do the checklist (y)’’
4. Discussion

As a consequence of the management system structure, each
actor pursues its own goals, given its area of responsibility. Goal
conflicts arise, in particular, for helicopter pilots who have to
report potential maintenance problems but can undermine their
own pay and the helicopter company’s profitability, because the
timing of inspections delays the return to service. Both the
company and the crew earn more money when the aircraft is
flying (the oil company only pays the company for helicopter
hours actually flown, or, at least, were eligible to be flown). If a
helicopter is removed from service for maintenance, the helicop-
ter companies do not earn income and have reduced capability to
provide the contracted service. Furthermore, officially reporting
technical problems may ground the craft until the next scheduled
ANAC inspection (carried out by a contractor) which occurs every
15 days. As a result, a helicopter can be out of service extra time,
while waiting for the next inspection to release the craft for
further service.

The organizational and financial relationships create pressure
to keep helicopters flying. Under this pressure, pilots face a
dilemma when considering if a problem is major enough to
initiate the official maintenance cycle. Pilots may notice problems
that are early indicators of future in-flight failures and could
report symptoms of problems to maintenance to catch problems
before they become a safety threat (plus the substantial direct and
indirect financial losses associated with in-flight failures). But
sending the helicopter for maintenance removes it from service,
losing flight time against a demanding schedule, and the lost
flight time directly leads to lost earnings, given the financial pay
and incentive systems in place.

This is an example of a sacrifice judgment created by pressure
to be faster, better, cheaper. Discovering the sacrifice dilemma
allowed the team to investigate how the system has adapted
formally and informally to cope. Fig. 5 uses a flowchart structure
to capture the basic character of the sacrifice judgment pilots face.
The system adapts to categorize problems into two classes:
those severe enough to require going directly into the official
process, including the wait for an inspection after the repairs
have been completed, and others which are light enough to allow
being reported directly to maintenance staff for correction or
investigation.

The dilemma faced by the pilot refers to whether to report a
condition officially or not. The unofficial route may keep an
aircraft in service, while maintenance evaluates the information
or orders parts. Maintenance may even ground the aircraft. The
decision to report officially or not is the pilot’s responsibility.
However, the pilots may not be maintenance experts who can
interpret the seriousness of the technical problems.

The room to maneuver arises in part because there is a
difference between two rule sets. The regulatory rules are stricter
than the MEL rules (an aircraft-specific list of items that must be
functioning in order for an aircraft to be eligible to fly, allowed by
international regulations). Thus, although there may be problems,
reporting them may not be mandatory, and if they fall short of the
stricter rules those problems may be reported directly to
maintenance independent of the official process.

The analysis uncovered a point of brittleness that leads the
overall system to be operating riskier than any party realizes.
Identifying a specific naturally occurring sacrifice dilemma
allowed the team to critically examine concepts about resilience
and brittleness in a specific system. For example, the cases involve
cross-scale interactions as pilots and maintenance adaptations
influence and are influenced by management policy decisions in a
competitive business environment. Another issue addressed was
whether recognizing a point of brittleness helps anticipate how
the system can fail and helps define interventions. The dilemma
arises in part due to gaps at organizational boundaries. How can
multiple groups or organizations coordinate roles to ensure
resilience?
5. Conclusion

Our aim in this research was to show that it is possible to
create foresight in the multiple organizations that are dealing
with the Campos Basin helicopter offshore transportation system,
using the resilience engineering framework. Resilience engineer-
ing, using CTA, looks at how sharp-end practitioners adapt to
various types of pressures and reveals brittle points in the system.
Studying cross-level interactions inside the system indicates
situations where people are acting riskier than desirable from a
safety perspective, pushing system operation closer to safety

Fig. 5. Flowchart of pilots’ sacrifice judgment.
boundary conditions. Creating foresight means anticipating the
weaknesses and discussing, reflecting and learning about the
brittleness of the system. We also wanted to show the differences
between the new resilience engineering paradigm that affords
proactive safety management, and the traditional industrial safety
paradigm of counting errors after accidents or mishaps, followed
by specific interventions to reduce this count, and the specific
errors identified.

Resilience engineering aims to understand the big picture by
articulating the local and situated activities and constraints with
the macro-organizational issues (understanding cross-level inter-
actions), to help people in different roles within the organizations
to make better production/safety tradeoffs.

In the Columbia accident, NASA’s safety management system
failed to understand the safety implications of the production/
safety tradeoffs people made in the context of foam strikes that
occurred in almost all shuttle flights. A similar situation occurred
in the Challenger accident related to the O-rings’ conditions. The
lack of understanding of the big picture regarding the loss of foam
during the previous launches that contributed to the tradeoffs
before and during the last Columbia mission (e.g. why was the
video of the foam loss during the launch disregarded by the
mission command?) is directly connected to the local decisions
made by people in the various organizational levels.

In our study we note that pilots’ sacrifice decisions depend on
perceiving symptoms of main flight operations (see, read,
interpret), and the MEL’s accuracy and freshness, and also on
pilot expertise in discriminating non-deferrable critical main-
tenance issues from postponable non-critical maintenance con-
cerns. This last issue is related to the offshore transportation
companies’ commercial relations with their pilots and with their
main customer, the oil company. Through understanding the big
picture it is possible to act and improve safety.

A proactive safety management should dig out early indicator
signs of brittle points without waiting for adverse events. This
may be possible through the understanding of the interplay
between local, situated (e.g. how and why people in different
organization positions are making decisions) and organizational
issues (contracts, investments, hierarchy).

Therefore, a proactive safety management system using
resilience engineering concepts should provide the organization
with effective means to balance safety and high-productivity
goals, by restructuring cross-level interactions to better balance
safety with production pressures. To do so, safety must be treated
as a core value, continuously monitoring the risk model and
sacrifice decisions that people are using in their daily activities.

Such resilient organizations must escape from hindsight bias, a
situation in which we have to wait for accidents to think about
safety. Indeed, as already pointed out by Weick [13] ‘‘safety is a
dynamic non event’’, or in other words, the actual safety level may
remain unnoticed if events do not happen. Rather than viewing
past success as an indicator of a good safety level, resilient
organizations must continue to invest in anticipating the chan-
ging potential for failure because they appreciate that their
knowledge of the gaps is imperfect and that their environment
constantly changes.

Accident Analysis and Prevention 40 (2008) 426–434

Routes to failure: Analysis of 41 civil aviation accidents from the Republic
of China using the human factors analysis and classification system

Wen-Chin Li a, Don Harris b,∗, Chung-San Yu c

a Psychology Department, National Defense University, No. 70, Sec. 2, Jhongyang N. Rd., Beitou District, Taipei City 112, Taiwan, ROC
b Department of Human Factors, School of Engineering, Cranfield University, Cranfield, Bedford MK43 0AL, United Kingdom

c Ministry of Defense, Taipei Postal Mailbox No. 90251-9, Taiwan, ROC

Received 28 November 2006; received in revised form 17 July 2007; accepted 17 July 2007

bstract

The human factors analysis and classification system (HFACS) is based upon Reason’s organizational model of human error. HFACS was
eveloped as an analytical framework for the investigation of the role of human error in aviation accidents, however, there is little empirical
ork formally describing the relationship between the components in the model. This research analyses 41 civil aviation accidents occurring to

ircraft registered in the Republic of China (ROC) between 1999 and 2006 using the HFACS framework. The results show statistically significant
elationships between errors at the operational level and organizational inadequacies at both the immediately adjacent level (preconditions for unsafe
cts) and higher levels in the organization (unsafe supervision and organizational influences). The pattern of the ‘routes to failure’ observed in the

ata from this analysis of civil aircraft accidents show great similarities to that observed in the analysis of military accidents. This research lends
urther support to Reason’s model that suggests that active failures are promoted by latent conditions in the organization. Statistical relationships
inking fallible decisions in upper management levels were found to directly affect supervisory practices, thereby creating the psychological
reconditions for unsafe acts and hence indirectly impairing the performance of pilots, ultimately leading to accidents.

2007 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.

ficatio

eywords: Accident analysis; Human error; Human factors analysis and classi

. Introduction

When investigating the causes of aviation accidents, the
uman factors focus has now shifted away from investigat-
ng skill deficiencies and has moved toward other factors such
s decision-making, attitudes, supervisory factors and organi-
ational culture (Diehl, 1989; Jensen, 1997). This change in
mphasis has resulted in human error frameworks and accident
nvestigation schemes being developed that investigate and cat-
gorise the organizational factors and psychological precursors
urrounding the accident in an attempt to develop a more com-
lete understanding of the circumstances and hence aid in the
evelopment of effective prevention strategies.

The human factors analysis and classification system

HFACS) (Shappell and Wiegmann, 2001, 2003, 2004; c

iegmann and Shappell, 1997, 2001a,b,c, 2003) is perhaps the e
ost widely used human factors accident analysis framework. h

s
u

∗ Corresponding author. Tel.: +44 1234 758227; fax: +44 1234 758209. c
E-mail address: d.harris@cranfield.ac.uk (D. Harris). s

001-4575/$ – see front matter © 2007 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
oi:10.1016/j.aap.2007.07.011
n system (HFACS)

FACS was developed from Reason’s organizationally based
odel of human error (Reason, 1990, 1997). In this model active

ailures (errors) of front-line operators (in this case pilots) com-
ine with latent failures lying dormant in the system to breach its
efences. These latent failures are spawned in the upper levels of
he organization and are related to management and regulatory
tructures.

HFACS addresses human errors and the factors underpinning
hem at four levels. The framework is described diagrammati-
ally in Fig. 1. Level-1 (unsafe acts of operators—active failures)
s the level at which the majority of accident investigations have
een focused in the past. These are the behaviours of the flight
rew on the flight deck that contribute directly to the accident.
ailures at this level can be further classified into two sub-
ategories: errors and violations. Errors fall into three basic
rror types (skill-based, decision and perceptual). Violations,
owever, are instances of the willful disregard of rules, which

ubsequently result in an accident. Level-2 (preconditions for
nsafe acts—latent/active failures) addresses the psychologi-
al pre-cursors to the active failures at level-1, such as the
ubstandard conditions of the operators and the operating envi-

ig. 1. The HFACS framework. Each upper level is proposed to affect items at
he lower levels (Wiegmann and Shappell, 2003).

onment which predispose them to making an error. Level-3
unsafe supervision—latent failures) traces the causal chain of
vents producing the unsafe acts up to the level of the front-line
upervisors. Level-4 (organizational influences—latent failures)
escribes the contributions of fallible decisions in upper levels of
anagement that directly affect supervisory practices, as well as

he conditions and actions of front-line operators. Each higher
evel affects the next downward level in HFACS framework.
einach and Viale (2006) argue that the use of a classifica-

ion system to investigate incidents and accidents has several
enefits compared to a less structured, more ad hoc process.
t provides a consistent and format for accident/incident data
ollection and analysis; it ensure the accident and incident inves-
igations are undertaken systematically and comprehensively; it
elps counteract investigator’s heuristics and biases and it allows
he comparisons of contributing factors across industries.

Wiegmann and Shappell (2001b) claim that the HFACS
ramework bridges the gap between theory and practice by pro-
iding safety professionals with a theoretically based tool for
dentifying and classifying the human errors in aviation mishaps.
iven that the system focuses on both latent and active failures
nd their inter-relationships, it facilitates the identification of p
he underlying causes of human error. HFACS was originally r
esigned and developed as a human error framework for inves- s
igating and analysing human error accidents in US military o
Prevention 40 (2008) 426–434 427

viation operations (Shappell and Wiegmann, 2001) however the
ramework’s developers have also demonstrated its applicability
o the analysis of accidents in US commercial aviation (Shappell
nd Wiegmann, 2001; Wiegmann and Shappell, 2001a,c) and
S general aviation (Shappell and Wiegmann, 2003). The sys-

em has also been used to analyse the underlying human factors
auses in accidents involving remotely piloted aircraft (Tvarnyas
t al., 2006); air traffic control incidents (Scarborough and
ounds, 2001) and has been further developed to investigate
oth maintenance error in aircraft (Krulak, 2004) and for the
nvestigation of railroad accidents (Reinach and Viale, 2006).

Although the HFACS framework has received wide accep-
ance as a tool for investigating and analysing aircraft accidents
here have been some criticisms of it. Beaubien and Baker (2002)
oted that it was often difficult to collect information about the
atent conditions from incident or accident reports. Furthermore,
hey added that all the evidence collected relating to its reliability
nd validity had been collected and analyzed by the developers
f the framework. However, other authors have now successfully
sed and proven the system outside the US, for example, in India
nd the Republic of China (Gaur, 2005; Li and Harris, 2005a).
hese studies have also shown the framework to have a high
egree of inter-rater reliability when coding accident narratives.
ekker (2001) suggested that the HFACS framework has only a

light link between human error and working environment and
here is some confusion between categorization and analysis.
he simple assignment of errors to categories has no explana-

ory power and suggests neither what contributes to these errors
eing made nor remedial actions to avoid them in the future.
e added that the framework merely repositioned human errors
y shifting them from the forefront to higher up in the organiza-
ion instead of finding solutions for them. Although HFACS was
ased directly on the organizational theory of failure espoused by
eason (1990, 1997) at the time it was derived there was little or
o quantitative data to support the theoretical model upon which
t was based. However, recently several studies have uncovered
tatistical relationships describing the cause/effect relationships
etween various components at the different levels in the analy-
is and classification system, giving support to the underpinning
heory behind the framework (Li and Harris, 2006a,b; Tvarnyas
t al., 2006). These analyses begin to describe statistically how
ctions and decisions at higher managerial levels promulgate
own through the organization to result in operational errors
nd accidents.

However, it needs to be noted that the studies by Li and Harris
2006a,b) and Tvarnyas et al. (2006) were undertaken in the con-
ext of military flight operations and the operation of uninhabited
ir vehicles and only in the case of the Li and Harris papers, did
he analyses progress to the highest organizational levels. Li and
arris (2006a) clearly showed that deficiencies in organizational
rocesses and organizational climate (HFACS level-4) increased
he likelihood of deficiencies in inadequate supervision (HFACS
evel-3) 13-fold. The category of ‘inadequate supervision’ had a

articularly strong association with the level-2 category of ‘crew
esource management’ (CRM). It was suggested that failures of
enior officers in supervisory positions to provide guidance and
perational doctrine to pilots by promoting good CRM prac-

28 W.-C. Li et al. / Accident Analys

ices was indirectly associated with active, operational failures.
gain, the odds ratios suggested failures as a result of poor
RM were almost 13 times more likely to occur in the pres-
nce of a concomitant failure in the category of ‘supervisory
ailure’. Finally, Li and Harris (2006a) observed that several
atent pre-conditions for unsafe acts (at HFACS level-2) showed
trong associations with active failures at level-1. These level-2
actors exhibited Reason’s classic ‘many to one’ mapping of psy-
hological precursors to active failures in several of the level-1
ategories.

As stated in the previous paragraph, this research applied
he HFACS framework to analyse Republic of China (ROC)
ir Force accidents. The objective was to provide probabilities

or the co-occurrence of categories across adjacent levels of the
FACS to establish how factors in the upper (organizational)

evels in the framework affect categories in lower (operational)
evels. It was suggested that once the significant paths were iden-
ified the development of intervention strategies should proceed

ore rapidly and effectively. Civil airlines have very quite dif-
erent organizational imperatives and organizational structure.
arris and Morley (2006) argue that military aviation is a much

ess open system than is commercial civil aviation (see Katz and
ahn, 1978). The armed forces exert far more control over flight
perations and, at least in peacetime, the operating environment.
ir Forces are responsible for much of the maintenance of the

ircraft they fly; they operate their own airfields and provide their
wn air traffic control services; they train their own pilots and
ngineers and all personnel are indoctrinated into the military
ulture and way of working. In contrast, civil airlines operate
nto a wide range of airports (none of which they own); aircraft

aintenance is often provided by third parties; aircraft ramp
ervicing is almost invariably provided by a range of external
uppliers and ATC is provided by the air traffic services providers
rom the countries into which they either operate or overfly. From
n organizational perspective, airlines can be considered to be
onsiderably more ‘open’ than military aviation. As a result, it
eeds to be established if the same routes to failure exist in the
peration of commercial aircraft.

This study examines 41 accidents occurring to civil aircraft
n the ROC using the HFACS framework. Using data from ROC
ivil aviation accidents allows a direct comparison (without any
ultural confounding effects) of the organizational routes to fail-
re with the earlier work undertaken with the air force of the
ame country.

. Method

.1. Data

The aviation accident reports were obtained from ROC Avi-
tion Safety Council between 1999 and 2006. A total of 41
ccidents and reportable incidents occurred within this time
eriod. All accidents and serious incidents conformed to the

efinition within the 9th edition of the Convention on Interna- d
ional Civil Aviation, Annex 13 (International Civil Aviation o
rganisation, 2006). There were 24 different types of aircraft

nvolved in the accidents analysed, including commercial jets a
Prevention 40 (2008) 426–434

irliners (Airbus A300, A320 and A330; Boeing B737 and
747; McDonnell-Douglas MD11, MD82, MD83 and MD90):
rivate jets (Bombardier BD700): turbo-prop powered aircraft
ATR 72-200; De Havilland Canada DASH-8-300, Fokker 50)
nd commercial helicopters (Bell UH-1H, 206 and 430; Boe-
ng 234; Eurocopter BK117). Full copies of all these accident
eports may be found on the ROC Aviation Council web site
http://www.asc.gov.tw/asc en/accident list 1.asp).

.2. Classification framework

The version of the HFACS framework described in
iegmann and Shappell (2003) was utilised in this study

see Fig. 1). Level-1 of the HFACS categorises events under
he headings of ‘unsafe acts of operators’ that can lead to
n accident. This comprises four sub-categories of ‘decision
rrors’; ‘skill-based errors’; ‘perceptual errors’ and ‘violations’.
evel-2 of HFACS is concerned with ‘preconditions for unsafe
cts’. This has seven sub-categories within it: ‘adverse mental
tates’; ‘adverse physiological states’; ‘physical/mental lim-
tations’; ‘crew resource management’; ‘personal readiness’;
physical environment’ and ‘technological environment’. Level-

of HFACS is concerned with ‘unsafe supervision’ which
ncludes the four categories: ‘inadequate supervision’; ‘planned
nappropriate operation’; ‘failure to correct known problem’ and
supervisory violation’. Level-4, the highest level in the frame-
ork is labelled ‘organizational influences’ and comprises of

hree sub-categories: ‘resource management’; ‘organizational
limate’ and ‘organizational process’.

.3. Coding process

Two aviation human factors specialists coded each accident
eport independently. The analysts had previously been trained
ogether on the use of the analysis and categorization framework
o ensure that they achieved a detailed and accurate understand-
ng of it. This training consisted of three half-day modules
elivered by an aviation psychologist. The training syllabus
ncluded an introduction to the HFACS framework; explana-
ion of the definitions of the four different levels of HFACS;
nd a further detailed description of the content of the eighteen
ndividual HFACS categories. Subsequent to this the raters then
ointly analysed 2 years of the ROC air force accident data to
evelop a shared understanding of the categorization process
nd achieve a common understanding of the categories. Prior to
ndertaking the present study these analysts also undertook the
nalysis of 523 ROC air force accidents reported elsewhere (Li
nd Harris, 2006a,b).

The presence or the absence of each HFACS category was
ent, thus this count acted simply as an indicator of the presence
r absence of each of the 18 categories within a given accident.

Where there were discrepancies in the categorisation of an
ccident, the raters convened and resolved their observations.

. Results

.1. Inter-rater reliability

Prior to resolution of discrepancies in coding between the
aters, the inter-rater reliabilities, calculated on a category-by-
ategory, basis were assessed using Cohen’s Kappa. In half the
ategories the Kappa values was in excess of 0.40, which is
egarded as being acceptable (Landis and Koch, 1977). For the
emainder of the categories, though, the Kappa value failed to
chieve this level. Values for Kappa range between 0 and 1.00,
here 0 represents complete independence between raters and
.00 is indicative of perfect agreement (Cohen, 1960).

Below 0.40 is regarded as a poor level of inter-rater relia-
ility. However, Cohen’s Kappa has several weaknesses as an
ndex of inter-rater reliability. Low observed frequencies can
istort Kappa values, deflating its value where there is actually
very high level of agreement. Cohen’s Kappa becomes unreli-
ble when the vast majority of observations fall into just one of
he categories and there is also a high percentage of agreement
etween raters in this category (as in the category ‘adverse phys-
ological states’—see Table 1). In such a case Cohen’s Kappa
ill be low as the statistic is based upon expected probabili-

ies calculated from the marginal observed totals (Huddlestone,
003). Gwet (2002a,b) also observed that Kappa does not take
n account raters’ sensitivity and specificity. Gwet also observed
hat Kappa becomes unreliable when raters’ agreement is either

ery small or very high. As a result, inter-rater reliabilities were f
lso calculated as a simple percentage rate of agreement. These r
howed reliability figures of between 63.4 and 95.1%, indicating r
cceptable reliability between the raters. l

.2. Overview of analysis rationale

The data were cross-tabulated to describe the strength of
ssociation between the categories at adjacent levels in the
FACS framework. Chi-square (χ2) analyses were performed

o estimate the statistical strength of association between the
ategories in the higher and lower levels of the framework.
owever, as the χ2 test is a simple test of association these

nalyses were supplemented with further analyses using Good-
an and Kruskal’s lambda (λ) which was used to calculate

he proportional reduction in error (PRE). The lambda statistic
s a measure (ranging from 0 to 1, where 1 represents cer-
ainty) of the extent that knowledge of the category of one
ariable improves the prediction of the other variable. Lambda
Goodman and Kruskal, 1954) has the advantage of being a
irectional statistic. The lower level categories in the HFACS
ere designated as being dependent upon the categories at the

mmediately higher level in the framework, which is congru-
nt with the framework’s underlying theoretical assumptions.
rom a theoretical standpoint, lower levels in the HFACS can-
ot adversely affect higher levels. Higher levels in the HFACS
re deemed to influence (cause) changes at the lower orga-
izational levels, thus going beyond what may be deemed a
imple test of co-occurrence between categories. Finally, odds
atios were also calculated to provide an estimate of the like-
ihood of the presence of a contributory factor in one HFACS
ategory being associated with the concomitant presence of a

actor in another category. However, it must be noted that odds
atios are an asymmetric measure and so are only really theo-
etically meaningful when associated with a non-zero value for
ambda.

.3. Results

In total 330 instances of human error, describing the under-
ying causal factors of the 41 accidents, were recorded using the
FACS framework.
Initial results found that there were 107 instances of acts

32.4% of all errors recorded) at the level of ‘unsafe acts of oper-
tors’ (level-1). Errors at this level were implicated in 85.4%
35) of accidents. There were 84 (25.4%) instances of errors
t the level of ‘Preconditions for unsafe acts’ level (level-2).
rrors recorded at this level were implicated in 82.9% (34) of
ll the accidents analysed. At the level of ‘unsafe supervision’
level-3), there were 82 (24.8%) instances of error that were
mplicated in 75.6% (31) of accidents. Finally, at the ‘organiza-
ional influences’ level in the HFACS model (level-4) there were
7 (17.3%) instances of error recorded, implicated in 68.3% (28)
f accidents in the sample (see Table 1).

To keep the overall type I error rate across the whole analysis
o p < 0.05, using a Bonferroni adjustment (Sankoh et al., 1997)
he individual alpha level for each analysis was set to p < 0.001.
sing this value, 16 relationships between categories at adjacent
FACS levels showed significant associations (see Table 2).
Analysis of the strength of association between categories at

FACS level-4 ‘organizational influences’ and HFACS level-3
unsafe supervision’ indicated that of a possible 12 relation-
hips, six pairs of associations were significant (p < 0.001)
etween categories at adjacent levels. ‘Organizational process’

as significantly associated with all four supervisory factors at a

s over 10 times more likely to occur when there are organiza-

imilarly, inadequate supervision is nine times more likely to
ccur in the presence of poor organizational processes, an issue
lso associated with inflating the likelihood of failing to correct
nown problems by a similar amount.

or unsafe acts’ shows just three pairs of associations to be sig-

hese were ‘inadequate supervision’ at level-3 versus ‘CRM’
t level-2; ‘planned inappropriate operations’ at level three with
he ‘physical environment’ at level-2 and ‘supervisory violation’
ersus ‘personal readiness’. Of these comparisons it can be seen
hat poor CRM was over eight times more likely to occur in the
resence of ‘inadequate supervision’ at the higher organizational
evel.

ith three categories of unsafe act: ‘decision errors’; ‘skill-based

rrors’ and ‘violations’. In the second grouping ‘adverse mental

f ‘perceptual error’.

Of particular note are the downward relationships the cat-
gory of poor CRM has with the three level-1 categories.
nspection of the associated odds ratios show between (approx-
mately) a 30–40-fold increase in the likelihood of error or
iolation in the presence of poor CRM practices.

. Discussion

It can be seen from the data presented in Table 1 that the
ajority of HFACS categories had large enough numbers of

nstances of occurrence in the data set to allow reasonable
onfidence in the pattern of results obtained. All categories
lso exhibited good levels of inter-rater reliability ranging from
3.4% for the HFACS level-4 category of ‘resource manage-
ent’ to 95.1% for the level-2 category of ‘adverse physiological

tates’. These were as good as, or in excess of the levels reported
n previous studies (e.g. Gaur, 2005; Li and Harris, 2005a;

iegmann and Shappell, 1997).
Reason (1990, 1997) proposed that latent conditions promot-

ng unsafe acts are inevitably present in all systems. The original
ecision on how to allocate resources made at the highest levels

n the organization may originally have been based on sound ‘
ommercial arguments but such inequities can create reliabil- e
ty or safety problems in other, operational parts of the system. d
he analyses in this paper clearly show that inadequacies at e
the significant associations (p < 0.001) using Chi-square (χ2) and lambda (λ).

FACS level-4 (‘organizational influences’) had associations
ith further inadequacies at HFACS level-3 (‘unsafe supervi-

ion) (see Table 2 and Fig. 2). The category of ‘organizational
rocess’ is a particularly important factor at this highest organi-
ational level. Poor ‘organizational processes’ were associated
ith inadequacies in all categories at the level of ‘unsafe super-
ision’ and hence indirectly were ultimately at the root of many
perational errors resulting in accidents. In the HFACS frame-
ork inadequacies in ‘organizational processes’ includes such

ssues as imposing excessive time pressures on staff; poor mis-
ion scheduling; poor incentivization; management failing to set
learly defined objectives; poor risk management programmes;
nadequate management checks for safety and failing to establish
afety programmes. ‘Resource management’ (which involved
he selection, staffing and training of human resources at an
rganizational level; excessive cost cutting; providing unsuit-
ble equipment, and a failure to remedy design flaws) also
howed strong relationships with the two level-3 categories
f ‘inadequate supervision’ and ‘planned inappropriate opera-
ions). Both Reason (1990) and Wiegmann and Shappell (2003)
ypothesized that inappropriate decision-making by upper-level
anagement can adversely influence the personnel and practices

t the supervisory level, which in turn affects the psychological
re-conditions and hence the subsequent actions of the front-
ine operators. This study provides statistical support for this
ypothesized relationship. A similar pattern of results was also
ound in the analysis of 523 ROC air force accidents previously
eported by Li and Harris (2006a,b).

The level-2 (‘preconditions for unsafe acts’) category of

CRM’ was perhaps the key factor in HFACS framework. CRM
ncompasses issues such as a lack of teamwork on the flight
eck; poor communication between flight crew; failures of lead-
rship and inadequate crew briefings. Inadequacies in CRM

32 W.-C. Li et al. / Accident Analys

ractices were particularly influenced by the level-3 category of
inadequate supervision’. This category in the HFACS frame-
ork encompasses issues such as a failure to provide proper

raining or adequate rest periods; a lack of accountability; failure
o track qualifications and performance of personnel; the use of
ntrained supervisors and a general loss of situation awareness
t the supervisory level). The risk of ‘inadequate supervision’
as itself greatly inflated by poor organizational processes and

esource management issues. The earlier study of Li and Harris
2006a,b) also observed that the issue of inadequate supervision
as the key link between inadequacies at the higher organiza-

ional levels and poor CRM.
Reason (1990) suggested that human behaviour is governed

y the interplay between psychological and situational fac-
ors. In particular two pre-conditions for unsafe acts (HFACS
evel-2—‘CRM’ and ‘adverse mental states’) showed a strong
tatistical relationship with the active failures of the operators at
evel-1 (see Table 2 and Fig. 2). This was also observed in the
arlier study of ROC air force accidents. Poor CRM is associ-
ted with ‘decision errors’ (instances in this category included,
electing inappropriate strategies to perform a mission; improper
n-flight planning; making an inappropriate decision to abort a
ake-off or landing, or using improper remedial actions in an
mergency) and ‘skill based errors’ (for example, inappropriate
tick and rudder coordination; excessive use of flight controls;
lideslope not being maintained, and adopting an improper air-
peed or altitude). However, it is also noticeable that poor CRM
ractices are associated with the level-1 category of ‘violations’,
hich encompasses issues such as intentionally ignoring stan-
ard operating procedures (SOPs); neglecting SOPs; applying
mproper SOPs; and diverting from SOPs. Adverse mental states
re those conditions that affect pilot performance, such as loss
f situational awareness; task fixation; distraction and mental
atigue due to stress. It is perhaps not too surprising that such
ental states pre-dispose accident involved pilots to all the main

ategories of human error.
Reason (1990, 1997) has suggested that there is a ‘many to

ne’ mapping of the psychological precursors of unsafe acts
nd the actual errors themselves, making it difficult to predict
hich actual errors will occur as a result of which precondi-

ions. The results of this study using the HFACS framework
upport this assertion. It is also evident that accidents rarely
nvolved just a single error. Overall every accident involved an
verage of over eight individual errors distributed across the
arious organizational levels and even across different organi-
ations. These erroneous actions need not be confined to either
he flight deck or the airline. For example, the report on the
nvestigation of the runway incursion occurrence involving a
ransAsia Airways aircraft (flight GE543) concluded that there
as inadequate planning and implementation in airport con-

truction safety procedures by both the Taiwanese Civil Aviation
uthority (CAA) and the Military Authorities (both problems

n ‘organizational process’, HFACS level-4); there was an error

n issuing an approval to land after the curfew time (‘decision r
rror’, HFACS level-1) and insufficient cooperation and coor- e
ination between the CAA and the military base authorities r
rior to commencing construction work (‘inadequate supervi- fi
Prevention 40 (2008) 426–434

ion’, HFACS level-3). There was also a lack of communication
etween ATC and workers on the ground (a coordination and
ersonnel resource management [‘CRM’] issue, HFACS level-
) and finally there was the action of workers to enter the airfield
ithout approval by ATC (‘violation’, HFACS level-1) (Aviation
afety Council, 2004).

The errors finally resulting in an accident may also be sepa-
ated by considerable periods of time. To illustrate, in the China
irlines CI-611 accident, the principal causal factors could be

racked back to a failure to follow the Structural Repair Manual
or the repair of a tail strike occurring to the aircraft 22 years
rior to the accident. It was concluded that there was no work
udit system and a failure to provide both flight safety inspection
raining and a handbook for inspectors (‘resource management’
nd ‘organizational process’ issues, HFACS level-4). There was
lso inadequate supervision when conducting the Corrosion Pre-
ention and Control Program (‘inadequate supervision’, HFACS
evel-3). There was a failure of communication between the

aintenance Operation Center and the Maintenance Planning
ection in the airline (a ‘CRM’ issue, HFACS level-2) and a
ailure to provide adequate equipment for structural inspections
‘technology environment’, HFACS level-2). This contributed
irectly to the line operator’s failure to detect structural defects in
he rear pressure bulkhead (‘perceptual’ and ‘skill-based errors’,
evel-1). The pressure bulkhead finally ruptured causing the air-
raft to break up in flight (Aviation Safety Council, 2002; Li and
arris, 2005b).
There are statistically significant associations between causal

actors at the higher organizational levels, psychological contrib-
tory factors and errors in decision-making, skilled performance
nd the violations committed by pilots (see Table 2 and Fig. 2).
t can even be suggested that poor organizational processes at
he highest levels in the organization result in poor supervisory
versight, which itself results in poor CRM resulting in wilful
iolations of the SOP. However, some care needs to be taken
hen interpreting the statistical relationships presented within
ig. 2. In a few categories the frequency counts are moderately
mall. Furthermore, the frequency counts within categories were
ll derived from accidents. It is unknown (and at the higher orga-
izational levels perhaps it is unknowable) how often instances
ithin the various HFACS categories have occurred in day-to-
ay operations that have not resulted in an accident. Thus, the
elationships between HFACS levels and categories should not
e interpreted outside the accident causal sequence. However,
ome insight into the relative frequency of instances of error that
ave not resulted in an accident at HFACS levels 1 and 2 may
erhaps be gained by the retrospective analysis of LOSA (Line
perations Safety Audit) data. These are data gained through

he observation (by trained observers) of crews on the flight
eck engaged in everyday line operations, undertaken for the
urpose of identifying the day-to-day threats to commercial avi-
tion safety (see Klinect et al., 1999; Helmreich et al., 2001).
or example, in LOSA data collected by Thomas (2003) it was

eported that in line operations, crews often did not demonstrate
ffective error detection. More than half of all errors observed
emaining undetected by one or both of the flight crew. Further,
ne-grain analysis of this type of data may give some insight

W.-C. Li et al. / Accident Analys

nto the frequency with which undetected errors committed at
he lower HFACS levels do not subsequently result in an acci-
ent. Nevertheless, the results of this study of civil aviation
ccidents occurring in the ROC show a remarkable similarity
o the study of military accidents conducted in the air force of
he same country (Li and Harris, 2006a,b).

. Conclusions

This study provides an understanding, based upon empirical
vidence, of how actions and decisions at higher managerial lev-
ls in the operation of commercial aircraft in the ROC result in
rrors on the flight deck and subsequent accidents. The results
how clearly defined, statistically described paths that relate
rrors at level-1 (the operational level) with inadequacies at both
he immediately adjacent and also higher levels in the organi-
ation. To reduce significantly the accident rate these ‘paths to
ailure’ relating to these organizational and human factors must
e addressed. This research draws a clear picture that supports
eason’s (1990) model of active failures resulting from latent
onditions in the organization. Furthermore, the mechanisms
elating to operational errors seem to be common to both civil
nd military operation in the ROC despite the different orga-
izational imperatives and organizational structures underlying
hese types of operation (see Harris and Morley, 2006). Further
ork needs to be undertaken to establish if a similar pattern of

esults are found in other countries and cultures.
The results suggest that interventions at HFACS levels 1 and

would only have limited effect in improving overall safety.
or example, CRM inadequacies are associated with subsequent
rror in three out of four categories at HFACS level-1. How-
ver, improving CRM practices alone is unlikely to have a major
mpact on safety unless the supervisory processes (level-3) and
rganizational processes (level-4) are in place to provide things
uch as training facilities; the mechanisms to oversee CRM train-
ng and monitor its effectiveness, and respond to any further
hanges required in the training program. All of these activi-
ies require organizational commitment and capacity, which can
nly be provided by the highest levels of management. Further-
ore, on a ‘bang for the buck’ basis, interventions at higher

evels are likely to be the most cost effective in the net safety
enefits they realize. Remedial safety actions should be aimed at
he higher organizational areas that share the greatest numbers
f associations with factors at lower organizational levels, in
his case ‘organizational processes’ and ‘resource management’
t HFACS level-4. These are the categories at the root of the
aths of association with many other HFACS categories. This
trongly suggests that the greatest gains in safety benefits could
e achieved by targeting actions in these areas.

Probabilistic modeling of chloride-induced corrosion in concrete structures
using first- and second-order reliability methods
Bassem Saassouh ⇑, Zoubir Lounis
National Research Council Canada, Institute for Research in Construction, Ottawa, Ontario, Canada K1A 0R6

a r t i c l e i n f o a b s t r a c t
Article history:
Received 3 March 2011
Received in revised form 3 April 2012
Accepted 4 May 2012
Available online 11 May 2012

Keywords:
Chloride ingress
Corrosion initiation
Uncertainty modeling
First- and second-order reliability methods
Importance factor
Probability of corrosion
⇑ Corresponding author. Tel.: +1 613 993 6290.
E-mail address: bassem.saassouh@nrc.gc.ca (B. Saa

0958-9465/$ - see front matter Crown Copyright  2
http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.cemconcomp.2012.05.001
Concrete structures are subjected to chloride-induced corrosion that can lead to shortened service life.
Reliable predictions of life cycle performance of concrete structures are critical to the optimization of
their life cycle design and maintenance to minimize their life cycle costs. This paper presents two simpli-
fied semi-analytical probabilistic models based on the first- and second-order reliability methods to
model the uncertainty of the key parameters including surface chloride concentration, chloride threshold,
cover depth and diffusion coefficient, which govern the chloride ingress into concrete and corrosion of
reinforcing steel. A case study of a reinforced concrete highway bridge deck is used to illustrate the capa-
bility and efficiency of these simplified probabilistic models in modeling the uncertainty and predicting
the time-dependent probability of corrosion. The models enable to quantify the impact of the different
governing parameters on probability of corrosion and service life, which can be used to develop cost-
effective management strategies.

Crown Copyright  2012 Published by Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
1. Introduction

The main cause of deterioration of different types of concrete
structures (bridges, marine structures, off-shore platforms, parking
garages, etc.) is due to chloride-induced corrosion of the steel rein-
forcement. The primary source of chlorides is from the external
environment, either from deicing salts using during the winter
months or from seawater. The rate of ingress or penetration of
chlorides is dependent primarily on the quality of concrete and
more particularly on the water–cement ratio of the concrete mix,
the presence of supplementary cementing materials (e.g. silica
fume, fly ash, or slag), and/or protective systems that delay or slow
down chloride ingress (e.g. protective membrane). In porous med-
ia, such as concrete, chlorides can penetrate concrete via different
physical mechanisms, such as diffusion, capillary absorption, elec-
trical migration, presence of cracks and permeation due to hydrau-
lic pressure heads [25]. Furthermore, the penetration process is
also influenced by chemical reactions, such as chloride binding.

The corrosion of the steel reinforcement leads to concrete frac-
ture through cracking, delamination and spalling of the concrete
cover, reduction of concrete and reinforcement cross sections, loss
of bond between the reinforcement and concrete, and reduction in
strength and ductility. As a result, the serviceability, strength,
safety and service life of concrete structures are reduced.
ssouh).

012 Published by Elsevier Ltd. All
In the last three decades, chloride-induced corrosion has been
extensively studied [43,39,8], particularly, as a result of the high
costs of highway bridge repair in North America and Europe from
the effects of chloride-induced corrosion and deterioration of con-
crete infrastructure. Most of these studies are based on the assump-
tion of a process of diffusion for predicting the time and space
variations of chloride content in concrete and on the concept of
chloride threshold to define the corrosion resistance of reinforcing
steel to chloride attack. Despite the large amount of research, the
design of concrete structures for durability is mainly based on pre-
scriptive rules, such as specifying minimum concrete cover depth
(depending on the environmental exposure), maximum water-to-
cement ratio (to achieve low chloride diffusivity), use of more cor-
rosion resistant reinforcing steels (e.g. stainless steel), and use of
protection systems. However, a considerable level of uncertainty
may be associated with one or more of the above parameters. This
is due to: (i) heterogeneity and aging of concrete with temporal and
spatial variability of its chloride diffusivity; (ii) variability of con-
crete cover depth, which depends on quality control, workmanship
and size of the structure; (iii) variability of surface chloride concen-
tration, that depends on the severity of the environmental expo-
sure, precipitation, washing of structure; and (iv) uncertainty in
chloride threshold level that depends on the type of reinforcing
steel, type of cementing materials, test methods, etc. [1]. It is clear
that the combination of these uncertainties leads to a considerable
uncertainty in the model output i.e. the time to corrosion initiation
or service life. This uncertainty in the model output could have seri-
ous consequences in terms of inadequate design, planning of
rights reserved.



B. Saassouh, Z. Lounis / Cement & Concrete Composites 34 (2012) 1082–1093 1083
inspection and maintenance which in turn will shorten the service
life of the structure and increase the life cycle cost [50,11]. There-
fore, for an effective performance-based life cycle design that in-
cludes the use of mechanistic service life models will lead to a
minimization of cost at the same time provides the level of service
life required.

The objectives of this paper are threefold: (i) present a perfor-
mance-based approach to durability or life cycle design based on
mechanistic models for chloride ingress into concrete and onset
of steel reinforcement corrosion; (ii) present simplified semi-ana-
lytical probabilistic models based on first-order and second-order
reliability methods (FORM/SORM) that take into account the
uncertainty and variability in the governing parameters and prop-
agate it to predict the time-dependent probability of chloride con-
tamination of concrete cover and probability of corrosion of steel
reinforcement; and (iii) to assess the efficiency of the simplified
analytical FORM and SORM methods by comparing their predic-
tions to those obtained by using Monte Carlo simulation (MCS)
with a large number of simulations on a typical highway concrete
bridge deck exposed to deicing salts during winter.
2. Overview of mechanisms of chloride ingress into concrete
structures

Since concrete is a porous medium, chlorides can penetrate con-
crete through a number of mechanisms. First, if the concrete is not
fully saturated, the salt solution is absorbed into the unfilled
spaces by capillary action [19]. As the chlorides migrate into the
concrete, some chlorides are absorbed onto concrete and react
with the cement hydrates [38], which slows down the rate of chlo-
ride migration [31]. When the surface dries, the salt crystallizes.
With successive cycles, there may be a progressive build-up of salts
at the surface and in the near surface layers until, after few cycles, a
limiting value is reached [36]. The time it takes to achieve a stable
surface level will, however, depend on the severity of exposure. In
addition to this early absorption, the chloride ions migrate further
into the concrete by diffusion through the pore water that is driven
by the concentration gradient between the surface and the sub-
surface layers. As the chlorides migrate further, so the process of
‘‘binding’’ continues to remove chlorides from the pore solution
[2]. In concrete, which is permanently submerged and saturated,
this diffusion process is the only means of transport after the initial
wetting. However, even without drying there may be a build-up of
chlorides in the pores water to a level above that of the surround-
ing saline solution, due to a process defined as chloride ‘‘condensa-
tion’’ [35]. This is may be due to the physical adsorption of
chlorides onto the pore surfaces by the electric double layer in ce-
ment and can result in chloride concentrations, which can be two
to four times those of the solutions to which the concrete is ex-
posed. These are commonly referred to as loosely-bound chlorides.

Therefore, in the absence of shrinkage phenomena, the main
transport mechanisms of chlorides into concrete are diffusion
and adsorption [25]. However, adsorption occurs in concrete sur-
face layers that are subjected to wetting and drying cycles, and it
only affects the exposed concrete surface down to 10–20 mm
[44], so beyond this adsorption zone, the diffusion process will
dominate [44,14].

Otherwise, if the concrete cover presents a considerable amount
of cracking, two modeling options might be proposed:

 In a cracked concrete cover with relatively few and small cracks,
the effect of cracks on the chloride ingress can be considered, by
using the same diffusion model with a modified ‘‘apparent’’ dif-
fusion coefficient that takes into account the effect of cracks on
the diffusion as in [12,17,16,6].
 In a cracked concrete cover with several and large cracks, diffu-
sion may not be the governing mechanism and the effect of
cracks may lead to ‘‘permeability’’ as a mechanism for chloride
transport that, by itself, needs to be modeled. This article does
not take into account this case.

The chloride diffusion phenomena is a transfer of mass by ran-
dom motion of free chloride ions in the pore solution, resulting in a
net flow from regions of higher to regions of lower concentration
[10]. The rate of chloride ingress is proportional to the concentra-
tion gradient and the diffusion coefficient of the concrete (Fick’s
first law of diffusion). Since in the field, chloride ingress occurs un-
der transient conditions, Fick’s second law of diffusion can be used
to predict the time variation of chloride concentration for one-
dimensional flow. This law can be expressed as a relationship be-
tween the diffusion coefficient and the gradients of concentration,
by direct analogy with the equations of heat conduction [10], as
follows:  
@Cx;t ¼ @ @C

D x;t ð1Þ
@t @x @x

where Cx, t is the concentration of chlorides at depth x and time t; D
is the diffusion coefficient of chlorides, which is assumed to have a
constant value, called ‘‘apparent’’ (or ‘‘effective’’) diffusion coeffi-
cient [2].

In fact, at early age, the diffusion coefficient presents a wide
range of scatter (especially with respect to the 28-day value); how-
ever this scatter becomes less significant, especially after 5 years.
All types of remaining uncertainties, such as time and space varia-
tion are supposed to be captured by the probabilistic model pro-
posed. Therefore, the diffusion coefficient used is taken as a
random variable having its characteristics based on, an ‘‘effective’’
time-invariant mean value, which is a good fit in the diffusion
model, and varies in a narrow range of standard deviation to take
into account the remaining uncertainties, that are induced by the
effects of chloride binding and concrete aging.

Under the assumptions of a constant diffusion coefficient, a con-
stant surface chloride concentration Cs as the boundary condition,
and the initial condition specified as Cx, t = 0 for x > 0, t = 0, Crank’s
solutionyields [10]:

Cx;t ¼ Cs 1 pxerf ffiffiffiffiffi ð2Þ
2 Dt

where erf(.) is the error function or Gauss error function, which is
very similar to the standard normal (or Gaussian) cumulative distri-
bution function [U(.)]. erf(.) and U(.) differ only by scaling and
translation as follows:

pffiffiffi
erf ðxÞ ¼ 2Uðx 2Þ  1 ð3Þ
In modeling the chloride ingress as a Fickian process, the following
assumptions are made:

 Some phenomena (e.g. capillary flow, chloride binding, etc.) are
not explicitly considered.
 Crank’ solution for a plane sheet assumes dealing with plane

isotropic concrete structures, i.e. one dimensional diffusion.
 The diffusion coefficient and surface chloride concentration are

assumed time-invariant.
 Initial concentration of chlorides in the concrete is negligible.
 Interaction with other ions and with electrical double layer is

ignored [9].

These conditions are not necessarily satisfied, for many reasons.
For example, the diffusion coefficient is not constant because it is
influenced by the following factors [25]: (i) concrete is not



1084 B. Saassouh, Z. Lounis / Cement & Concrete Composites 34 (2012) 1082–1093
homogenous with some micro-cracks or some shrinkage-induced
cracks; (ii) moisture is unlikely to be uniform, and its input can
be considerable when the degree of saturation is high; (iii) some
chlorides are bound by cement hydrates; (iv) D is influenced by
concentration of chlorides content; and (v) chloride binding
changes with time.

Therefore, to minimize the error in assuming time-invariant diffu-
sion coefficient and surface chloride concentration, concrete cores are
usually taken from the concrete structure to determine the ‘‘apparent’’
or ’’effective’’ diffusion coefficient and ‘‘apparent’’ surface concentra-
tion by fitting the field data to Fick’s 2nd law of diffusion.

3. Overview of mechanism of corrosion of steel reinforcement
in concrete structures

The corrosion of conventional carbon or ‘‘black’’ steel reinforce-
ment embedded in concrete structures is an electrochemical pro-
cess in which the steel is dissolved [46]. In the general corrosion
process of steel in concrete, the steel loses its ions at the anode
by the oxidation reaction (Fe ? Fe++ + 2e). The electrons are re-
leased and transported to the cathode for the reduction of oxygen
(O2 + 2H2O + 4e? 4OH). The flow of ions through the concrete
pore solution and electrons form an electrical current for the reac-
tions to proceed. As a result, the electrical resistivity of concrete,
availability of oxygen, and chemistry of the pore solution are the
determining factors for the corrosion kinetics of the carbon steel
in a concrete medium [43,50]. The concrete pore solution is natu-
rally alkaline with a pH level between 12.5 and 13.5. Embedded in
such an environment, the steel is protected from corrosion by pas-
sivity, which is a condition for corrosion resistance of an active me-
tal caused by the formation of a thin surface film of corrosion
products [46]. The composition of the passive film is uncertain, it
is either ferrous (Fe2+) or ferric (Fe3+) oxides.

The hydroxyl ions (OH) act as a corrosion inhibitor in concrete
that is opposing the corrosive action of chloride ions, which destroy
the protective passive film. Although, the interaction mechanism be-
tween the chlorides and passive film is not well understood, the
mechanism can be analyzed from various viewpoints, including:

(i) Disruption of the passive film: The presence of chlorides
changes the composition, thickness, and density of the pas-
sive film, and increases its water content [33]. This facilitates
chloride penetration and consequently breaks down the pas-
sive film;

(ii) Reduction of the pH level: The pH level or concentration of
OH and Cl are two competing forces in the corrosion pro-
cess. The pH is reduced by an acid hydrolysis reaction as fol-
lows [23]:

Fe2þ þ 2H2Oþ 2Cl ! FeðOHÞ2 þ 2HCl ð4Þ
(iii) Catalyst of oxidation: Chlorides act also as a catalyst for the
oxidation of the steel by taking an active part in the reac-
tions [46]:

2Feþ 6Cl ! 2ðFeCl3Þ þ 4e ð5Þ
2ðFeCl3Þ þ 2OH ! FeðOHÞ2 þ 3Cl ð6Þ
The reactions consume OH ions and then release Cl ions back
into the solution. It results in a concentration of Cl and a reduc-
tion of pH at the point of corrosion initiation, which accounts for
pitting corrosion. The role of chlorides, more specifically free Cl,
in inducing corrosion is generally thought to be a competing
process with OH- that stabilizes the passive film. Therefore, a
relative high ratio of OH/Cl is critical to maintain the integrity
of the passive film. The quantity of chloride ions that initiate
corrosion is referred to as the ‘‘threshold chloride concentration’’
(Cth).
Corrosion in concrete structures can be described as a two
stage process [43]: (i) corrosion initiation; and (ii) corrosion prop-
agation. The corrosion initiation stage corresponds to the process
of chloride ions (chlorides) ingress into concrete (and/or carbon-
ation) while the steel remains passivated. The corrosion propaga-
tion stage starts after the initiation of active corrosion, and it
often occurs after the chloride concentration on the steel surface
reaches a certain threshold value (so-called ‘‘chloride threshold’’
Cth [43]) which depassivates the steel. Ideally, the corrosion initi-
ates at time Tei , which corresponds to an amount of chlorides (at
the steel level), which is greater than or equal to the chloride
threshold as follows:

C
