Quantitative risk analysis offshore—Human and organizational factors
Jon Espen Skogdalen n, Jan Erik Vinnem
Department of Industrial Economics, Risk Management and Planning, University of Stavanger, 4036 Stavanger, Norway
a r t i c l e i n f o

Article history:
Received 20 May 2010
Received in revised form
10 December 2010
Accepted 24 December 2010
Available online 4 January 2011

Keywords:
Quantitative Risk Analysis
Human factors
Organizational factors
viations: ALARP, As Low As Reasonably Pra
onal Risk Analysis; DP, Dynamic Positioning;
fshore; HSE, Health, Safety and Environment
ve [United Kingdom]; HOF, Human and Orga
Reliability Analysis; PSA, Petroleum Safety a
ative Risk Analysis
esponding author. Tel./fax: +47 99 02 41 71.
ail addresses: jon.espen.skogdalen@gmail.com
ventor.no (J.E. Vinnem).

20/$ - see front matter & 2011 Elsevier Ltd. A
016/j.ress.2010.12.013
a b s t r a c t

Quantitative Risk Analyses (QRAs) are one of the main tools for risk management within the Norwegian
and UK oil and gas industry. Much criticism has been given to the limitations related to the QRA-models
and that the QRAs do not include human and organizational factors (HOF-factors). Norway and UK
offshore legislation and guidelines require that the HOF-factors are included in the QRAs. A study of 15
QRAs shows that the factors are to some extent included, and there are large differences between the
QRAs. The QRAs are categorized into four levels according to the findings. Level 1 QRAs do not describe
or comment on the HOF-factors at all. Relevant research projects have been conducted to fulfill the
requirements of Level 3 analyses. At this level, there is a systematic collection of data related to HOF.
The methods are systematic and documented, and the QRAs are adjusted. None of the QRAs fulfill the
Level 4 requirements. Level 4 QRAs include the model and describe the HOF-factors as well as explain
how the results should be followed up in the overall risk management. Safety audits by regulatory
authorities are probably necessary to point out the direction for QRA and speed up the development.

& 2011 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
1. Introduction

Authorities are basing their regulations and operators are
basing their designs on the use of Quantitative Risk Analyses
(QRAs) as a tool to determine which safety barriers are needed, as
well as what should be the dimensioning loads and requirements.
QRA processes have been used by the offshore industry for more
than three decades. QRAs have traditionally been focused on
technical systems and capabilities. Much less attention has been
focused on the human and organizational factors (HOFs). The
knowledge about HOFs started within the field of ergonomics; the
art and science of interfacing people with the systems that they
design, construct, operate and maintain. This approach is funda-
mentally one that focuses on a proactive reduction in the like-
lihoods of malfunctions [1]. During the past decade much
research effort has been aimed to reveal, isolate and measure/
predict human and organizational factors and their influence
on risk.
cticable; BORA, Barrier and
FPSO, Floating Production
and Health and Safety
nizational Factor; HRA,
uthority [Norway]; QRA,

(J.E. Skogdalen),

ll rights reserved.
During the period between 1970 and about 2000, there was an
extensive new building activity in the North Sea. Since 2000, the
new buildings have reduced to one, or possibly a couple of new
production facilities per year in the entire North Sea. Currently,
operations, maintenance and modification of existing facilities are
the main focus in the North Sea, and decommissioning will also
soon be a main activity. In addition, the trend towards more
extensive use of floating production systems, operations in the
arctic and deepwater suggests that operational aspects of safety
will be more important in the future, in order to mitigate hazards
and control risks. Revealing the HOFs are therefore of great
importance to the oil and gas industry.

This article has three objectives. The first objective is to study
in what way the legislation related to offshore activities on the
Norwegian shelf is influencing QRAs’ development. Special atten-
tion is given to reveal if and how requirements considering
human and organizational factors are included in regulations.
Secondly, a set of QRAs is investigated to see in what way the
legislation is reflected, and if and how the human and organiza-
tional factors are included. Thirdly, a classification of different
levels for the integration of human and organizational factors in
QRAs is defined.

The research method used is almost in reverse fashion from
traditional research, and may at first appear to contradict the
scientific method. Rather than beginning by researching and
developing a hypothesis, the first step was data collection in the
form of gathering QRAs. From the data collected, the key points
were extracted and marked with a series of codes related to
human and organizational factors. The codes were then grouped



J.E. Skogdalen, J.E. Vinnem / Reliability Engineering and System Safety 96 (2011) 468–479 469
into similar concepts. From these concepts, categories in form of
levels of QRAs were defined.

Several professionals have made the following comment in
regard to this article, that ‘‘QRAs do not include human and
organizational factors. Case closed!’’ However, there is little
research to prove the validity of this statement. The field of QRAs
is under constant development, and everyday hundreds of highly
skilled people are doing their best to ensure further development
among regulators, operators and consultancy firms. This article is
a contribution to establish the present state of QRA.

This article does not include an explanation of how QRAs
are performed. For literature regarding this matter, refer to
Refs. [2–4]. Legislation related to the Norwegian continental shelf
form the basis for this analysis. In addition, regulations related to
the British shelf are briefly described, owing to the close relation-
ship between the Norwegian and British shelves. The data, (QRAs)
is mainly from the Norwegian Continental Shelf. The scientific
area of human and organizational factors is enormous and not
settled. This article focuses on how human and organizational
factors are reflected to control and avoid major accidents. ‘Major
accident’ in the offshore industry is often understood to be an
accident sequence that is out of control and that has the potential
to cause five fatalities or more. This may be, for instance, gas leaks
where ignition has occurred or, in the case of structural failure,
where at least local structural failure has occurred. This inter-
pretation is in accordance with HSE’s definition [5]. Hazards in the
QRA, which can potentially cause major accidents are: process
leaks, blowouts, riser and pipeline accidents, structural collapse,
loss of stability/position and riser accidents. Helicopter accidents
are also major accidents, and they are not included in this article.

The history of the QRA as part of safety engineering is shortly
described in section 2. It is important to understand the frame-
work that QRA has been and is being developed under. Human
and organizational factors and how they interact in the risk
analysis process, are described. Thereafter the legislation, guide-
lines and official documents from HSE and the Norwegian
Petroleum Safety Authority are reviewed and presented. The
results from the analyses of QRAs and HOFs are described in
section 3. The discussion and conclusion follows thereafter.
2. Theory

2.1. QRA as part of safety engineering

QRA is used as the abbreviation for ‘Quantified Risk Assess-
ment’ or ‘Quantitative Risk Analysis’. The context usually has to
be considered in order to determine which of these two terms is
applicable. Risk assessment involves risk analysis, as well as an
evaluation of the results. QRA is applied frequently to offshore
operations. This technique is also referred to as Probabilistic Risk
Assessment (PRA), Probabilistic Safety Assessment (PSA), Concept
Safety Evaluation (CSE) and Total Risk Analysis (TRA). Despite
more than two decades of use and development, no convergence
towards a universally accepted term has been seen. QRA and TRA
are the most commonly used abbreviations [2]. In this article the
term QRA refers to all the different techniques.

The status of the QRAs is very much a part of the development
of safety regimes in the offshore industry. Oil-drilling on the
Norwegian continental shelf began in the summer of 1966. The
first drilling rigs were American, as were the drilling equipment,
the expertise, the language and the culture. The Norwegian
workers referred jokingly to certain rigs as ‘Texas’. Key features
of the culture found on board these rigs were a frenetic pace of
work, a high degree of risk-taking and numerous accidents.
Workers were constantly reminded of accidents, either because
they were frequent or through stories, myths and symbols.
Accident rates approached 70 accidents per 1,000 rig days. Since
many accidents were never reported, the actual figure was most
likely higher [6]. The first major accident was an explosion and
short duration fire on the Ekofisk Alpha platform, causing three
fatalities during evacuation. The second major accident was a
blowout that occurred on April 23rd, 1977, on the steel jacket
wellhead platform Ekofisk Bravo, during a workover on a produc-
tion well. The BOP was not in place on the platform, and could not
be reassembled correctly in time. All personnel were evacuated
without injuries by means of evacuation capsules and one dinghy,
and were subsequently rescued by a supply vessel. The well was
mechanically capped 7 days after the blowout [2]. The ‘Texas
Period’ culminated with the catastrophe that occurred on March
27th 1980. The accommodation platform Alexander L. Kielland
capsized on the Ekofisk field in the Norwegian sector of the North
Sea, killing 123 oil workers [7]. The disaster came as a shock and
sparked off many initiatives in order to prevent a similar
catastrophe from reoccurring. An extensive program (‘Safety Off-
shore’) was initiated in 1978 in order to implement the Norwe-
gian culture, work approach and worker protection on the
Norwegian continental shelf. The Government followed-up with
a series of directives, with requirements for education, training,
safety courses and other safety measures. QRAs played an
important role in the requirements, and in 1980, the Norwegian
Petroleum Directorate issued its Guidelines for safety evaluation
of platform conceptual design [8]. These guidelines required that
QRA should be carried out for all new offshore installations in
Norway in the conceptual design phase. After an initial period, an
efficient methodology was established, and subsequently
extended to the analysis of existing installations. A significant
step in the development of offshore use of QRAs resulted from the
official inquiry led by Lord Cullen in the UK, following the tragic
accident on the Piper Alpha platform in 1988. Lord Cullen in his
report, recommended QRAs to be implemented into the UK
legislation in much the same way as had been done in Norway
nearly 10 years earlier. In 1991, the Norwegian Petroleum
Directorate replaced the 1981 guidelines for risk assessment with
Regulations for the Use and Execution of Risk Analysis that
formally, though less so in practice, extended the scope consider-
ably for these studies. Since 1992, the Safety Case Legislation in
the UK has required, although not formally but in practice, that
the industry use offshore risk analyses as part of the safety cases
for existing and new installations. From mid 1995, the safety
cases have been extended with the assessment of provisions for
Prevention of Fire and Explosion and Emergency Response
(PFEER), according to the so-called PFEER regulations [9]. These
have, over time, become a small core group of international
standards, of the International Organization for Standardization
(ISO), reflecting a risk-based approach to decision-making in the
offshore industry (see Refs. [10–13]).

An important role of the QRAs was to improve the incorpora-
tion of safety in design, owing to the fact that a high number of
accidents had their root in the design process. During the first
decade, the focus was mainly on the engineering phases, thus
after the installation type and concept had been decided. QRAs
were not commonly used during the initial choice of the high-
level concept for fulfilling the system objectives. When designing
oil and gas installations, it made no sense for the contractor
responsible for the detailed design to question the high-level
choices that led the operator to define the need for the specified
installation, rather than another type of installation or other
design capacities. Another issue that followed defining the
boundaries for design was the inclusion of the operation phase
where humans and organizational factors played an even more
important role [14]. The QRAs were updated due to several



470 J.E. Skogdalen, J.E. Vinnem / Reliability Engineering and System Safety 96 (2011) 468–479
factors, for instance: experience, modifications, model improve-
ments, changes in criteria, operational mode, manning level and
maintenance philosophy. Safety systems were usually addressed
on an individual basis that did not allow dependencies and
common mode/cause failures to be identified. For instance,
release statistics in the 1990s showed that half of the leaks from
hydrocarbon systems on the Norwegian Continental Shelf were
caused by manual interventions in the system [4,15]. Engineered
defenses were often partially deactivated during these operations,
in order to avoid disruption of stable production. These occur-
rences indicated that safety barriers relating to containment of
leaks did not function sufficiently. There is an obvious need to
better understand the performance of barriers (both technical and
non-technical) during operations [4].

According to Bea [16], experience has shown that the primary
hazard is not the ocean environment itself; the industry has
learned how to engineer, build, operate and maintain structures
that can survive the extreme storms, earthquakes, ice and sea
floor soil movements that frequent the offshore environment. The
primary hazard is associated with human and organizational
factors (HOFs) that develop during installations lifecycles. Studies
have shown that while the majority of the structure failures occur
during operations and maintenance, a majority of these failures
have sources that are founded during the design phase. Structures
are engineered and designed to have inherent flaws; they cannot
be built as intended and they are difficult to operate and
maintain. While they may have the requisite strength or capacity,
they lack the required durability, serviceability and compatibility
[17]. The design and operation phases of a structure are carried
out by two separate organizations. Decisions made in the design
phase influence, and should therefore be reflected in the later
phases where HOFs are largely influencing the major hazards. The
need for understanding the human and organizational factors
(HOF) were also pointed out early by the HSE [18].

‘‘Management of human factors is increasingly recognized as
having a vital role to play in the control of risk y. Successful
management of human factors and control of risk involves the
development of systems designed to take proper account of
human capabilities and fallibilities y. It is now widely accepted
that the majority of accidents in industry generally are in some
way attributable to human as well as technical factors in the
sense that actions by people initiated or contributed to accidents,
or people could have acted better to avert them’’.

Major hazard prevention, the use of QRAs and revelation of
HOFs are important elements of risk management.
2.2. Human and organizational factors

It is not the intent of this article to attempt to determine a final
definition for human and organizational factors. However, it is
important to ensure that some understanding of how the expressions
are understood by the authors is mentioned. There are three areas of
influence on people at work, namely: (a) the organization, (b) the job
(c) personal factors. These are directly affected by: (a) the system for
communication within the organization and (b) the training systems
and procedures in operation; all of which are directed at preventing
human error [19]. Human factors are understood as the branch of
science and technology, which includes what is known and theorized
about human behavioral and biological characteristics that can be
applied validly to the specification, design, evaluation, operation and
maintenance of products and systems to enhance safe, effective and
satisfying use by individuals, groups and organizations [20]. Human
factors are seen as a range of issues, including the perceptual, physical
and mental capabilities of people, as well as the interactions of
individuals with their jobs and the working environments, the
influence of equipment and system design on human performance
and, above all, the organizational characteristics that influence safety-
related behavior at work. The terms ‘human factors’ and ‘human
error’ are often used interchangeably, but, as pointed out by Gordon
[21], it is important to distinguish between the underlying causes of
accidents (human factors) and their immediate causes (human
errors). Traditionally, human factors are defined as the interaction
between man and machine, although many variations exist (see Ref.
[22]). Human error can be defined as: ‘‘the failure of planned actions
to achieve their desired ends—without the intervention of some
unforeseeable event’’ [23]. According to Jacobs and Haber [24], human
errors may be of various origins and part of larger, organizational
processes that encourage unsafe acts, which ultimately produce
system failures.

Organizational factors are characterized by the division of
tasks, design of job positions, including selection, training and
cultural indoctrination, and their coordination to accomplish the
activities. The main issues of organization and safety include
factors such as complexity (chemical/process, physical, control
and task), size and age of plant and organizational safety perfor-
mance shaping factors such as leadership, culture, rewards,
manning, communications and coordination and social norms
and pressures [25]. The term operational risk is most commonly
found in risk management programs of financial institutions,
which must organize their risk management program according
to Basel II [26]. Operational risk is then defined as: ‘‘The risk of loss
resulting from inadequate or failed internal processes, people and
systems or from external events’’. Practically this can be described
as a company’s acceptance of the fact that its people, processes
and systems are imperfect, and that losses will arise from errors
and ineffective operations. The losses the companies are prepared
to accept, because the cost of correcting the errors or improving
the systems is disproportionate to the benefit they will receive,
determines their hunger for operational risk.

The term ‘operational risk’ has, within safety engineering, been
linked mostly to the complexity and framework for processes and
systems, and is often overlapped by the definitions for human and
organizational factors. Within safety engineering is acceptance of
operational risk not been pursued in the same way as within
finance. Operations are performed by humans as part of an
organization. They can all be described by factors. In this article,
the operational factors are not distinguished.

To predict human reliability in the QRA processes, Human
Reliability Analysis (HRA) is often used. The following list of high-
level requirements captures the essence of HRA. The HRA method
should [27]:

 identify human response (errors are the main focus) in the
QRA context,


 estimate response probabilities,


 identify causes of errors to support development of preventive

or mitigating measures,


 include a systematic procedure for generating reproducible

qualitative and quantitative results,


 have a causal model of human response with roots in cognitive

and behavioral sciences, and with:
J elements (e.g. Personal Shaping Factors) that are directly or

indirectly observable, and
J a structure that provides unambiguous and traceable links

between its input and output


 be detailed enough to support data collection, experimental

validation and various applications of QRA. Data and model are
two tightly coupled entities.

The model should be data-informed. Conversely, the data
collocation and analysis must be model-informed. A coordinated



J.E. Skogdalen, J.E. Vinnem / Reliability Engineering and System Safety 96 (2011) 468–479 471
model-based collection and analysis of experimental and filed data
should support the development and application of the model and
the quantification of its parameters [27].

There have also been parallel efforts to develop methods for
the formal inclusion of human and organizational factors into
QRA. Examples from the nuclear and airline industry includes
Model of Accident Causation using Hierarchical Influence Net-
work (MACHINE) [28], the Work Process Analysis Model (WPAM)
[29,30], System-Action-Management (SAM) [31], Omega Factor
Model [32], I-RISK [33,34], Integrated Safety Model (ISM) [35],
and Causal Modeling of Air Safety [36]. With respect to QRA in the
offshore industry, Organizational Risk Influence Model (ORIM)
[37], Barrier and Operational Risk Analysis (BORA) [38,39] and
Operational Conditional Safety (OTS) [4,15] are relevant. Common
among these are the following parts:
(1)
 a set of organizational factors,

(2)
 a link to the system risk model,

(3)
 a set of modeling techniques,

(4)
 a set of measurement methods.
For example, SAM describes the organization using a set of
theories: a rule-based model for rule-based decisions, a rational
and a bounded-rational model for knowledge-based decisions,
and a model related to the actual execution for skill-based
actions. The selection of relevant organizational factors depends
on which of the explicit models can best describe a given decision.
The existing frameworks and methods have some similar steps
and features, but they also have many unique features. The key
questions in this line of research can be summarized thus:
(1)
 What are the organizational factors that affect risk?

(2)
 How do these factors influence risk?

(3)
 How much do they contribute to risk?
Each method answers these questions in different ways [40].
Many proposed methods still lack sufficient theoretical and/or
experimental basis for their key ingredients. Missing from all
methods is a fully implemented model of the underlying causal
mechanisms linking measurable Personal Shaping Factors (PSF) or
other characteristics of the context of operator response. The
problem extends to the quantification of HOFs where the majority
of the proposed approaches still rely on implicit functions relating
PSFs to probabilities. Again, often only limited theoretical or
empirical bases are provided for such relations. In many cases
numbers are the result of expert elicitation using arbitrary
scales [27].

Several of the methods use variations of the Bayesian Belief
Network (BBN) or the Influence Diagram. WPAM utilizes flow
diagrams to relate work processes and organizational factors.
Measuring and estimating model elements in organizational
safety models pose significant challenges. Some of the proposed
safety causal models have relied exclusively on expert judgment
for measurement. WPAM suggests surveys, behavioral checklists
and interviews, in addition to expert judgment. These measure-
ment methods were suggested by Jacobs and Haber [24] in their
list of twenty organizational factors developed for the nuclear
power industry [40]. Others have used a combination of expert
and historical data (e.g. BORA).

Defining and modeling safety barriers is central when analyz-
ing the influence of human and organizational factors. The
concept of safety barriers is applied in practice, discussed in the
literature and required in legislation and standards within differ-
ent industries. Safety barriers are physical or non-physical means
planned to prevent, control or mitigate undesired events or
accidents. The means may range from a single technical unit or
human action to a complex socio-technical system. The term
‘planned’ implies that at least one of the purposes of the means is
to reduce the risk. In line with ISO:13702 [11], the term ‘preven-
tion’ means reduction of the likelihood of a hazardous event, and
‘control’ means limiting the extent and/or duration of a hazardous
event to prevent escalation, while ‘mitigation’ means reduction of
the effects of a hazardous event. Undesired events are, e.g.,
technical failures, human errors, external events or a combination
of these occurrences that may realize potential hazards. Accidents
are undesired and unplanned events that lead to loss of human
lives, personal injuries, environmental damage and/or material
damage [41].

Barrier systems may be classified according to several dimen-
sions, for example, as passive or active barrier systems, and as
physical, technical or human/operational barrier systems. Several
attributes are necessary in order to characterize the performance
of safety barriers; functionality/effectiveness, reliability/availabil-
ity, response time, robustness and finally a description of the
triggering event or condition. For some types of barriers, not all
the attributes are relevant or necessary in order to describe the
barrier performance [41].

The terminology proposed by a working group named ‘Work-
ing together for safety’ (see Ref. [41]) involves the levels, barrier
function, barrier element (or system) and the barrier influencing
factor. The following definitions are extracted from Sklet [41,42]:

 Barrier function: A function planned to prevent, control or
mitigate undesired events or accidents.


 Barrier element: Part of barrier, but not sufficient in order to

achieve the required overall function alone.


 Barrier influencing factor: Factor that influences the perfor-

mance of barriers.

QRAs studies for the offshore petroleum industry have tradi-
tionally had a rather narrow analysis of barrier performance. The
nuclear industry on the other hand, has carried out extensive
studies of barrier performance [4,15,38,39].

2.3. Human and organizational factors and major accidents

For a long time, it was assumed that occupational accidents
were a relevant basis on which major hazard risks were judged.
Occupational accidents were often judged to be a result of human
error. In the late 1990s, in the Norwegian petroleum sector, it was
apparent that occupational accidents were not relevant for
evaluation of major hazard risks, at least to the authorities and
their advisors. Unfortunately this was not apparent globally. One
of the main findings after the Texas City refinery explosion in
2005, was that major hazard risks had not been evaluated
properly on the basis of occupational accident statistics [43]. In
the same way, human error was judged to be the root cause for
several major accidents, for example, in the case of the accident at
Esso’s gas plant at Longford, Victoria, in Australia in September
1998 [44]. This was the position taken by Esso at the Royal
Commission. The company argued that operators and their super-
visors on duty at the time should have known that the attempt to
reintroduce warm hydrocarbons in liquid phase could result in
brittle fracture of the steel and release of hydrocarbons. The
company claimed that operators had been trained to be aware of
the problem and Esso even produced the training records of one
operator in an attempt to show that he should have known better.
However, the Commission took view of the fact that none of those
on duty at the time understood just how dangerous the situation
was, indicating a systematic training failure [45]. Not even the
plant manager, who was away from the plant at the time of the



472 J.E. Skogdalen, J.E. Vinnem / Reliability Engineering and System Safety 96 (2011) 468–479
incident, understood the dangers of cold metal embrittlement.
The Commission concluded that inadequate training of operators
and supervisors was the ‘real cause’ of the accident. Therefore, it
is clear that operator error does not adequately account for the
Longford incident. According to Reason [46], this is a general
finding of all inquiries into major accidents. For the Norwegian
offshore installations, manual intervention related to mainte-
nance is one of the main contributors to major accident risk.
When accident and incident investigations are considered, it is
often the case that human and organizational errors dominate.
Consider, for instance, the 2004 Snorre Alpha subsea gas blowout
in the Norwegian sector of the North Sea. The main categories of
deviations that contributed to the accident were: failure to follow
steering documentation, lack of appreciation of and execution of
risk assessments, inadequate management involvement and
requirements for well barriers not fulfilled [47]. There is a need
to analyze human and organizational factors in a structured way
where these factors are not only seen as a cause but also as
barriers to avoid major accidents.

2.4. Norwegian legislation

The Norwegians regulations cover the field of health, environ-
ment and safety (HES), but note that this field encompasses
safety, working environment, health, external environment and
financial assets; the latter includes production and transport
regularity (operational availability). The regulation is divided into
five categories:

 Framework regulations


 Management [of health, environment and safety] regulations


 Information duty regulations


 Facilities regulations


 Activity regulations

The regulations attach much importance to risk reduction
principles related to health, environment and safety, as means
for reducing the risk of accident, personal injury, health injury
and harm to the environment to the greatest degree possible. The
structure of the regulations reflects the fact that the requirements
are largely common or multidisciplinary and hence is not
repeated. The requirements related to safety barriers are an
example of the multidisciplinary approach. The provisions of the
regulations are largely formulated as functional requirements.
The functional requirements impose requirements to various
aspects, characteristics or qualities that the product, process or
service in question is intended to have. The particular require-
ment expresses what result the product, process or service is
to produce, and what the supervisory authorities wish to
achieve with the requirement. The guidelines to the respective
regulations recommend solutions, inter alia in the form of
industry standards (recognized norms) as a means of fulfilling
the requirements contained in the regulations. If a recommended
solution is opted for, the requirement is considered to be
fulfilled [48].

In the Facilities regulations (especially Section 9), human and
organizational factors are focused upon. It states that plants,
systems and equipment plants shall have a design that is robust
and as simple as possible, so that the possibility of human error or
mistake is limited. The guidelines comment that the principles
relating to risk reduction, harm or danger of harm to people, the
environment or to financial assets shall be prevented or limited,
in accordance with the legislation relating to health, the environ-
ment and safety, including internal requirements and acceptance
criteria. Over and above this level, the risk shall be further
reduced to the extent possible. Assessments on the basis of this
provision shall be made in all phases of the petroleum activities.
In effectuating risk reduction, the party responsible shall choose
the technical, operational or organizational solutions which,
according to an individual as well as an overall evaluation of
the potential harm and present and future use, offer the best
results, provided the associated costs are not significantly dis-
proportionate to the risk reduction achieved [2,49].

In the Norwegian offshore legislation, barriers play an essen-
tial role in the prevention and mitigation of incidents and
accidents. In the facilities regulations, it is stated that the operator
or the party responsible for the operation of a facility, shall
stipulate the strategies and principles on which the design, use
and maintenance of barriers shall be based, so that the barrier
function is ensured throughout the lifetime of the facility. It shall
be known what barriers have been established, and for which
function they are intended in relation to risk reduction. It shall be
known what performance requirements have been defined in
respect of the technical, operational or organizational elements,
and which are necessary for the individual barrier to be effective.
It shall be known which barriers are not functioning or have been
impaired. The party responsible shall take necessary actions to
correct or compensate for missing or impaired barriers [50]. For
more information about barriers see Refs. [41,51].

The Management regulation, Section 14, states that quantita-
tive risk analyses and other necessary analyses shall be carried
out to identify contributors to major accident risk. Section 15
requires that quantitative risk analyses that provide a balanced
and as comprehensive picture as possible of the risk, shall be
carried out. Section 6 requires that the operator shall set accep-
tance criteria for major accident risk, and that this should include
acceptance criteria for personnel on the facility as a whole, and
for groups of personnel, which are particularly risk-exposed. The
Management regulations focus also on the working environment
and requires, in Section 17, that necessary analyses shall be
carried out, which will ensure a sound working environment,
and provide decision support in the choice of technical, opera-
tional and organizational solutions. The analyses shall, inter alia,
contribute to improving the health, well-being and security
of the employees, and prevent personal injury, death and work-
related disease as a result of: (a) mistakes that may lead to
situations of hazard and accident and (b) exposure and physical or
mental strain [50]. Attention is also given to the QRA follow-up,
and in Section 21 it is stated that the party responsible shall
follow-up to see that all elements of their and other participants’
management system are established and functioning as intended,
and that a fully satisfactory level of health, environment and
safety exists. This follow-up shall contribute to identifying tech-
nical, operational or organizational weaknesses, failures and
deficiencies. Methods, frequency and extent of the follow-up
and the degree of independence in conducting it, will be adapted
to the significance of the element to health, environment and
safety [50].
2.5. UK regulation

The UK’s Offshore Safety Case Regulations came into force in
1992. A ‘Safety Case’ is a written document within which the
company must demonstrate that an effective management sys-
tem is in place to control risks to workers and, in particular, to
reduce the risks from a major accident to ‘As Low As Reasonably
Practicable’ (ALARP) [52]. The duty holder (owner or operator) of
every offshore installation operating in British waters is required
to prepare a Safety Case and submit it to the UK HSE Offshore
Safety Division for formal acceptance. The main thrust of a Safety



J.E. Skogdalen, J.E. Vinnem / Reliability Engineering and System Safety 96 (2011) 468–479 473
Case is a demonstration by the installation operator that the risks
to the installation from Major Accident Hazards have been
reduced to ALARP. Traditionally, the offshore industry has found
it difficult to integrate human and organizational factors into the
Safety Cases. Although there is a requirement to address human
factor issues, the guidance has been unclear on how this should
be achieved. A variety of tools and techniques have been initiated
by a legislative focus, which are currently in use to varying
degrees by different operators. There are two sections within
Safety Cases, which are of high importance, the Safety Manage-
ment System and Risk Assessment sections. Within these sec-
tions, are a number of factors that should be addressed in order to
meet the legislative requirements of the Safety Case [20]. Human
and organizational factors are among those to be addressed, and
in Principle 1 it is stated that: ‘‘The safety case should demonstrate
that the management system is adequate to ensure compliance with
the relevant statutory provisions’’. A safety case that does not
include the following elements in the descriptions of the manage-
ment system is unlikely to demonstrate that the system is
adequate[53]: organizational structure, accountability and safety
culture, professional health and safety advice, involvement of the
workforce and risk assessment systems.

The HSE clearly states that many forms of organizational
change can affect management of major hazards. Changes could
include, changes to roles and responsibilities, organizational
structure, staffing levels, staff disposition or any other change
that could directly or indirectly affect the control of the hazard
[18]. HSE also states the link between QRAs and the human and
organizational factors by requiring in Principle 8 that, where the
lines of defense against major hazards and escalation are pre-
sented, the role of the human element should be made clear, and
a demonstration provided, so that the lines of defenses can be
delivered reliably when required. The HSE expects that assess-
ments would progress through the following stages to provide an
appropriate demonstration [54]: Qualitative (Q), in which
frequency and severity are determined purely qualitatively,
Semi-quantitative (SQ), in which frequency and severity are
approximately quantified within ranges and/or Quantified risk
assessment (QRA), in which full quantification occurs.

For a new installation, the greatest scope to eliminate or
minimize hazards is at the design stage. The design process
should address all contributors to risk from major accident
hazards, with emphasis on the most significant risks. Risks should
be reduced to ensure compliance with the relevant statutory
provisions, primarily through sound engineering design, sup-
ported by appropriate management controls and human factor
assessment [52]. Human performance problems should be eval-
uated systematically. This should involve evaluating the feasibil-
ity of tasks, identifying control measures and providing an input
to the design of procedures and personnel training, as well as the
interfaces between personnel and the plant. The depth of analysis
should be appropriate to the severity of the consequences of
failure of the tasks [52,54].
2.6. Examples of HOF and safety barriers

If a certain hazard mechanism is dominated by HOFs, a
detailed analysis of operational barriers should be performed in
the design phases. One practical example where this was done is
related to collision risk between shuttle tanker (ST) and FPSO in
relation to offloading. The collision scenario most feared, is called
‘drive-off’. This implies a powered yet uncontrolled forward
movement of the shuttle tanker causing a collision. Usually the
shuttle tanker uses dynamic positioning (DP) for station keeping
and the FPSO has a fixed 12–16 point anchoring system and
ability to weather wane (rotate) 3601 unassisted. The DP system is
a fully automated system. The system receives input usually from
two or three positioning (reference) systems and thrust measure-
ments from each propeller/thruster and speed measurements.
There are also warning and alarm limits associated with auto-
matic actions such as controlled disconnection of the hose before
the hose is torn off. Active human intervention is not required
during normal operations. The role of the DP operator is mostly
passive and involves monitoring and fine adjustment tasks. If the
DP system fails and cause a drive-off and a possible collision, the
tasks for the DP operator changes dramatically. The intervention
by the DP operator on the bridge is the only remaining option to
control the situation. This implies that when alarms activate in
response to a failure, the control response is completely depen-
dent on the DP operator. The DP operator has a very limited time-
span (in the order of 30–45 s) for completion of an emergency
response. The drive-off scenario is particularly critical, due to the
movement of the shuttle tanker towards the FPSO. The fact that
the shuttle tanker often will accelerate towards the FPSO implies
that it increases the impact speed of a collision. Hence, a quick
response is needed. When these aspects were analyzed during the
design phase of new systems from around the year 2000, the
frequency of incidents and accidents was significantly reduced
from several per year in the North Sea in the late 1990s, to less
than once every three years, in the 2000s. For more details see
Ref. [55].

Another example of the importance of better understanding
the HOFs as part of the QRA-modeling is the preliminary findings
pertaining to failures of safety barriers in the Deepwater Horizon
accident. There are several initiatives to investigate the accident,
and what they all have in common, is the fact that they do not
point to a single failure, but multiple violations of safety barriers.
During the time this article was written, the official investigation
by the Presidential Commission was not completed. Summarized
are some of the findings to date [56,57]:

 The annulus cement barrier failed to isolate the hydrocarbons


 The shoe track barriers possibly failed to isolate the

hydrocarbons


 The negative-pressure test was accepted although well integ-

rity had not been established


 Influx was not recognized until hydrocarbons were in the riser


 Well control response actions failed to regain control of

the well


 Diversion to the mud gas separator resulted in gas venting

onto the rig


 The fire and gas system did not prevent hydrocarbon ignition


 The BOP emergency mode did not seal the well

It can be seen that the failure of several safety barriers in the
Deepwater Horizon accident was based on human intervention.
The Deepwater Horizon accident has demonstrated a need for
further improvement related to modeling of risk related to deep-
water drilling as well as understanding HOFs.

Both UK and Norwegian regulations require that risk is
considered using QRA, and that safety barriers are analyzed and
included in the QRA. HOFs are essential parts of the safety
barriers, and several barriers are solely based on human inter-
vention. The safety barriers represent an essential part of the risk-
reducing measures during operations and maintenance. It is
therefore the authors’ view that it is not possible to fulfill the
requirements in the regulations without including HOF in the
QRAs. The HOFs should be analyzed in a structured manner, and
should also be as close to reality as practically possible.



474 J.E. Skogdalen, J.E. Vinnem / Reliability Engineering and System Safety 96 (2011) 468–479
3. Results

3.1. About the data (QRAs)

Altogether, fifteen QRAs for different installations were col-
lected from five different operator companies. The collection was
based upon a random sample among all installations on the
Norwegian Shelf. In addition, two safety cases for UK installations
were collected. The QRAs are internal company documents and
their results are not published. The document owner is often the
installation management, and the QRA is treated as sensitive
information owing to the detailed information about the installa-
tions’ weak spots. As a result, the information retrieval took close
to a year. A high response rate was emphasized, and finally, all
requested QRAs except one were collected. Table 1 shows the year
of last revision of report and the number of reports

In Norway, there are three dominant consultancy firms con-
ducting QRAs. The QRAs are usually regulated by framework
agreements. The budget in man-hours is usually about 1500–
2500 h over a period of 3–5 months. The main results are usually
summarized in a main report. In addition, the different hazards
are further described in appendices and sub-appendices. The
main report is usually about 50–150 pages. The appendices are
usually 500–1500 pages in length. The analyses use mostly the
same generic data sources, accident statistics, failure databases
(loss of containment etc.), equipment failure databases, physical
properties of various substances and company internal accident
and incident databases. The use and interpretation of the data
differs.

As described in the objective, the research method used for
this study was somewhat in reverse fashion from traditional
research. Based on the examination of the QRA, a set of terms
related to human and organizational factors were chosen. Pre-
defined terms or tools were also used to describe or calculate
human and organizational factors, including: human error,
human factor, Human Error Probability (HEP), Human Reliability
Assessment (HRA), organizational error, organizational factor,
culture, safety culture, safety climate, Risk influence factor (RIF),
Barrier and Operational Risk Analysis (BORA), and Human Error
Assessment and Reduction Technique (HEART). The QRAs were
categorized according to the level at which they included,
analyzed, described, quantified and explained human and orga-
nizational factors. As a result of the examination, the QRAs have
been categorized into four levels.
3.2. Level 1—‘‘HOF not existing’’

Level 1 analysis gives no attention to human and organiza-
tional factors. At this level the QRA is solely a technical analysis
consisting of technical inputs such as number of valves, flanges,
bends, instrument connections, risers, pipelines, pressure vessels,
number of supply vessel operations, number of crane operations,
etc. The generic data sources are not sorted or adjusted for any
human or organizational factors. Frequencies for initiating events
are calculated separately for each piece of equipment or system,
Table 1
Year of QRA and number of reports/installations.

QRA Year Installations

2008–2009 4
2006–2008 3
2004–2006 5
2002–2004 2
2000–2002 1
and each leak category is based either on system or equipment
values. The different inputs are calculated using fault-tree and/or
event-tree analysis. According to guidelines for QRA [58], the
following risk elements related to major hazard risk should as a
minimum be considered for offshore oil and gas installations:

 Blowouts, including shallow gas and reservoir zones, unignited
and ignited


 Process leaks, unignited and ignited.


 Utility areas and systems fires and explosions.


 Fire in accommodation areas.


 Falling/swinging objects.


 Transportation accidents.


 Transport of personnel from shore to the installation.


 Helicopter crash.


 Collisions, including fields related traffic and external traffic,

drifting and under power.


 Riser and pipeline accidents.


 Accidents from subsea production systems.


 Escape, evacuation and rescue accidents, i.e. until a so-called

‘safe place’ has been reached.


 Structural collapse, including collapse of bridges between fixed

and/or floating installations.


 Foundation failure.


 Loss of stability/position.

All suggested risk-reducing measures for Level 1 analyses are
related to technical solutions, for instance, passive fire protection,
riser bumper protection, etc. Consequences are expressed
numerically (e.g., the number of people potentially killed) and
their likelihood of occurrence are expressed as probabilities or
frequencies.

3.3. Level 2—‘‘HOF explained’’

The Level 2 analysis does explain the importance of the
human, operational and organizational factors. The explanations
are related to the technical analysis and the influence on the
system is only explained to a limited extent. Human error has, to
some extent, been considered implicitly. For instance, component
failure rate data used in the QRA reflects to some extent good and
bad design/operations for both old and new installations. Human
error as a cause of failure is partly embodied in the failure rate
data. One example is the failure database related to onshore oil
pipelines reported in the CONCAWE (CONservation of Clean Air
and Water in Europe) reports on ‘‘Performance of Oil Industry
Cross-Country Pipelines in Western Europe’’ [59]. In a Level 2
analysis the incidents are divided into mechanical failure and
operational error. However the result is not adjusted to reflect the
actual status for the installation for which the QRA is performed.

The Level 2 analyses also often include experience from
incident reporting systems. Examples of previous incidents and
their relation to major hazard risk are often shortly described. The
recommendations that prevent recurrence of the events leading
up to the incident can sometimes be addressed as assumptions.
The recommendations are often related to human and organiza-
tional factors.

Another example is the use of interviews and reviews related
to barriers, and the awareness the crew have to human interac-
tion as a barrier. One example is the barrier element that the crew
is discovering gas leaks. Using interviews the risk analysis team
tries to reveal to what extent this is likely based on earlier
incidents, routines, competence and training. The process opera-
tors are interviewed to reveal the response time for human
interaction in control room when a gas detection alarm sounds.



J.E. Skogdalen, J.E. Vinnem / Reliability Engineering and System Safety 96 (2011) 468–479 475
Interviews are also used to describe whether changes in the
workforce influence the risk. In one of the QRAs, the human
ergonomic is commented to not be satisfactory and may lead to
longer response time.

The Level 2 analyses do not follow a structured pattern related
to revealing and analyzing the human and organizational factors.
The specific results from the interviews, experience from earlier
incidents and/or document review are not included in the QRA-
models, but are described in text. The role of HOFs as possible
hazard causes as well as safety barriers, is explained.

For Level 2 analysis the study of HOFs appear often to be based
on a specific request from the customer (installation operator).
Therefore, the problems related to the working environment of
individual installations are often the basis for the HOFs selected.
An area of particular attention, unsurprisingly, is staffing.
3.4. Level 3—‘‘HOF explained and the models are adjusted’’

Level 3 analyses study the human and organizational factors in
a structured way and also adjust the QRA-models based on the
results from the studies. The Level 3 analyses are based on named
and documented methods for revealing and analyzing human and
organizational factors, for example BORA [4,15,38,39], Human
Error Probability (HEP), Human Reliability Assessment (HRA),
Human Error Assessment and Reduction Technique (HEART),
Human Error Probability Index (HEPI) [60]and Technique for
Human Error Rate Prediction (THERP). In some of the analyses
the methods are combined. In other just parts of a method are
used. One example is related to human operations in connection
with discovery, identification and rectification of plant malfunc-
tions in the control room and in the field. The risk analysis team
has, by means of interviews with control room operators and field
operators, sought to assess the operators’ confidence toward the
reliability of instrument readings in the Central Control Room
(CCR) when leaks occur. Also the possibility of a successful
intervention from the CCR and/or by field operators in the case
of a leak (oil or gas) in the process area on the platform is
assessed. Based on the interviews, the HOF-factors are revealed
and the QRA-models are adjusted. With respect to the example, it
is argued that the factors related to human error are conservative.
This is based on the interviews and experience from earlier
incidents, showing that field operators have intervened with
success on a number of occasions (gas or oil leaks). Thorough
knowledge and familiarity with each part of the plant the
operators had the necessary assuredness to prevent panicky
reactions that could have led to failed operations, and instead
controlled the incidents.

The HOFs that are studied in Level 3 analyses are related to but
limited to work practice, competence, communication, manage-
ment, procedures, safety management system, workload and
physical working environment and change management. Each of
these can again be divided into subgroups, e.g. competence,
training, education (both general and specific), courses, system
knowledge, etc. The methods used to study the HOFs are inter-
views, document review of procedures and safety management
system and review of earlier incidents.

HOFs in relation to gas detection are often studied in the Level
3 analysis. The steps that influence the risk were explained and
included among others maintenance, the operators’ ability and
training to detect and respond to leaks and the collaboration
between the operator and central control room. The capability to
isolate a leak locally and manually was often described. The
control room’s response to depressurize process systems was also
described.
In one of the QRAs were HOFs related to evacuation and escape
studied and formed basis for input to the QRA-model. In another
QRA were the HOFs in relation to stand-by vessel operations and
the hazards related to collision studied. The model included
various tasks of the crew members and the stressful surroundings
of the work environment.

The Barrier and Operational Risk Analysis is so far the most
documented methodology to include HOFs in offshore QRAs. The
BORA analyses the effect of safety barriers introduced to prevent
hydrocarbon releases. BORA-Release comprises the following
main steps [38,39,61]:
1.
 development of a basic risk model, including release scenarios,

2.
 modeling the performance of safety barriers,

3.
 assignment of industry average probabilities/frequencies and

risk quantification based on these probabilities/frequencies,

4.
 development of risk influence diagrams,

5.
 scoring of risk influencing factors,

6.
 weighting of risk influencing factors,

7.
 adjustment of industry average probabilities/frequencies,

8.
 recalculation of the risk in order to determine the platform-

specific risk related to hydrocarbon release.

The BORA project has investigated which barriers exist to
prevent initiating events from actually developing into a leak.
Many of these barriers are human and organizational rather than
technical. One example of such barriers is an error documented as
follows: ‘‘valve in wrong position after maintenance’’. Barriers
intended to prevent the valve in wrong position are self-checking
work tasks (i.e. the person performing the work should check the
valves when work is accomplished). An independent person
should then check the valves and carry out a leak test. These
barriers are described and modeled in the BORA project. The
project do also describe which barriers must fail in order for a
leak to occur. A wide range of platform-specific conditions are
included in the methodology, e.g., employee competence, working
environment issues, accessibility and arid quality of documenta-
tion and procedures. By evaluating the state of such factors
mentioned on the installation in question, the factors can be
considered when the leak frequency is set.

3.5. Level 4—‘‘HOF explained, models adjusted and included in the
overall risk management’’

Guidelines, requirements and research form the basis for
Level 4 QRA analysis. Level 4 analysis includes the same struc-
tured approach to reveal, model and calculate the human and
organizational factors as the Level 3 analysis, with the addition
that at this level the human and organizational factors are just as
important as the technical ones. HOFs form the basis for the
preparations, analysis and interpretation of the results in the QRA.
The safety barriers are defined and explained with regards to how
the different HOFs influence. The status of the technical factors
and the HOFs is controlled by the use of risk indicators. The QRA is
updated based on the indicators and other means to evaluate the
status for the technical factors and HOFs.

Level 4 analyses are a central part of the company’s safety and
risk management system. The QRA is thereby a part of the
decision-making process where the definition of goals and objec-
tives are key elements. The decision problem is characterized by
expected losses and gains and uncertainties. Risk management is
managed according to the principles for ALARP. The QRA form the
basis for training and drills. The QRA is known and accepted at all
levels of the organization and form the basis for the daily risk
management.



476 J.E. Skogdalen, J.E. Vinnem / Reliability Engineering and System Safety 96 (2011) 468–479

Table 2
Level and requirements.

Level Requirements

Level 4  The QRA is an integrated part of the safety and risk management system.
 Results from the QRA form the basis for daily risk management.
 The QRA is known and accepted at all levels of the organization.
 QRA is combined with risk indicators to reveal the status of the safety barriers.

Level 3  Systematic collection of data is related to HOF.
 QRA-models are adjusted according to findings from HOF.
 Identifies causes of errors to support development of preventive or mitigating measures.
 Includes a systematic procedure for generating reproducible qualitative and quantitative results (BORA, OTS y)

Level 2  Explains the importance of HOF
 The HOF-factors’ influence on different part of the system are partly described
 Human error is calculated separately
 Interviews with parts of the crew. The results are revealed but the models and calculation are not adjusted.

Level 1  Analysis of technical and operational factors. Technical factors are valves, flanges, bends, instrument connections, water depth, pressure,
hydrocarbon composition. Operational factors are number of flights, number of shipping arrivals etc.
 Risk-reducing measures are technical; for example, passive fire protection and riser bumper protection. They can also be operational, like fewer

shipping arrivals.

Result of the QRAs

Table 3
Level and number of installations.

Level Number of
installations

1—None existing 5
2—Explained but not adjusted 8
3—Explained and the models are adjusted 2
4—Explained, models adjusted and included in the overall 0

risk management
Necessary sensitivity calculations and evaluations of uncer-
tainties are done as part of the QRA. The QRA also forms the basis
for easy updates based on revised input. The risk analysis team
and the management work close together to ensure that the
objectives with the QRA are in accordance with long-term goals
for safety, and can be used as important input to the strategy for
attaining the goals.

The requirements related to the different levels are summar-
ized in Table 2.

Table 3 shows the different levels of QRA analysis defined in
this article, including the number of installations containing each
level of analysis.

Not surprisingly do none of analyses fulfill the requirements
for a Level 4 analysis. This level is the authors’ interpretation of
the intentions given in guidelines, requirements and research
described earlier in the article.
4. Discussions

QRA methodology should focus on establishing good princi-
ples, methods and models for analyzing and describing risk. The
use of risk analysis in risk assessment should focus on establish-
ing good principles, methods and models for evaluating risk,
communicating risk and managing risk. The question remains,
‘‘how good is good enough’’? It is clearly not sufficient to simply
refer to the fulfillment of requirements set by various standards.
One needs to go one step further and ask what the basis is for
establishing the standard. We share the belief with Aven [62] that
there are some principles that should be fulfilled; (a) The analysis
team should have a thorough understanding of the system
performance and the decision-making process and context.
(b) Models used should have sufficiently accurate representations
of the world, their suitability in describing the world have been
evaluated. (c) All observable quantities of the analysis should be
precisely defined. (d) The meanings of risk and uncertainty should
be fully understood and treated consistently. (e) The background
information for the assessments should be documented.

We cannot verify if the analysis team has a thorough under-
standing of the system performance and the decision-making
process and context, but it is possible to reveal if they have tried
to collect the necessary information. As seen, there are major
differences between the different levels of QRA. The situation is
similar in relation to the models, if they are sufficiently accurate
representations of the world. We have evaluated how detailed the
models are in relation to HOFs. Performing a QRA is extensive
work, and it can take years before revisions are made. The QRA
can be a valuable basis for risk control, as well as a risk manage-
ment strategy. Safety and risk indicators are an important part of
the follow-up process of a QRA, and the same criteria are relevant
for both QRAs and risk indicators. Risk indicators must be:
observable and quantifiable, sensitive to change, transparent
and easily understood, robust against manipulation and valid
[63]. The reliability and validity of risk analysis have been
discussed previously by Aven and Heide [64]. It is our under-
standing that incorporating the HOFs in QRAs is essential to fulfill
the legislation requirements. Incorporation of HOFs must be done
in accordance with the described principles for QRAs.

The need to consider HOF and the extent of the consideration
varies according to the objectives and the timing of the QRA
study. For example, if the QRA is used in order to define Design
Accidental Loads, then the focus is on design aspects, and the
need for HOF consideration is limited. Still it is important to
determine the likelihood of accidents, which needs to reflect the
HOF contributions to accident frequencies, at least on an overall
level. When the QRA is used in the operations phase in order to
propose measures that may reduce risk, it is essential to include
detailed modeling of HOF mechanisms and contributions, as a
basis for identification of possible risk-reducing measures,



J.E. Skogdalen, J.E. Vinnem / Reliability Engineering and System Safety 96 (2011) 468–479 477
including technical as well as operational measures. There may be
exceptions to this general principle.

The QRA as part of the total risk management system has been
described as an essential part of the Level 4 analysis, but this in
itself does not ensure minimization of the risk. At one end of the
spectrum, the most prudent strategies rely on a robust system
design, frequent preventive maintenance and early response to
warnings of deterioration. At the other end, strategies are driven
only by an aggressive production schedule. They rely on a less
robust (and often cheaper) system design and minimal inspection
and maintenance, in the hope of obtaining maximum production
at minimum initial cost with minimum interruptions in opera-
tions. Many risk management strategies are based on intermedi-
ate solutions such as having a robust system that would require
minimal maintenance. The difference, of course, lies in the long-
term costs, the actual frequency of operation interruptions and
the probability and costs of catastrophic failures [65]. As
described in section 2.2 the legislation requires focus on HOF,
but does not demand to which depth these considerations shall
be performed. One difficulty with organizational assessments is
that they usually rely on observations and interviews. For a
method to be reliable and valid, the results should not depend
on the assessor. This requirement is hard to fulfill, because signals
of deteriorating performance may be hard to detect and the
assessor may have certain prejudices. The methods have also to
be based on mutual trust between the assessor and those
assessed that recommendations will be to the point and fairly
treated. Assessors sometimes negotiate a kind of agreement with
those to be assessed as to how data will be collected, validated
and reported. Some of the difficulties can be solved by training
the assessors, but training alone cannot compensate for the need
of insight, tact and discretion [66]. Another difficulty is connected
to the fact that all organizational factors interact with each other.
It is therefore difficult to build models of organizational behavior
in the form of cause–consequence relationships. A theory of how
performance is related to various organizational factors could
help in this respect, but unfortunately no such theory currently
exists. In approaching organizational problems it is necessary to
identify key variables and to select a framework within which the
problem can be defined and solved. Sometimes it is necessary to
combine methods or even create new ones. Also, the use of
different approaches can sometimes provide additional validation
of the results that have been obtained [67].

This article has documented that although there has been a
requirement in the legislation for almost ten years, the considera-
tion of HOFs in the QRA studies is usually quite superficial. This is
one of the reasons why the revised NORSOK Z-013 [68] has
strengthened the requirements and states: ‘‘An evaluation of the
effect of human and organizational factors shall be performed. This
may range from a qualitative discussion to a detailed analysis of
human and organizational errors, depending on the criticality of such
aspects for the risk picture.’’ The analysis of HOF aspects in QRA
studies for Shuttle tanker—FPSO collision risk, and its success in
contributing to reducing the occurrence rate of incidents and
accidents, shows that it is possible to successfully account for the
HOFs. Another example is the ongoing research activity ‘Risk
OMT’, Risk Modeling—Integration of Organizational, Human and
Technical factors [4] that is expected to provide detailed modeling
for the analysis of hydrocarbon fire and explosion risk.

The Norwegian performance based regulations specify the
performance or function that is to be attained or maintained by
the party responsible. The regulatory role here involves defining
the safety standards and acceptance criteria, which companies
must meet. Norway authority’s intention of such a regulatory
regime is to have the operator focus on Health, Safety and
Environmental performance and to be ‘self-regulatory’ regarding
this matter [69]. We have earlier stated that it is not possible to
fulfill the requirements in the regulations without including HOF
in the QRAs. However, there is a large degree of responsibility and
freedom for the operator, to demonstrate how their safety
management system and performance comply with the regula-
tions. The regulatory authority has the opportunity to sanction
their view through use of safety audits. In October 2009 the PSA
conducted an audit of one main operator’s use of quantitative risk
analysis (TRA/QRA). The objective of the audit was to assess
whether the operator had established internal requirements and
work processes, which ensure that the assumptions, conditions,
limitations and recommendations of the risk analysis were
fulfilled. In the PSA notification letter, the PSA asked to be
presented with the operator’s requirements and guidelines,
including practical examples and experiences acquired from their
own facilities on the Norwegian Shelf. Several items with room
for improvement were identified, e.g., no competence require-
ments had been set relating to relevant positions offshore, which
require detailed knowledge of the risk analysis that had been
carried out (limitations, assumptions and results) [70]. The audit
was a clear message from the PSA that the QRA should be a part of
the safety management system. The audit revealed that the QRA
was not incorporated into the safety management systems, and
thereby the operational risk management where HOFs are essen-
tial. Even though the findings were serious, there have not been
any following audits conducted of the operator, or any other
operator. Several similar audits can reduce the gross variance in
the application of the QRAs.

This article documents the relationship between HOFs, legislation
requirements and the different levels of QRAs. The focus has been on
the Norwegian and UK Shelves and performance-based regulations.
Following the Macondo well blowout the U.S. has adopted partly the
performance-based regulation by making essential components of the
API RP 75 mandatory [71]. According to API RP 75 hazard analysis is
to be performed to identify, evaluate and, where unacceptable, reduce
the likelihood and/or minimize the consequences of uncontrolled
releases and other safety or environmental incidents. Human factors
should be considered in this analysis [72].
5. Conclusion

As clearly stated in the previous chapters, HOFs play an
important role in the Norwegian and UK legislation. HOFs must
be modeled and their role as safety barriers must be revealed to
fulfill the legislation requirements. The study of the QRAs shows
that there are large differences between the analyses and this is
also reflected in the four level classification system. The QRAs
differ largely depending on the extent to which they incorporate
HOFs, and none of the QRAs are fulfilling the requirements to
include HOFs such that they can be considered as Level 4 analysis.
Relevant research projects have been conducted to fulfill the
requirements of Level 3 analysis. Further research needs to be
carried out, and the different scientific disciplines need to be
further united to be considered as a Level 4 analysis. Further
safety audits by regulatory authorities focusing on QRAs are most
likely necessary to point out the direction and role for QRAs.
Acknowledgements

The authors are grateful to the operators who contributed by
providing QRAs. A special thanks also to Jr. Specialist Jahon Khorsandi
at UC Berkeley for reviews and comments. We also appreciate the
comments and suggestions made by the referees, and the financial
support from the Norwegian Research Council and Statoil.

Quantitative risk analysis offshore—Human and organizational factors
