3.1. Prior probabilities for a node without parents

Suppose a node X has no parent and has k states (S1,S2,. . .,Sk).
To elicit prior probabilities that X is at each state (w¼ ½w1,w2,. . .,
ws,. . .,wk, where ws is the probability when X is at state Ss).
Traditionally, ws is specified directly by experts, using their
knowledge and experiences. When the number of states is small,
such a method may be feasible. With the increase of states,
estimating probabilities directly to all states at one time may
inevitably involve biases and inaccuracies. An alternative way is
giving triangular fuzzy number to perform pair-wise comparisons
between states for generating their probabilities. The fuzzy AHP
method is able to solve uncertain ‘fuzzy’ problems. In this model,
the prior probability of each state can be determined by the
following pair-wise comparison matrix:

1 c~12 . . . c~1k

triangular fuzzy number.

C~ ij is fuzzy linguistic scale specified by asking the domain
experts questions like ‘‘comparing states Si and Sj, which one is
more likely to occur and how much more?’’ Domain experts will
answer the question using the following fuzzy linguistic scale in
Table 2 (Magdy and David, 2001).

3.2. Conditional probabilities for a node with multiple parents

A node X has k states (S1,S2,. . .,Sk). The node X has n parents,

4. Probability analysis of offshore fire scenarios

In this section, the probabilities of the most credible fire
scenarios are analyzed using the model mentioned above. Firstly,
Fig. 5. Fault tree of BLEVE follow
the most credible fire scenarios of four hazardous units are
analyzed using FT. Secondly, the FTs are converted to BNs and
the BNs are extended to incorporate HOFs. Thirdly, the probability
of the most credible fire scenarios and the importance degree
of each basic event are estimated according to BN inference.
The principal steps are shown in Fig. 3.

4.1. Fire scenarios of offshore production

Offshore production involves extracting the hydrocarbons
from offshore fields and transporting them from wells through
flow lines to a processing facility for separation (Elgsæer et al.,
2010). A simplified process flow diagram is presented in Fig. 4.

The sources of major hazards in offshore production are
flammable materials in the risers, slug catchers, separators and
high speed rotating equipments such as turbines, compressors
ed by fire for oil separator.



2048 Y.F. Wang et al. / Ocean Engineering 38 (2011) 2042–2055
and export pumps (Khan and Amyotte, 2002). There are mainly
seven offshore units, such as oil pipeline, gas pipeline, separator,
pump, flash drum, drier and compressor. The fire and explosion
hazard of oil separator, condensate separator, flash drum and
compressor are larger than other units. So fire and explosion risk
of those four units should be assessed in the first place. The most
credible scenarios of these four units are identified as follows
(Khan et al., 2002):

 Scenario 1 (oil separator unit): Excessive pressure in oil
separator might cause boiling liquid expanding vapor explo-
sion (BLEVE). BLEVE would cause oil/gas releasing. If the
releasing oil/gas meets with ignition source, it generally
causes fire.



Table 4
Basic events of the fault tree for the most credible scenario of condensate
separator.

Basic event Failure frequency Important degree
Scenario 2 (condensate separator unit): The release of flam-
mable material from the condensate separator or its pipeline
might form vapor cloud. If the vapor cloud is ignited with
delay, it would generally cause vapor cloud explosion (VCE).
Thereafter, unreleased liquid left in the condensate separator
is liable to be ignited by external heat. This regularly leads to
pool fire.
(per year) of probability

X0 Release from valve 0.0455 0.03208
X1 Release from separator 2 0.0084 0.03999
X2 Release from pipeline 0.096 0.03999
X3 Excessive pressure in vessel 0.203 0.03999
Scenario 3 (compressor unit): The flammable gas is likely to
continuously release from compressor or its pipeline. If the
released gas is ignited along with releasing, it would cause a
jet fire. The jet fire is easy to bring about the failures of
other units.
release of chemical


X4 Ignition due to external heating 0.2 0.02191

from surroundings
X5 Ignition due to explosion energy 0.15 0.02335
X6 Ignition due to electric spark 0.25 0.02334

X8 Ignition due to fire heat load 0.25 0.03123
X9 Release from vessel after 0.05 0.09232

explosion
X10 Release from pipe after 0.1 0.09263

explosion
Scenario 4 (flash drum unit): Gas release from the flash drum
or its pipeline. Released gas might product a flammable vapor
cloud. The vapor cloud is liable to meet with ignition source
with delay, it will generally cause vapor cloud explosion.

4.2. Fault tree analysis

FTs of the most credible scenarios are built to analyze the causal
effect of fire escalation in this section. The failure probability data
Fig. 6. Fault tree of Vapor Cloud Explosion fo
of basic events were collected from Offshore Reliability Data
Handbook (Anon, 2002.), World-wide Offshore Accident Databases
(Veritec, 1998) and reports by HSE (Veritas, 2007).

The FT of scenario 1 is constructed as shown in Fig. 5. These
basic events, together with their important degree of probability
and yearly failure probability, are shown in Table 3. According to
the quantitative analysis of FT, the occurrence probability of
scenario 1 is 0.0000064.

In Fig. 6, the FT of scenario 2 (VCE followed by fire for
condensate separator) is built. The basic events, their important
degree of probability and yearly failure probability are shown in
Table 4. According to the inference of FT, the probability of
scenario 2 is 0.0139.

The inherent causes of jet fire for compressor unit are analyzed
and its FT is constructed in Fig. 7. The basic events together with
llowed by fire for condensate separator.

their important degree of probability and yearly failure probabil-
ity are shown in Table 5. From the computing results, it can be
concluded that the probability of jet fire for the compressor unit is
0.00777.

The FT of scenario 4 (VCE with fire for flash drum unit) is
constructed as shown in Fig. 8. The basic events, their important
degree of probability and yearly failure probability are shown in
Table 6. According to the quantitative analysis results, the
occurrence probability of scenario 4 is 0.0882.

From the above computation results, it can be seen that
scenario 4 (VCE with fire for flash drum unit) is the most common
fire accident scenario. The next in order is scenario 2 (VCE
followed by fire for condensate separator). So the two units
should be paid more attention during safety design and security
monitoring.
4.3. Bayesian network inference

The discrete BNs corresponding to the above FTs are extended
to incorporate HOFs in Figs. 9–12. The computation of the
posterior marginal probabilities of root nodes given the scenario
occurrence, which is the advantage of BN, indicates the impor-
tance degree of basic events. The mutual information can calcu-
late quantitatively the effect of basic events to the top event.
Which basic events should be paid more attention and what
safety measures should be adopted can be induced based on the
sequence of importance degree.

In Fig. 9, the BN of scenario 1 is constructed. The occurrence
probability of scenario 1 is computed as 0.0572. According to
Bayesian inference, mutual information and the conditional
probability of each basic event given the fire happened are shown
in Table 7. The results reveal that the basic events X6, X8 and X7
contribute greatly to the occurrence of accident. Mutual informa-
tion and the posterior probability of each HOF given the fire
happened are shown in Table 8, from which, it can be concluded
that ‘‘not_obey_standards’’ and ‘‘improper_safety_organization’’
generally contribute mostly to the occurrence of scenario 1. Thus,
the probability of fire accidents would drastically be reduced by
controlling these important causes.

The BN of scenario 2 is mapped in Fig. 10. The occurrence
probability of scenario 2 is 0.0021. Mutual information and the
conditional probability of basic events given the fire happened are
shown in Table 9, from which, it can be seen that basic events X3,
X9 and X10 are the most crucial causes. Mutual information and
the marginal probabilities of HOFs given the fire happened are
shown in Table 10. It can be concluded that ‘‘not_obey_standards’’
and ‘‘not_comply_with_instruction’’ generally contribute mostly
to the occurrence of scenario 2. Controlling these crucial events
would reduce the probability of fire accident.

In Fig. 11, the BN of jet fire in compressor unit is con-
structed. The occurrence probability of jet fire in compressor
unit is estimated to be 0.102. The quantitative computation
results of BN inference are presented in Table 11. It is can be
seen that X6, X7, X11 and X12 have the larger contribution to
the probability of eventual fire accident. Mutual information
and the posterior probability of HOFs given the fire happened
are shown in Table 12, from which, it can be concluded that
‘‘not_comply_with_instruction’’ and ‘‘inefficient_emergency_
plan’’ generally contribute mostly to the occurrence of scenario
3. This analysis concludes that attention should be focused
on X6, X7, X11, X12, ‘‘not_comply_with_instruction’’ and



2050 Y.F. Wang et al. / Ocean Engineering 38 (2011) 2042–2055

Fig. 8. Fault tree of Vapor Cloud Explosion with fire for flash drum.

‘‘inefficient_emergency_plan’’ as these six events are most
likely to cause this accident.

The BN of scenario 4 is built in Fig. 12. The occurrence
probability of scenario 4 is computed as 0.0221 per year. Mutual
information and the conditional probability of basic events given
fire happened are shown in Table 13. From Table 14, it can be
concluded that basic events X1, X2 and X3 have the largest
contribution to the probability of the eventual accident. Mutual
information and the marginal probabilities of each HOF given the
fire happened are shown in Table 14. It can be concluded that
‘‘inefficient_emergency_plan’’ and ‘‘not_comply_with_instruc-
tion’’ generally contribute mostly to the occurrence of scenario
2. Controlling these crucial events would reduce the probability of
fire accident.

4.4. Discussion

From Table 7 to 14, it can be seen that the occurrence
probability of scenario 3 is the largest, incorporating the effect
of HOFs. We can also see that the importance ranking based on
posterior probability is different from the one based on prior
probability. In fact, for diagnostic purposes, it is more suitable to
consider the posterior joint probability given the fire scenario
happened as evidence. Thus, the most probable causes, which
have the larger contribution to the probability of the eventual
accident, are really identified. Controlling these crucial causal
events would reduce the probability of eventual accident con-
siderably. Some safety measures associated with these crucial
causal events are proposed as follows:
1.
 For the oil separator unit, the most important safety measure is
to install appropriate safety valve, pressure controller and to
ensure that they can work efficiently. The second important
measure is installing flow controller to prevent excess flow in
separator. The most important HOF is obeying safety standards.
2.
 For the condensate separator unit, it is crucial to prevent
release by controlling the pressure and to detect immediately
the release by installing efficient detectors. At the same time,
some measures and special material should be adopted to
prevent electric spark, such as insulator, ceramic wool and
silicone resin coat. The most important HOF is obeying safety
standards.



Y.F. Wang et al. / Ocean Engineering 38 (2011) 2042–2055 2051

Fig. 9. Bayesian network of BLEVE followed by fire for oil separator.

Fig. 10. Bayesian network of Vapor Cloud Explosion followed by fire for condensate separator.
3.
 For the compressor unit, it is most important to take measures
to protect the pipeline and vessel from fire heat, such as
fireproof coatings and installing water spray system. It is also
important to take measures to prevent electric spark. The most
important HOF is complying with instructions.
4.
 For the flash drum unit, the most important safety measure
is to prevent release from pipeline, especially for the joint of
gas pipeline. Measures of eliminating the ignition source
are also important. Such as adopting explosion proof electric
apparatus, installing fire detection, alarm and water spray
system. The most important HOF is also complying with
instructions.
5. Conclusions

In this paper, the probability of offshore fire is analyzed by
integrating FT with BN. Firstly the most credible scenarios of



2052 Y.F. Wang et al. / Ocean Engineering 38 (2011) 2042–2055

Fig. 11. Bayesian network of jet fire for compressor unit.

Fig. 12. Bayesian network of vapor cloud explosion with fire for flash drum unit.
offshore fire are modeled by FT, which provides a systematic
guideline for the construction of BN. This is then followed by
converting FT into BN. Subsequently, the BN is extended to
incorporate the effect of HOFs to fire scenario and provide a more
precise quantitative links between the event nodes. At last, the
probability of the most credible offshore fire scenarios and the
importance degrees of all basic events are estimated according to
historical data and BN inference. Based on the above analysis, the
corresponding safety measures are proposed to prevent the
accidents reoccurring.

In this paper, Fuzzy AHP helps reduce the subjective biases by
avoiding the need to spell out explicit probability values for the
variables’ states. The decomposition method simplifies greatly the
CPT elicitation by allowing conditioning to be done on each
parent separately. The new model integrates the power of FT for
modeling deterministic causal paths with the flexibility of BN for

Floating offshore wind turbine reliability analysis based on system
grading and dynamic FTA
Xu Zhang a, Liping Sun a, Hai Sun a,b,n, Qiang Guo a, Xu Bai c
a Harbin Engineering University, China
b Naval Architecture and Marine Engineering, University of Michigan, USA
c School of Naval Architecture & Ocean Engineering, Jiangsu University of Science and Technology, Zhenjiang, Jiangsu, China
a r t i c l e i n f o

Article history:
Received 29 April 2015
Received in revised form
13 April 2016
Accepted 19 April 2016

Keywords:
Floating offshore wind turbine
Reliability analysis
Dynamic fault tree analysis
System grading
esponding author at: Harbin Engineering Uni
ail address: sunhai2009@gmail.com (H. Sun).

x.doi.org/10.1016/j.jweia.2016.04.005
05/& 2016 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
a b s t r a c t

Currently, floating offshore wind power acts as a new form of marine renewable energy, attracting much
attention all over the world. Due to constraints of technology and cost, floating wind turbines are still at
the stage of preliminary trials. Reliability analysis on floating wind turbines is essential and urgent for
solving the issues caused by high cost and challenges in searching failure causes. Primarily, this research
proposes the scientific, systematic grading of floating wind power equipment system based upon its
structure and function, providing with explicit internal relation of system and the precondition of failure
modes analysis. Dynamic fault tree analysis on floating wind turbines is conducted to discuss sequen-
tially dependent failures and redundancy failures. Furthermore, failure rates of relevant offshore struc-
tures are searched according to previous studies, databases and reports. Different modules of floating
wind turbines take respective proper failure cases for references. The overall reliability results of floating
wind turbines has been achieved. The research contributes to designing, construction and maintenance
of floating wind turbines to control investment and enlarge economic benefits.

& 2016 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
1. Introduction

Offshore wind power installation is predicted to occupy
increasing capacity in clean energy industry, which takes the
advantage of space availability, less problems with public accep-
tance and more steady winds (Pinson et al., 2008). Large-scale
wind farms have been established in many countries, such as
Spanish, U.S., Denmark, Germany and China. In Europe, the wind
energy capacity is expected to grow up to more than 130 GW
within the next decades (Dörenkämper et al., 2015). Compared
with fixed wind turbines, the concept of Floating Offshore Wind
Turbine (FOWT) was firstly adopted in the 1990s.

Reliability analysis on FOWT is necessary. On one hand, FOWT
is usually designed to operate at deep sea, where the system's
maintenance costs too much and is difficult to be implemented
(Tong, 1998; Galanis and Christophides, 1990; Cockerill et al.,
2001). FOWT's downtime and maintaining cost will be decreased
once reliability promotion is processed with the assumption of
invariant devices’ cost. On the other hand, the conventional design
of FOWT is unreliable based on demands of structural strength and
reliability analysis is merely referred in this area. The primary
versity, China.
reasons that limit the reliability through the strength based
designing are related to the following issues: (i) deviation exists in
prediction of extreme waves in the simulation because there is
little methods addressing strong stochastic characteristics of
extreme waves; (ii) motion and stress responses of FOWT's
mooring system are evaluated with uncertainty due to non-linear
effects causing by the motion of mooring line is influenced by
ocean environment, and floating bodies appears obvious non-
linearity (Webster, 2009; Dong and Sang, 2015; Agarwal and
Manuel, 2011; Marino et al., 2011a, 2011b, 2013; Hieu et al., 2013;
Kareem, 1983).

Reliability of wind turbine (WT) on land has been analyzed for
many years. Arabian-Hoseynabadi et al. (2010) carried out a
comprehensive Failure Modes and Effect Analysis (FMEA) on a
complete 2 MW WT, and identified the ten top most frequent
Failure Modes and Root Causes. Márquez et al. (2012) described
the different maintenance strategies, condition monitoring tech-
niques and methods of wind turbines. They analyzed failure
modes of wind turbine qualitatively via a Fault Tree Analysis (FTA).
Pérez et al. (2013) compared failures rates and downtimes repor-
ted independently for different types of WTs across the world, and
concluded that failure rates vary lightly in different studies for
some elements, such as hubs, generators, sensors, brakes, systems
and structure. Both Ribrant and Bertling (2007) and Pérez et al.



22 X. Zhang et al. / J. Wind Eng. Ind. Aerodyn. 154 (2016) 21–33
(2013) summarized that gearbox's downtime per failure is high in
comparison to other components in WTs, and larger WTs tend to
appear more failures than smaller ones. Chou and Tu (2011)
reviewed the historical accidents of tower collapse worldwide and
their causes.

Precious efforts on reliability analysis on offshore WTs have
been developed. Dai et al. (2013) concluded that collisions
between turbines and service vessels even at low speed might
cause structural damage to the turbines. Bai et al. (2012) presented
the risk assessment of offshore wind turbine in transportation and
installation using Risk Priority Number method. Hameed et al.
(2011) outlined and categorized potential challenges and issues
pertaining to the reliability and maintainability data collection of
offshore wind turbines. Due to less experience with offshore WTs,
it is necessary to collect on-hand information involving the
existing models to ensure reliability.

Floating wind power equipment has attracted increasing
attention in terms of cost and safety optimal. Laura and Vicente
(2014) put forward that the main costs of all the types of floating
platforms are related to manufacturing, maintenance and instal-
lation. Myhr et al. (2014) presented a comprehensive analysis and
comparison of the levelised cost of energy (LCOE) for several types
of FOWT concepts. There are also some papers focusing on the
dynamic performances and optimal design of FOWTs (Zhang et al.,
2013).

Nevertheless, there has been little reported research on
dynamic reliability evaluation for a complex system like FOWT. So
the reliability condition has not been acknowledged and little
comprehensive research on FOWT failure diagnosis and prog-
nostics has been conducted. So effective suggestions during
design, construction and operation phases for FOWT system are
needed through the systematic reliability analysis. However, large-
scale and complexity of technology has raised challenges in
reliability evaluation of FOWT system. These challenges are dis-
played as follows. (i) Complicated structural composition and
operating principle, which restricts the efficiency of failure locat-
ing of FOWT system. (ii) Dynamic characteristics of failure modes
should be considered, such as redundancy parts and sequentially
dependent faults. (iii) Insufficient failure data. Failure data can
hardly be gathered in the prototype testing stage. And the main
innovative features introduced in this paper are the approaches to
addressing the challenges for reliability analysis for FOWT system
as a whole.

System grading provides a disciplined, applicable approach to
clarify system composition. Interactive grading information
deduced from the two perspectives assists efficient failure loca-
tion. Thus the reliability analysis of FOWT based on system grading
is feasible and issue (i) can be solved.

Qualitative FTA is used in future research on fault diagnosis
(Márquez et al., 2012). FTA is an effective, promising methodology
for analyzing reliability and safety. Which makes it possible to
search for failure modes and weak links. Quantitative assessment
is conducted by evaluating the occurrence probability of the
minimal cut sets, therefore deducing the probability of occurrence
of the top event according to the logical relationship. And it allows
indication of system reliability at operational time and deriving
maintenance references. FTA is an alternative technique for large-
scale systems, such like FOWT. However, conventional static FTA is
inadequate to describe the dynamic relations between failure
events. Bechta Dugan et al. (1992) and Dugan et al. (2015)
imported dynamic logic gates into fault trees to address sequence
failure, dependent failure and redundancy allocation. So dynamic
FTA is an optional methodology to capture the dynamic behaviors
of FOWT system's failure mechanisms (Bechta Dugan et al., 1992).

Historical failure data of basic events is required as an abso-
lutely necessary element to implement system reliability analysis
via dynamic quantitative FTA. However, as for FOWT systems,
inadequate data makes the reliability analysis difficult. It can be
noted that there are few variations from floating WTs devices to
on-land ones. Thus, failure rates of on-land WTs are summarized
by reviewing Refs. Arabian-Hoseynabadi et al. (2010), Márquez
et al. (2012), Pérez et al. (2013), Ribrant and Bertling (2007); Chou
and Tu (2011); Dai et al., 2013), Hai et al., (2011) and Bai et al.
(2012), relevant reports (GWEC (Global wind report annual market
update, 2013)) and database (OREDA–Offshore Reliability Data
Handbook (OREDA, 2009)), which supply references for FOWTs.
Considering influences of marine environment conditions on
FOWTs, the derived WT's failure probability based on on-land data
should be modified. Meanwhile, failure data of FOWTs’ floating
foundation and mooring system can be evaluated by taking failure
cases of other offshore structures as reference. Relevant failure
data is achieved through the database of OREDA.
2. FOWT system grading

A FOWT system consists of many various modules, which are
either independent or auxiliary to each other to guarantee normal
operation of the whole system. It is difficult to obtain independent,
proper failure modes due to the intersections among modules.
Convenient location of failure causes and efficient, authentic
reliability analysis will be achieved if FOWT system's modules are
classified reasonably in advance. Previous work has been con-
centrated on studies of onshore WTs, but little work of system
grading for FOWT has been addressed. This work is expected to
propose systematic FOWT system grading in multi-dimension.

According to the structural levels and functional relevance, the
FOWT system is respectively divided into subsystems and equip-
ment. Thus system levels and the correlation between various
modules are displayed as follows.

2.1. Function based system grading

Fig. 1 illustrates the operating principles of the FOWT, based
upon which the resulting functional modules of the FOWT are
determined as energy receiving/transmission/conversion system,
control system, support system and auxiliary system, as shown in
Fig. 2.

2.1.1. Energy receiving/transmission/conversion system
The marine environment makes blades vulnerable to salt-spray

corrosion and vibration damage. Serious damage can result in
voids, cracks and even fracture. For long time of operation without
timely detection and maintenance, blades will face the risk of
quality degradation. The hub failure results mostly from fatigue,
wear and imbalance.

The major risks for transmission system are vibration, main
shaft/ bolt damage, high-speed coupling damage, shafting bearing,
and overwarming. For gearbox, failure reasons can be identified at
gear fatigue/fracture, bearing failure, shaft bending, cooling system
failure, etc. The generator failure is generally attributable to
abnormal speed, high temperature, bearing damage, stator insu-
lation damage, inter turn short circuit, collector ring fault etc. The
failures of transmission system and generator may decrease the
efficiency of generator, in which circumstances, the profits
obtained by power enterprises are likely to be lower than their
investments.

2.1.2. Control system
The control system is the most versatile part of the FOWT. Its

primary failure causes are feedback error, application error, time
error, record error, the battery failure, temperature control cabinet



X. Zhang et al. / J. Wind Eng. Ind. Aerodyn. 154 (2016) 21–33 23

Wind
Generating

Pitching Speed control
Rotor system Power 

networks

& wind Power measurement

Control system

Aerovane

Yawing

Fig. 1. Schematic diagram of FOWT.
failure and sensor failure. Any communication link failure in the
loop of control system will lead the system running into errors.

2.1.3. Support system
Support system involves mechanical supporting parts, for

instance, the nacelle, chassis, tower, floating foundation and
mooring system. Its main failure modes of the tower and floating
foundation can be shown as: fatigue, corrosion, welding cracking
and hull collision. Upon extreme sea conditions, the floating
foundation, the mooring lines and the tower vibrates intensely to
make the WT unstable. And even severe accidents may occur, for
example, tower fracture, mooring system failure and blades
damage.

2.1.4. Auxiliary system
Auxiliary system serves as a supporting role for specific com-

ponents or modules. When lubrication system or cooling system is
broken down, overwarming of gears, short circuits, and even fires
will happen. If the lightning protection module fails, components
of FOWT system may be burned out by lightning, which can cause
serious economic losses. Hydraulic system's failure modes are
primarily hydraulic pressure error, temperature error, timeout of
hydraulic pump, low oil level, motor failure, pressure of high-
speed braking failure and increasing main force of filter. Personnel
auxiliary devices refer to workbench, ladder/safety chain, lighting
devices, etc. These devices’ failures may delay the time for
detection and maintenance.

2.2. Structure based system grading

For reliability analysis, the system grading based on structure
ensures spatial integrity. Thus it is easy to keep the continuity of
the structure and determine the failure causes. In our case, FOWT
system is divided into four parts: wind turbine, tower, floating
foundation and mooring system, as shown in Fig. 3.

2.2.1. Wind turbine
Variable pitch WTs are selected for case study in this research.

Three blades with their respective drivers adjust pitch to obtain
maximum power. It is mainly consisted of rotor system, nacelle
and yawing, among which, the internal structures of nacelle are
crucial and complicated, which have a high failure rate. In the
nacelle, control system, blades/ pitching and gear's failure rates are
the three highest ones (Pérez et al., 2013). In addition, the possible
failures of the wind turbine can be described as rotor system
failure, bearing or shaft failure, gearbox failure, braking system
failure, generator failure, sensors failure, yawing failure, drive train
failure, control system failure, hydraulics failure, structural failure
and electrical failure.

2.2.2. Tower
Tower is used to support the WT. In addition to the supporting

function, tower is also expected to resist force and moment pro-
duced by the wind load. The failure of tower occurs mainly at the
flange bolt and weld area, caused by fatigue loads. Meanwhile,
failure modes like hit by blades, the braking system failed, strong
wind/ wave, lighting strike, ice storm, Storm, and Material fatigue
also exist under the effect of extreme loads. Severe consequences,
such as tower collapses, will result in sinking of the WT and ser-
ious losses. Detailed investigation of failure analysis and risk
management of collapsed large WT tower can be seen from Chou
and Tu (2011).

2.2.3. Floating foundation structure and mooring system
Floating foundation is the most important support compo-

nents. Considering that different types of floating foundations
have corresponding mooring systems, the two modules are illu-
strated as a whole section as follows.

Floating foundation structure is similar to marine oil and gas
platforms. The main types are Spar, Tension Leg Platform (TLP) and
Semi-platform. The entire risk of Semi- platform FOWT is the
lowest of the three types of floating platforms. When the fixed
anchor fails, the platform is likely to keep afloat. The operating risk
of Spar is the lowest. Spar's gravity center is much lower than
buoyant center, which helps it survive even when the setting of
anchor is damaged. Although the risk of stranding and running on
rocks exists, Spar is unlikely overall overturned. For TLP platform,
if it uses solid tube tendon and vertical anchor, its risk will stay at a
low level at the time of installation and extreme weather. Semi-
floating foundation is chosen after a comprehensive consideration.

Accordingly, its possible failures are pillar damage, corrosion of
pipe joint, weld defect of pipe joint, fatigue of pipe joint, insuffi-
cient detection, typhoon, crash of planes, biological collision and
capsize.

Floating foundations fracture results in the unbalance of upper
structures. Floating foundation is tightly relative to mooring sys-
tem. As for the mooring system for Semi-floating foundation, the
possible failures are the limit state of sea, corrosion of fairlead,
fatigue of fairlead, mooring lines broken, unsatisfied sea state for
operation, insufficient emergency measures for abrupt scenes and
analysis and calculation fault. When either of the two modules
fails, the system will be in jeopardy.
3. Dynamic fault tree (DFT)

DFT analysis has been one of the most promising techniques for
the reliability evaluation of complex dependable systems (Manno
et al., 2014). For a complex system like FOWT, dynamic behaviors
like sequence failure of events, redundancy equipment and trig-
gered failure of other events. However, conventional fault tree is
unable to describe the dynamic behaviors leading to deviation
from the real conditions. So when this paper conducts reliability
analysis of FOWT system, dynamic FT is introduced to acquire
more credible results than static FT.

On the basis of the original static FT (SFT) technique, the con-
struction of a DFT is a TOP-DOWN exercise where a Top Event (TE)
is related to its basic causes (i.e., basic events, BEs) by logical gates.
BEs are the leaves of the Fault Tree and represent the elementary
events, not requiring further investigations. The differences of DFT
from SFT appear when dynamic logic gates are used.

When the failure data of BEs are given, the failure probability of
the TE can be derived by mathematical theory. According to the
logic relations among the events in a fault tree, minimum cut sets
can be obtained. Minimum cut sets stand for the minimum sets of
all basic events triggering occurrence of the top event. Based on
the assumption of none repeated basic events, all the cut sets are

The resolution of a DFT is much different than the one of the

SFT because temporal and cross dependencies cannot be modeled
and solved through Boolean algebra (Akers, 2007). For SFT, there
are common logic gates, such as “AND” gate and “OR” gate,
regardless of time dependent failure and redundancy configura-
tion failure mechanisms, which are involved using dynamic logic
gates, namely Priority And Gate (PAND), The Spare Gate (SP) and
The Functional Dependency gate (FDEP), Sequence enforcing gate
(SEQ) and so on (Manno et al., 2014).

At the state of the art several techniques have been proposed to
solve dynamic FTs when addressing complex dynamic FT models.
And a dynamic fault tree always need to be converted to Markov
chain, Bayesian network or Petri net with the workload increased
largely (Manno et al., 2012; Chiacchio et al., 2011; Boudali et al.,
2009; Montani et al., 2006; Bechta Dugan et al., 1997, 2000; Sul-
livan et al., 1999; Sahner and Trivedi, 1986). The method proposed
by Suprasad Amari simplified the process for computing the fail-
ure probability of the top event of the dynamic logic gates without
transformation to other graphic forms (Rauzy, 2011). So this paper
applies the method and conduct quantitative analysis of the FOWT
system. In this paper, the dynamic FT of FOWT system has used
two kinds of dynamic logic gates, Priority And Gate (PAND) and
Cold Spare Gate (CSP) (Spare Gates include Cold Spare Gate, Warm
Spare Gate and Hot Spare Gate). So we provide the theories of
these two dynamic logic gates. The schematic diagrams of PAND
and CSP are shown in Fig. 4.

3.1. Priority And Gates (PAND)

The failure of a Priority-AND (PAND) gate occurs only if its
inputs fail in the order from left to right in the gate graphical
notation (Manno et al., 2014). A PAND over events E1…Ek is rea-
lized if E1…Ek occur in this order. Priority And Gates are



26 X. Zhang et al. / J. Wind Eng. Ind. Aerodyn. 154 (2016) 21–33
interpreted as asymmetric shuffles.
PAND ðE1;…; EkÞ ¼ ð…ðE1; E2Þ; E3Þ…; EkÞ ð4Þ

A PAND gate has two input events. Input event 1 follows the
distribution of G1(t) at the time T1, and input event 2 follows the
distribution of G2(t) at the time of T2. The output event follows the
distribution of G(t) at tZhe time of T. G(t) is expressed as follows.

3.2. Cold Spare Gates (CSP)

A SPARE gate can have only one primary input and an unde-
fined number of spare components which could be shared with
other SPARE gates (Manno et al., 2014). Components of SPARE
gates have the following characteristics:

(1) They are ordered (from left to right in the graphical
notation), and

(2) They can be active or stand-by, where “active” means oper-
ating in a SPARE gate and “stand-by” means not operating in
any SPARE gate.

When the primary input is operating, the spare inputs are in
stand-by state. And when the primary input fails, available spare
inputs are used in order until none is left, at which time the gate
occurs. Spares can be shared among spare gates, in which case the
first spare gate to utilize the spare makes the spare inaccessible to
the other spare gates.

When the primary input is right, the spare inputs are in stand-
by state. When the primary input fails, available spare inputs are
used in order until none is left, at which time the gate fails. Spares
can be shared among spare gates, in which case the first spare gate
to utilize the spare makes the spare inaccessible to the other spare
gates (Rauzy, 2011).

Primary input follows the distribution of G1(t) at the time T1,
and cold spare part follows the distribution of G2(t) at the time of
T2. The output event follows the distribution of G(t) at the time of
Fig. 4. Priority And Gate (left) and Cold Spare Gate (right).

4. DFTA analysis of FOWT

As for a large-scale system like FOWT, a static FT is incapable of
modeling dynamic behaviors, including spare components and
time dependent failure mechanisms. Dynamic logic gates, such as
“Priority and Gate” (PAND gate) and “Cold Spare Parts Gate” (CSP)
are employed. The FT of FOWT system can be divided into several
subtrees for simplification of construction and analysis. Regarding
the aforementioned system grading in two perspectives, the FTs
taking four structural modules failure as top events are established
respectively, as shown in Figs. 4–9.

Failure data of wind turbine module and tower module in
FOWT is collected referring to the failure data of WTs on land.
Through integration of failure cases of offshore structures, failure
data of the FOWT's floating foundation and mooring system are
estimated. Most failure data involves information review of OREDA
and some relative Refs. (Arabian-Hoseynabadi et al., 2010; Már-
quez et al., 2012; Pérez et al., 2013; Ribrant and Bertling, 2007;
Chou and Tu, 2011; Dai et al., 2013; Global wind report annual
market update, 2013; OREDA, 2009). Expert elicitation is intro-
duced to evaluate the remaining failure rates which are unavail-
able through historical cases.

 OREDA

OREDA is a project organization sponsored by oil and gas
companies with world-wide operations. Its main purpose is to
collect and exchange reliability data among the participating
companies and act as The Forum for co-ordination and manage-
ment of reliability data collection within the oil and gas industry. It
has established a comprehensive databank with reliability and
maintenance data for exploration and production equipment from
a wide variety of geographic areas, installations, equipment types
and operating conditions. Offshore subsea and topside equipment
are primarily covered, but onshore equipment is also included.

Data collected are in general not covering the whole lifetime of
equipment, but typically a time window of 2–4 years of operation.
Infant mortality failures are a rule not collected for topside
equipment; hence, the data collected are from the normal steady-
state operating time period.

 GWEC's Global Wind Report

Annual Market Update on the status of the global wind
industry is the authoritative source of information on wind power

with 24 countries having more than 1000 MW installed.

 Failure rate

The failure rate function expresses how likely it is that an item
that has survived up to time t, will fail during the next unit of time.
The failure rate function is usually a function of the time.

zð Þ ¼ 1 
t lim P toTrtþΔtjT4tÞ ð

Δt-1Δ 7Þ
t

This implies the approximation

zð ÞUΔt  
t P toTrtþΔtjT4tÞ ð8Þ
Actually, the life of a technical item may generally be split into

three different phases: the burn-in ('or early failure') phase, the
useful、phase and the wear-out phase. the failure rate function
may be decreasing in the burn-in phase, close to constant in the
useful life phase and increasing in the wear-out phase. The curve
of failure rate with time can be seen like a bath-tub shape.

Many of the items covered in OREDA are subject to some
maintenance or replacement policy. The items will therefore often
be replaced or refurbished before they reach the wear-out phase.

With the assumption of constant failure rate for every event in
the FT of FOWT system, mean failure rates are chosen for reliability
calculation that are obtained by the OREDA estimator and used
commonly in engineering. And the relation between failure
probabilitRy and failure rate is shown as below.

Where P(t) means failure probability or unreliability, and R(t)

The failure rates of basic events in FTs of four structural mod-
ules are displayed in Table 1. For convenience, the expressions of
basic events are substituted as corresponding codes in the Tables
and the FTs.

Once the subtrees are completed and the probability tables are
filled, it is straight forward to compute the failure probabilities of
four modules according to the theories described in Section 3.

Fig. 5 presents the FT of WT module failure. Once anyone of the
twelve events (W1-W12) fails, the top event malfunction will occur.
An “OR” gate can connect the twelve events with the top event
appropriately. Regardless of the sea state, as shown in Table 2, rotor,
control and electrical system failure, are most likely to occur, and
failure of mechanical system, structure and drive train, is less
probable to occur than other assembly units. Due to the constitu-
tional complexity and high-risk level, rotor system and generator
are studied emphatically to ascertain root causes (Figs. 6 and 7).

Though the quantitative analysis on FTs in Figs. 6 and 7, the
minimum cut sets and failure probability are solved. Probabilities
ranking (from high to low) of the three FTs’ minimum cut sets are

displayed in Tables 2–4, respectively. Table 2 demonstrates that
rotor system is the most hazardous in “WT structure module”. As
shown in Fig. 6, the accumulator acts as a cold spare part of
external power supply applying a “CSP” to describe the link
between them. The “Fault location” occurs if both Encoder 1 and
Encoder 2 fail. Results shown in Table 3 reveal that, the three most
likely minimum cut sets are limit switch fails, lighting protection
fails and safety chain cut off. As shown in Table 3, there are seven
minimum cut sets leading to rotor system failure, and the first one
“Limit switch fails” has the highest occurrence probability, 1.75E-
06. For generator failure shown in Table 4, (-)G1 means G1 does
not occur, and the cut set “Normal start and Spurious stop” ranks
the first likely occurred event among the 15 cut sets.

“Fail to start” and “Start normally but run with error” are two
likely reasons resulting in generator failure (Fig. 7). It is impossible
for either event to occur simultaneously with the other. An
exclusive-or gate (XOR gate) is employed to demonstrate the
relation. Results in Table 4 reveal that spurious stop, breakdown
and fail to start are primary failure modes of the generator. Eco-
nomic losses are serious upon generation outage. Proper operation
of condition monitoring system of generator should be guaran-
teed. The event–“Structural deficiency” should be prevented in the
design stage and monitoring timely in the operation is necessary.

Fig. 8 presents the FT of tower module failure. Influences of sea
conditions on the tower have been taken into consideration in the
FT. The failure of anyone of twelve events (T1–T10) triggers the
failure of tower, where an “OR” gate is employed. “Extreme
typhoon/storm” at sea has the highest failure probability of 9.64E-
6 leading directly to tower collapse. As shown in Table 5, extreme
environments, such as storm, strong wind/ waves and ice storm,
are principal threatening factors to tower's safety. Sufficient
investigation of extreme environment conditions at operation site
contributes to risk management and action plans.
4.2. FTA of Floating foundation

The FT of floating foundation failure is shown in Fig. 9. Taking
the advantages of low risk, semisubmersible (short for semi-)
foundation of FOWT is selected for analyzing its reliability. The
floating foundation system is significantly affected by wind load
(Myhr et al., 2014; Tran and Kim, 2015a, 2015b). In order for “Pipe
joint” to fail, the three events –“Corrosion of pipe joint”, “Weld
defect of pipe joint” and “Fatigue of pipe joint”, need to happen.
“Water tightness error” will be triggered when both “Insufficient
detection” and “Pipe joint failure” happen simultaneously. Table 6
shows that fallen things (such as biologics, planes and objects
brought by typhoon) present the highest failure probability. The
conclusions are then generated that installation of collision pro-
tection measures are required. Besides, degradation state of pillars’
strength demands periodic detection and proper maintenance to
prevent serious accidents.

4.3. FTA of mooring system

The FT of mooring system failure is shown in Fig. 10. As shown
in Table 7, broken lines’ failure probability is uppermost among all
causes, followed by unsatisfied sea state for operation. “Weather
and environment control error” has two input events–“Unsatisfied
sea state for operation” and “Insufficient emergency measures for
abrupt scenes”. When the left-hand side event fail before (or at the
same time as) the right-hand side one, the output event will occur,
i.e. PAND gate. The minimum cut sets with parentheses mean the
events inside is sequenced. Further discussion on reasons for
mooring lines fractured is carried out in detail via FTA. The PAND
gate is also employed between “Mooring lines wear” and its input
events–“Not effective maintenance” and “Accumulating wear”.
Fracture of partial mooring lines strikes system balance, which
results in stress increase of remaining mooring lines. The number
of fractured mooring lines should be restricted to less than two for
sustaining relatively reliable state of FOWT system. Results
demonstrate that “Abnormal stress”, “Transitional chain wear”,
and “Mooring winch failure” are main factors that must be con-
sidered when evaluating risk for the lifecycle of mooring lines
safety.
5. FTA of FOWT system

According to the consequences of the analyzed fault trees, the
failure rates are derived for the four structural modules, namely
wind turbine module, tower, floating foundation and mooring
system. In Fig. 11, the calculated failure rates of four structural
modules are illustrated.

FOWT system failure is not just caused by four failures of
structural module, when considering it as a whole system. The FT
of "Floating wind turbine failure" is established in Fig. 12 involving
three kinds of failure reasons. The first one is interaction between
four structural modules, such as tower's resonance with floating
foundation. The second one is environmental effect on the overall
system, such as fire and ice rainfall. The third one is other factors,
for example, transport and collision with other floating bodies
(Márquez et al., 2012; Xu et al., 2012).

Compared with onshore WTs, risk bearing capability of FOWT
system should be improved to handle severe circumstance, for
instance, salt-spray corrosion, moisture and condensation. High
possibility of erosion when exposed to salt-spray, surfaces of
blades and hub would result in serious consequences on the per-
formance. Meanwhile, under high humidity circumstance, water
existing in the inner of control cabinet and electrical system
increases the possibility of electrical accidents like short circuit. So
appropriate dehumidifiers and timely maintenance is essential for
prevention for dehumidify and gel.
FW7 FW1 FW2 FW3

6

offshore WT failure.



32 X. Zhang et al. / J. Wind Eng. Ind. Aerodyn. 154 (2016) 21–33
Generally, as for FOWTs, failure probability of WT module
failure and tower is higher than WTs on land. Given sea state has
been involved in FT of tower failure, modification can be ignored.
According to relative research and expert estimation, a correction
coefficient of 1.6 is assessed to promote accuracy of failure rate of
WT module. Processed failure rates of basic events in FT of FOWT
failure are shown in Table 8.

The Fig. 13 describes 20 highest-ranked occurrence prob-
abilities of minimum cut sets. Results reveal that collision with
non-service vessels is the predominant factor affecting FOWT's
reliability. And FOWT's collision with vessels is usually fatal acci-
dents. It is necessary to suggest the operators to monitor location/
examine/repair in time. Besides, in order to prevent the damage of
collision, the approach of installing collision protection devices is
indispensable. The following factor is WT module's degradation,
which relates to complexity of operation principle.

The unreliability of FOWT has been calculated for twenty years.
For hypothesis of failure period is in the middle section of the
bathtub curve of the life cycle, reliability of the FOWT is propor-
tional to time. The final value of unreliability of FOWT is 3.1548e-4

The input data for the FT of FOWT system.

Fig. 13. Probabilities ranking of the top mo
after twenty years, and the failure rate is 1.6805e-3 h1. The Mean
Time Between Failure (MTBF) is 595.06 hours. So maintenance
once every 24 days is appropriate for the safety of the FOWT. And
the detected elements should be emphasized on the aforemen-
tioned 20 minimum cut sets.

According to the method for evaluating the 90% confidence
internal mentioned above, for the input failure rates in this work,
the deviation of their values are roughly evaluated to be 20% as a
whole. In order to estimate the sensitivity of final results to input
data, when all the failure rates increase by 20%, the unreliability of
FOWT system is calculated to be 3.4100E-04 by repeating the
whole analysis process. Compared with the aforementioned result,
3.1548E-4, the deviation for the unreliability of the system is
8.09%, revealing that the sensitivity of the final results to failure
rates data is not obvious, and the calculation error is in the same
order of magnitude with 8.09%. So the consequences of the relia-
bility analysis on FOWT system via dynamic FTA method are
credible to some extent.
6. Conclusions

System grading and dynamic FTA are applied to predict the
reliability of FOWT. The FOWT system is graded scientifically based
on structure and function respectively in a top-down approach,
which decomposes the system down to subsystem and equipment
levels. Failure modes of FOWT system are analyzed comprehen-
sively, therefore, laying a foundation for the following dynamic
FTA. Furthermore, the dynamic FT of “Semi-FOWT system failure”
has been established. A series of qualitative and quantitative
results are derived. Relevant failure data are achieved from some
referring literatures and failure databases (OREDA) considering
insufficient failure data of FOWT. Failure data of WTs and towers
on land is used to estimate FOWTs. Failure data of offshore
structures and devices is employed to evaluate FOWTs’ floating
foundation and mooring system. The top event's failure rate is
st frequent twenty minimum cut sets.



X. Zhang et al. / J. Wind Eng. Ind. Aerodyn. 154 (2016) 21–33 33
1.6805E-3 h1, and the average detection maintenance cycle
should be less than 576 hours. Regarding to WT module, rotor
system and generator's failure has been stressed. Driver alarm of
pitching, failing to start, downtime of the generator should be paid
enough attention. The tower is easily affected by storm/ strong
wind. Semi-foundation is mostly affected by dropped objects, and
installations of protective devices are essential. Broken lines are
primary issues of the mooring system. Lines’ stress and transi-
tional chain should be detected in time.

With deviation between the estimation and reality, the FOWT
system's reliability is predicted generally. An approach has been pro-
vided for achieving sufficient failure data and provides reference for
reliability studies of newly sprouted products. The results propound
suggestions for future risk investment. For further research, the defi-
nition of the modification factor reflects environment loads at sea, and
should be deliberated for optimization.
